In the first study, a single steroid plus numbing medicine shot cured the locking and pain in 9 of 14 people (about 64%), compared with 2 of 10 (20%) who got numbing medicine alone. [1] In the second, a similar steroid shot worked in about 60% of fingers and thumbs, versus 16% with numbing medicine alone. [2]  

These studies suggest that a single steroid injection gives a real benefit beyond placebo for many, but not all, people with a primary trigger finger. [1][2] No important side effects were reported in these trials. [1][2]  

If the first shot only partly helps, some doctors offer a second injection before considering surgery. Effectiveness can be lower in people with diabetes or long-standing, very stiff fingers, so your own chances may vary. [1][2] Improvement usually appears over several days to weeks.

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks how *ineffective* a steroid shot is for a locking finger (trigger finger). The two trials provided compare steroid plus local anesthetic to anesthetic alone. In one study, 64% were cured with a single steroid shot, meaning 36% were not [1]. In the other, 60% improved, so 40% did not [2]. Both studies show steroid injections are clearly better than placebo (16–20% success) [1][2]. For a lay explanation, the key point is that about 4 in 10 people will not get full, lasting relief from one steroid shot, so while it is the best non-surgical first treatment, it fails for a substantial minority.

- **Full Answer**:  
Steroid shots for a “locking” finger (trigger finger) are not perfect, but they are much more effective than a numbing-medicine shot alone. In one study, a single steroid plus numbing injection completely relieved the triggering in 9 out of 14 people (about 64%), while only 2 out of 10 (20%) improved with numbing medicine alone [1]. In another controlled study, steroid injections worked in 60% of people, compared with 16% in the control group [2]. This means that about 4 out of 10 people (40%) will not get full, lasting relief from a single steroid shot [1][2]. Some people may have only partial or temporary improvement, and may need a repeat injection or surgery if symptoms continue. Overall, steroid injection is considered the best first non-surgical treatment, but it does fail in a significant minority of patients [1][2]. Your doctor can discuss whether another shot or surgery makes most sense personally.

- **Rationale**:  
Research on “trigger finger” (a finger that catches or locks) has tested steroid shots against placebo shots with numbing medicine. In both studies, people didn’t know which shot they got, and doctors checking results were blinded. This design helps show whether benefit is from the steroid itself or from the injection. Cure rates and side effects were recorded over follow‑up.

- **Full Answer**:  
For a locking “trigger finger,” a steroid shot into the tendon sheath often helps. In people with primary trigger finger, a single steroid shot cured about 64% of patients, compared with about 20% cured by a shot of numbing medicine alone [1]. Another similar study found about 60% success with steroid versus 16% with numbing medicine alone [2]. Some patients get only temporary relief, and a minority do not improve and may later need surgery [1][2]. In these studies, no important side effects from the steroid shots were reported [1].

- **Rationale**:  
Research has specifically tested steroid injections for trigger finger (a finger that locks or catches). In both studies, people were randomly given either steroid plus numbing medicine or numbing medicine alone, and neither the patients nor the assessing doctors knew who got which treatment. This design lets us see if the steroid itself provides real benefit beyond a placebo effect.

- **Full Answer**:  
Steroid shots often do work for a locking or “trigger” finger, but they are not 100% effective.  

In one study, a single steroid plus numbing shot cured about 64% of people with primary trigger finger, with no reported side effects [1]. Another controlled trial found about 60% success with steroid injection, compared with only 16% improvement from numbing medicine alone (placebo control) [2].  

So, if your finger is locking, a steroid shot gives you a better-than-even chance of lasting relief, but some people still need repeat shots or surgery [1][2].

- **Rationale**:  
I reviewed randomized clinical trials comparing cartilage cell surgery (various forms of autologous chondrocyte implantation) with other common treatments for localized knee cartilage damage, including microfracture, mosaicplasty, osteochondral cylinder transplantation, and simple debridement. Across studies, I focused on pain, function, failure rates, and the quality of the new cartilage seen on biopsy or arthroscopy. I then summarized how reliably these surgeries help, how they compare with alternatives, and the main uncertainties for long‑term benefit.

- **Full Answer**:  
Cartilage cell surgery (autologous chondrocyte implantation and related methods) generally helps most people with isolated knee cartilage defects. In randomized trials, 68–88% of patients had “good to excellent” function 1–2 years after chondrocyte implantation or the alternative procedure being tested [1][2][6]. Compared directly with mosaicplasty, one trial found similar results [1], while a larger study showed better function and better-appearing repair tissue with chondrocyte implantation and poor results for mosaicplasty in the kneecap area [6]. Compared with microfracture, traditional chondrocyte implantation gave similar short‑term symptom relief and no clear histology advantage [4], while matrix‑induced and “characterized” chondrocyte implants produced equal or better symptoms and clearly better-quality repair cartilage at 1–2 years [3][5]. However, one third of patients in a trial improved substantially after simple arthroscopic cleaning alone [1]. Overall, cartilage cell surgery is effective but it is not guaranteed, alternatives may work as well, and long‑term advantages remain uncertain.

- **Rationale**:  
Across several randomized trials, cartilage cell surgery (autologous chondrocyte implantation, including matrix‑induced and “characterized” versions) usually improves symptoms rather than being ineffective. In one trial, about 88% of patients had good or excellent results after cell implantation versus 69% after mosaicplasty at ~19 months [6]. Another, smaller study saw complete recovery in 68% after cell surgery and 88% after mosaicplasty, but one‑third of patients improved enough with simple debridement that they needed no further surgery, questioning whether all needed cell surgery [1]. Compared with other techniques (microfracture or osteochondral plugs), cell surgery gave similar or slightly better short‑term pain and function scores, with no major safety problems [2–5]. However, benefits often only modestly exceed simpler procedures, follow‑up is short (around 2 years), and some patients still fail or improve slowly [2,4,6].  

- **Full Answer**:  
Cartilage cell surgery for knee cartilage damage is not “ineffective,” but it is far from a guaranteed fix.  

Randomized studies consistently show that most people improve in pain and function after these procedures, with good‑to‑excellent results in about two‑thirds to nearly 90% of patients at 1–2 years [1,2,4,6]. In one large trial, 88% of patients had good or excellent results after cell implantation, better than the 69% seen with mosaicplasty [6].  

However, in another study 68% improved fully after cell surgery while 88% did so after mosaicplasty, and about one‑third of patients improved so much after a simple “clean‑up” (debridement) that they did not need any further surgery [1].  

When compared with microfracture or osteochondral plug transplantation, cell‑based surgery generally provides similar or only slightly better short‑term results, and sometimes improves more slowly [2–5].  

Overall, cartilage cell surgery helps many patients, but its added benefit over simpler, cheaper treatments is modest and not universal.

- **Rationale**:  
Multiple randomized trials have tested cartilage cell surgery (using your own cartilage cells, often called ACI or MACI) for isolated knee cartilage defects. Across studies, almost all groups—whether treated with cell surgery, microfracture, or bone plug (“mosaic”) techniques—had clear pain relief and better knee function over 1–2 years [1–4,6]. In some trials, cell surgery worked about the same as simpler options like microfracture [1,4] or bone plugs [1,2]; in others, it produced better clinical scores or better-looking repair tissue on arthroscopy or biopsy [3,5,6]. One study even found that about one‑third of patients improved a lot after simple cleanup (debridement) alone [1]. Most research involves younger patients with one localized defect, not widespread arthritis, and follow-up is usually only 1–2 years. So cell surgery works for selected cases, but its advantage over simpler surgery and its long‑term benefits remain uncertain.

- **Full Answer**:  
Cartilage cell surgery (autologous chondrocyte implantation and its newer versions) can work for knee cartilage damage, but mainly in specific situations and with some caveats.

In several randomized trials of younger adults with a single, localized cartilage defect, cell surgery improved pain and function over 1–2 years, similar to or better than other surgeries [1–4,6]. Compared with bone plug “mosaicplasty,” cell surgery produced equal or better knee scores and better-looking repair in some studies [1,2,6]. Compared with microfracture (making small holes in bone), standard cell surgery gave similar short‑term results [4], while matrix‑based and “characterized” cell techniques showed somewhat better function or better‑quality repair tissue in others [3,5].

However, about one‑third of patients improved substantially after simple arthroscopic cleanup alone in one trial [1], and long‑term results (beyond a few years) and benefits in generalized arthritis are still unclear [2,5].

- **Rationale**:  
Multiple randomized trials show that cartilage cell surgery (autologous chondrocyte implantation, including MACI and characterized chondrocytes) usually improves pain and knee function in people with focal cartilage defects. In some studies it works about as well as other surgeries like microfracture or osteochondral “plug” transplants, sometimes slightly worse early on, sometimes better over two years or in keyhole assessments. Some patients with similar lesions even improved after simple clean‑up surgery alone, so more invasive cell procedures are not always necessary. Overall, the evidence does not show that cartilage cell surgery “doesn’t work”; instead it suggests it is one of several effective options, with pros and cons depending on defect size, location, and patient factors.

- **Full Answer**:  
Cartilage cell surgery does appear to work for many people with localized knee cartilage damage. Trials show substantial symptom and function improvement after cell implantation, similar to or better than mosaicplasty or osteochondral plug transplantation at around two years [1,2,6]. Compared with microfracture, standard ACI gave similar short‑term results [4], while matrix‑induced ACI showed better 2‑year outcomes [3]. Characterized chondrocyte implantation produced better-quality repair tissue than microfracture, with similar short‑term symptom relief [5]. However, about one‑third of patients in one study improved after simple debridement alone, without needing further surgery [1]. Overall, cartilage cell procedures are effective but not universally superior; the best choice depends on lesion size, location, age, activity level, and response to simpler treatments.

- **Rationale**:  
Studies looked at special computer keyboards as supportive tools to improve wrist and hand position in people with hand pain or carpal tunnel–like symptoms. One six‑month trial found that an alternative “split” keyboard reduced self‑reported hand pain and improved hand function compared with a standard keyboard, especially with one specific model, but did not clearly improve physical exam findings of nerve problems [1]. Another 12‑week trial found that a keyboard with a different keyswitch design modestly reduced pain and slightly improved one provocative test for carpal tunnel, without changing nerve conduction or overall hand function [2].

- **Full Answer**:  
Supportive tools that improve wrist and hand position, such as certain ergonomic keyboards, can modestly reduce carpal tunnel–type hand pain over weeks to months [1][2]. Some users also notice small improvements in hand function or exam tests [1][2]. However, these tools do not reliably correct nerve damage or fully restore function for everyone [1][2].

- **Rationale**:  
The studies look at whether changing keyboards (a type of “supporting tool” or ergonomic aid) helps people with hand pain and possible carpal tunnel–type symptoms. Some alternative keyboards led to less pain and better reported hand function after several months, but they did not consistently improve objective medical tests of nerve function or physical examination findings. This means they can make people feel better but don’t clearly reverse the underlying nerve problem. Benefits were modest, took weeks to months to appear, and were not seen with all designs. Thus, ergonomic supports are not useless, but they are only partly effective and should be viewed as one small part of a broader treatment plan rather than a stand‑alone cure.

- **Full Answer**:  
Supportive positioning and tools (like alternative keyboards) provide only modest, slow symptom relief for carpal tunnel–type hand pain. Some designs reduced pain and slightly improved hand function over months [1,2], but they did **not** consistently improve physical exam findings or nerve test results [1,2].

- **Rationale**: Both studies examined computer users with hand or upper extremity symptoms, similar to carpal tunnel problems. In a 6‑month randomized trial, some “alternative geometry” keyboards led to trends toward less hand pain and better hand function compared with a placebo keyboard, though physical exam findings did not clearly improve [1]. In a 12‑week trial, one conventional keyboard design reduced hand pain and improved a carpal‑tunnel–related test, but did not change nerve tests or hand function [2].

- **Full Answer**: Specially designed keyboards and similar ergonomic tools can modestly reduce hand pain associated with carpal‑tunnel–type symptoms over weeks to months [1][2]. Users reported less pain and somewhat better function with certain alternative or low‑force keyboards [1][2]. However, these tools did not consistently improve nerve function tests or objective clinical findings [1][2]. So, supportive positioning and ergonomic devices can help manage pain and comfort but should be viewed as only partial relief, not a cure.

- **Rationale**:  
The studies looked at “supporting tools” for the hands and wrists—different keyboard shapes and key mechanisms—to see if they help pain related to conditions like carpal tunnel syndrome. One 6‑month trial found that certain alternative keyboards led to a trend toward less hand pain and better hand function versus a standard keyboard, although exam findings did not consistently improve [1]. Another 12‑week trial found that one keyboard design reduced hand pain and improved a clinical test (Phalen’s test), but did not change nerve test results or overall hand function [2]. This means ergonomic tools can ease symptoms but are not a cure.

- **Full Answer**:  
Supporting tools that improve hand and wrist position are not useless; they can modestly reduce pain in people with carpal tunnel–type symptoms. Some alternative keyboard designs led to less hand pain and better hand function over several months compared with a standard keyboard [1]. Another keyboard design reduced pain and improved a clinical test for nerve irritation, though it did not improve nerve conduction or overall function [2]. So, they may help symptoms but won’t fully fix the underlying problem.

- **Rationale**:  
The articles mainly look at people with serious illness whose constipation is caused by opioid pain medicines. In these studies, “poop medicines” include standard laxatives (senna, lactulose), an herbal oil, and a newer drug called methylnaltrexone that targets opioid-related constipation. I focused on how many people had a bowel movement, how fast it happened, and side effects, compared with placebo or another laxative.  

- **Full Answer**:  
For constipation caused by opioid pain medicines, “poop medicine” can be quite effective.  

Methylnaltrexone works quickly: about 48–62% of patients had a bowel movement within 4 hours of a dose, versus 14–15% with placebo, and many went within 30–90 minutes [1,3,5]. It did not reduce pain relief or trigger opioid withdrawal, but common side effects were cramping and gas [1,3,5].  

Traditional laxatives such as senna and lactulose worked similarly well to each other; senna was preferred mainly because it was cheaper [2]. An Ayurvedic herbal oil also worked about as well as senna, with once‑daily dosing and low cost, but needs more research [4].

- **Rationale**:  
Studies looked at people with severe, opioid‑related constipation in advanced illness, who often did not respond to usual laxatives [1,3,5]. Methylnaltrexone was added on top of these laxatives. It worked quickly for about half, meaning it did not work that fast for the other half [1,3]. Standard laxatives and an herbal option were similarly effective, but still allowed gaps without bowel movements [2,4]. Because these patients are unusually hard to treat, results likely underestimate how well these drugs work for typical constipation.  

- **Full Answer**:  
In these difficult cases, poop medicine is helpful but far from perfect. Methylnaltrexone caused a bowel movement within four hours in about 48–62% of patients, so 38–52% did not get rapid relief [1,3]. Very low doses helped only 10% [5]. Usual laxatives (senna, lactulose, herbal) worked similarly to each other but did not fully prevent several‑day periods without bowel movements [2,4]. Effectiveness is probably higher in milder constipation.

- **Rationale**:  
The articles look at “poop medicines” (laxatives and methylnaltrexone) mainly for constipation caused by opioid pain medicines in people with advanced illnesses. Several good-quality studies compare these medicines with placebo or with each other and measure how often and how quickly patients can have a bowel movement, as well as side effects and pain control.

- **Full Answer**:  
Yes, certain “poop medicines” do work for constipation, especially when it is caused by opioid painkillers.  

Methylnaltrexone, an injection that blocks opioids only in the gut, made 48–62% of patients with advanced illness have a bowel movement within 4 hours, compared with 8–15% on placebo, often within 30–75 minutes [1,3,5]. It did not reduce pain relief or cause opioid withdrawal, but could cause cramping and gas [1,3,5].  

Common oral laxatives like senna and lactulose worked similarly well for terminal cancer patients on opioids, with no clear winner, though senna was cheaper [2]. An Ayurvedic herbal oil worked about as well as senna in one small study [4].

- **Rationale**:  
Trials in people with constipation from opioid pain medicines show that several “poop medicines” often trigger bowel movements and are better than placebo. They don’t work for everyone, but overall they help many patients.

- **Full Answer**:  
In people whose constipation is caused by opioid pain medicines, several “poop medicines” do work.

The drug methylnaltrexone led to a bowel movement within 4 hours in about 48–62% of patients, versus 14–15% with placebo injections, and many went within 30 minutes [1][3]. Doses of 5 mg or more were clearly better than very low doses [5]. Importantly, it did not reduce pain control or cause opioid withdrawal [1][3][5].

Standard laxatives like senna and lactulose worked similarly well to each other in terminal cancer patients on opioids [2], and an Ayurvedic herbal laxative was as effective as senna [4].

So, “poop medicine” often helps, but you may need the right type and dose for your situation.

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks how well a “new medicine” works for a first serious brain problem, which in these studies is a first episode of psychosis (such as early schizophrenia). Both articles compare newer antipsychotics (risperidone or olanzapine) with an older drug (haloperidol) over 6 weeks. Effectiveness is measured by how much symptoms improve and how many patients respond, and safety is judged by movement side effects (like stiffness, tremor, and restlessness). The data show that risperidone is at least as effective as haloperidol and better tolerated, and olanzapine is more effective and better tolerated than haloperidol. This supports the idea that newer antipsychotics are effective short‑term options in first‑episode psychosis.

- **Full Answer**:  
In people having a first episode of serious psychosis (a severe brain-related mental illness), newer antipsychotic medicines work at least as well as older drugs, and often better, with fewer movement side effects.  

In one 6‑week study, about 63% of people taking risperidone had their overall symptoms cut in half, similar or slightly better than 56% on haloperidol, but with clearly fewer stiffness, tremor, and restlessness problems [1]. Many first‑episode patients did well on relatively low doses [1].  

In another 6‑week trial, olanzapine improved symptoms more than haloperidol on several standard rating scales, and about two‑thirds of patients on olanzapine responded versus only about one‑third on haloperidol [2]. Olanzapine also caused fewer movement side effects and restlessness [2].  

Overall, for a first serious psychotic episode, newer medicines like risperidone and especially olanzapine appear effective short‑term and are generally easier to tolerate than older drugs like haloperidol for most patients [1][2].

- **Rationale**:  
The question seems to ask how often newer antipsychotic medicines *do not* work in people having their first episode of a serious psychotic illness (like schizophrenia). In these studies, “worked” was defined as a large drop in symptom scores after about 6 weeks of treatment. So we can estimate “ineffectiveness” as the percent of patients who did *not* reach that level of improvement.

- **Full Answer**:  
The “new” medicines studied here are risperidone and olanzapine, used for a first episode of psychosis (serious mental/brain disorder such as schizophrenia) [1][2].  

In the risperidone study, 63% of first‑episode patients improved by at least 50%, meaning about 37% did **not** improve that much within 6 weeks [1].  

In the olanzapine study, 67.2% improved by at least 40%, so about 33% did **not** reach that level of response in 6 weeks [2].  

So, roughly one‑third of people with a first psychotic episode did not have a strong response to these newer medicines over the short term, even though they generally worked better and caused fewer movement side effects than older drugs like haloperidol [1][2].

- **Rationale**:  
  The question likely refers to newer antipsychotic medicines (like risperidone and olanzapine) used for a first episode of serious mental illness such as psychosis or schizophrenia. Two clinical trials compared these newer drugs to an older antipsychotic (haloperidol) in people having their first psychotic episode. Both studies looked at how much symptoms improved and how well patients tolerated the medicines.

- **Full Answer**:  
Yes. In people experiencing their first serious psychotic episode, newer antipsychotic medicines do work and are at least as effective as older drugs, often with fewer movement side effects.  

In one 6‑week trial of 183 patients, 63% of those taking risperidone had at least a 50% reduction in symptom scores, similar to haloperidol (56%), but risperidone caused fewer movement problems and less need for extra medicines to treat side effects [1].  

In another 6‑week trial of 83 first‑episode patients, olanzapine led to greater overall symptom improvement and higher response rates (67.2% vs 29.2% with haloperidol), and caused fewer movement side effects such as stiffness and restlessness [2].

- **Rationale**:  
The question seems to ask if “new medicines” (newer antipsychotic drugs) fail to work in people with a first serious “brain problem,” likely meaning a first episode of psychosis or schizophrenia. The two articles summarize randomized trials comparing newer antipsychotics (risperidone, olanzapine) with an older drug (haloperidol) in first‑episode psychosis. Both studies show that the newer medicines clearly reduce symptoms and often have better results and fewer side effects than the older drug. Therefore, the concern that new medicines “do not work” in first‑episode serious psychotic illness is not supported by this evidence.

- **Full Answer**:  
In people having a first episode of a serious psychotic illness (such as schizophrenia), newer antipsychotic medicines do work, and often work better and more safely than older drugs.

In one study of people with their first psychotic episode, about 63% of those taking risperidone had their overall symptom score cut at least in half over 6 weeks, similar or slightly better than the older drug haloperidol (56%), but with fewer movement‑related side effects and fewer people stopping the drug because of problems [1]. Lower doses were often enough for good benefit, which also reduced side effects [1].

In another study of first‑episode psychosis, 67% of patients taking olanzapine met a standard definition of clinical response, compared with only 29% taking haloperidol [2]. Olanzapine improved symptoms more and caused fewer movement problems [2].

So, current evidence suggests newer antipsychotics do work well for first‑episode serious psychotic disorders.

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks about the effectiveness of a medicine (azathioprine) for a specific liver disease, primary biliary cirrhosis. The first, smaller trial showed only limited and temporary improvements in liver tests and symptoms, with no clear effect on progression to cirrhosis or survival except a weak late signal based on very few patients [1]. The larger, better-designed international trial provides stronger evidence: it shows azathioprine modestly but significantly improves survival and slows worsening disability, with relatively few side effects [2]. It still does not cure the disease or fully prevent liver scarring, and prognosis is worse when the disease is more advanced [2]. Overall, the medicine is moderately effective, offering meaningful but not dramatic benefit.

- **Full Answer**:  
Azathioprine is a medicine studied for primary biliary cirrhosis, a chronic liver disease. In an early small trial, it briefly improved a blood liver test during the first year and may have reduced itching, but it did not clearly stop the liver from scarring or improve most lab values [1]. A later, larger international trial in 248 people showed clearer benefits: azathioprine reduced the chance of death to about 60% of that with placebo, extending average survival by about 20 months and slowing down worsening disability [2]. Side effects were relatively few in this study [2]. However, azathioprine does not cure the disease or fully prevent cirrhosis; people with higher bilirubin, older age, cirrhosis, low albumin, or more severe bile duct blockage still had a worse outlook overall [2]. These results suggest azathioprine is effective: it can extend life and slow progression, but regular monitoring and specialist care remain essential.

- **Rationale**:  
Azathioprine is one medicine studied for primary biliary cirrhosis, a chronic liver disease. The smaller early trial found only temporary improvement in one liver enzyme and no clear effect on liver scarring or early survival, suggesting limited benefit [1]. A later, much larger trial gave a clearer picture: azathioprine modestly but significantly improved survival and slowed disability, with relatively few side effects [2]. However, many patients still died and liver damage continued to progress [1][2]. So the medicine is not useless, but it is far from a cure.  

- **Full Answer**:  
In primary biliary cirrhosis, azathioprine offers modest help, not a cure. In one trial, it briefly improved a liver enzyme but did not stop scarring or clearly improve survival for the first five years [1]. In a larger study, it reduced the risk of death by about 40% and added roughly 20 months of life on average, with few side effects [2].

- **Rationale**:  
The question is broad, but the articles only address one specific autoimmune liver disease: primary biliary cirrhosis. The first, smaller trial showed limited short-term lab improvement and symptom relief, but no clear long-term benefit on liver scarring or early survival [1]. The larger, higher‑quality international trial did show that azathioprine modestly improved survival and slowed disability, with few side effects [2]. However, these results apply only to this disease, not all liver conditions.

- **Full Answer**:  
These studies looked at azathioprine, an immune‑suppressing medicine, in people with primary biliary cirrhosis, a chronic autoimmune liver disease [1][2].  

In a smaller early trial, azathioprine briefly improved one liver blood test in the first year, and patients may have had less itching, but it did not stop scarring of the liver or clearly improve survival during the first five years [1].  

A later, larger international trial found more convincing benefits. Compared with placebo, azathioprine reduced the risk of death to about 60%, extending average survival by roughly 20 months and slowing worsening disability, with relatively few side effects [2].  

These results show that for this specific liver disease, azathioprine can modestly improve length and quality of life, though it does not cure the disease or fully prevent cirrhosis [1][2]. They do not prove that azathioprine, or medicines in general, will help all types of liver disease, which have many causes.

- **Rationale**:  
The articles describe studies of azathioprine, a medicine used for a specific liver disease called primary biliary cirrhosis (now often called primary biliary cholangitis). In the smaller, early trial, liver enzyme tests improved in the first year but this did not clearly slow scarring (cirrhosis) or change most blood tests long‑term, though itching seemed better and survival was slightly better only after several years, with few patients left to study [1]. A later, much larger international trial found clearer benefits: people taking azathioprine lived longer, with their risk of death reduced to about 60% of that in the placebo group and an average survival gain of about 20 months; it also slowed worsening disability, with relatively few side effects [2]. This shows that medicine can help some forms of liver disease, though it may not cure them and the benefit can vary by disease stage and patient factors [2].

- **Full Answer**:  
No. These studies show that medicine can help some liver diseases, but often in limited ways rather than as a complete cure.  

In an early, smaller trial in primary biliary cirrhosis, azathioprine improved one liver enzyme test for about a year, and patients seemed to have less itching, but most blood tests and the progression to cirrhosis were similar to no treatment; survival only looked better after several years, with very few patients to evaluate [1].  

A later, much larger international trial found clearer benefits: azathioprine reduced the risk of death to about 59% of that seen with placebo and extended average survival by about 20 months, and it slowed worsening disability, with relatively few side effects [2].  

The larger study led researchers to recommend azathioprine as routine treatment for this liver disease [2]. So, medicine can work for liver disease, but the effect depends on the specific disease and how advanced it is.

- **Rationale**:  
I integrated results from six randomized clinical trials of colesevelam in adults with type 2 diabetes. I focused on how much it lowered long‑term blood sugar (A1c), daily glucose levels, and LDL (“bad”) cholesterol, and what side effects occurred compared with placebo or standard diabetes medicines.

- **Full Answer**:  
Colesevelam is not a cure, but it modestly improves blood sugar when added to usual type 2 diabetes medicines.

In people whose diabetes was not well controlled on metformin, sulfonylureas, or insulin, adding colesevelam lowered A1c by about an extra 0.5 percentage points compared with placebo over 3–6 months (for example, from 8.1% to about 7.6%) [2][3][4][5]. In early diabetes, starting metformin plus colesevelam gave a larger A1c drop (1.1% vs 0.8% with metformin alone) and more patients reached A1c under 7% [1]. Colesevelam also consistently reduced LDL cholesterol by roughly 10–16% [1][2][3][4][5].

The main side effect is constipation; weight and hypoglycemia risk did not increase versus placebo [2][3][4][5]. Overall, it provides a small but useful extra benefit.

- **Rationale**:  
The articles examine colesevelam added to standard diabetes drugs (metformin, sulfonylureas, insulin). Across studies, adding colesevelam typically lowered HbA1c (a measure of average blood sugar) by about 0.3–1.0 percentage points compared with placebo, and improved LDL (“bad”) cholesterol [1–5]. In early type 2 diabetes, metformin plus colesevelam lowered HbA1c from 7.8% to 6.6%, and 67% of patients reached the usual HbA1c goal <7%, versus 56% on metformin alone [1]. In people already on other medicines, colesevelam added about a 0.5% extra HbA1c drop [2–5]. However, many patients still did not reach recommended targets [1], and side effects (mainly constipation) were more common with colesevelam [2]. Mechanistic data suggest modest improvements in how the body handles glucose, not a cure [6]. So, medicines are not “ineffective”; they provide modest but meaningful improvements but do not fully normalize blood sugar for everyone.

- **Full Answer**:  
Based on these studies, type 2 diabetes medicines are **not** ineffective, but their benefits are **modest, not curative**.

When colesevelam was added early to metformin, average HbA1c (“3‑month average” blood sugar) fell from 7.8% to 6.6%, and 67% of patients reached the usual target <7%, compared with 56% on metformin alone [1]. In people whose diabetes was already not well controlled on pills or insulin, adding colesevelam typically lowered HbA1c by about an extra 0.5 percentage point versus placebo [2–5], and also improved LDL (“bad”) cholesterol [1,3–5].

However, a substantial fraction of patients still did **not** reach blood sugar goals [1], and side effects like constipation were more frequent with colesevelam than with placebo [2].

Mechanistic work shows improved handling of blood sugar, but no restoration to normal function [6].

Overall, medicines meaningfully improve blood sugar and cholesterol, but they do not usually normalize diabetes on their own and must be combined with lifestyle changes.

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks if medicine “works” for type 2 diabetes, which I interpret as whether medicines can meaningfully lower blood sugar and improve related risk factors. All provided articles are randomized, placebo-controlled trials testing colesevelam (sometimes with metformin, sulfonylureas, or insulin). Across multiple studies lasting 8–26 weeks, adding colesevelam consistently lowered HbA1c (a measure of average blood sugar) compared with placebo, often by about 0.5 percentage points or more, and improved LDL (“bad”) cholesterol. Side effects were generally similar to placebo, with constipation more common in one study. These findings show that, in real patients whose diabetes was not fully controlled, medication adjustments produced measurable improvements in blood sugar and cholesterol, supporting the conclusion that medicines do work for type 2 diabetes while they are taken.

- **Full Answer**:  
Yes. Several clinical trials show that medicines can meaningfully improve type 2 diabetes control.

People with early type 2 diabetes who took metformin plus colesevelam lowered their average three‑month blood sugar (HbA1c) from 7.8% to 6.6%, and more reached the common goal of under 7% than with metformin alone [1]. In people whose diabetes was not well controlled on pills, adding colesevelam lowered HbA1c by about 0.5 percentage points over 12–26 weeks [2,4,5]. In those already on insulin, adding colesevelam also cut HbA1c by about 0.5 points [3].

Colesevelam also improved “bad” LDL cholesterol by about 10–16% in these studies [1–5]. The most common side effect was constipation in one study, but overall side effects were similar to placebo across trials [1–3].

So in these trials, medicines lowered blood sugar and cholesterol in adults with type 2 diabetes over weeks to months [1–5].

- **Rationale**: These studies show several diabetes drugs, including colesevelam with standard treatments, lower blood sugar and cholesterol, but they do not cure type 2 diabetes.

- **Full Answer**: Medicines for type 2 diabetes do work, but they are not a cure.

In people with early type 2 diabetes, metformin plus colesevelam lowered A1C (a long‑term blood sugar test) from 7.8% to 6.6% in 16 weeks, more than metformin alone [1]. When colesevelam was added to pills like metformin or sulfonylureas, A1C fell about 0.5% more than placebo and fasting and after‑meal sugar levels improved [2],[4],[5]. Similar benefits occurred when it was added to insulin therapy [3]. Colesevelam also reduced LDL (“bad”) cholesterol and some inflammation markers [1],[2],[3],[4],[5].

One study suggests it may help the body use insulin more effectively over time [6].

So, medicines can meaningfully improve blood sugar and cholesterol in type 2 diabetes, but healthy eating, activity, and monitoring remain essential.

- **Rationale**: Studies tested prazosin, a blood pressure medicine, for Raynaud’s phenomenon (cold, pale or blue fingers) linked to autoimmune or skin disease.

- **Full Answer**: In trials, the blood pressure drug prazosin modestly helped cold, Raynaud-type fingers. In 14 patients with lupus, mixed connective tissue disease, or idiopathic Raynaud’s, 8 of 9 in this subgroup had fewer attacks on prazosin than on placebo [1]. Blood-flow tests also showed lower resistance in finger arteries, meaning better circulation, but only in these non‑scleroderma patients [1]. Among 5 people with progressive systemic sclerosis (a skin and connective tissue disease), only 1 reported improvement with prazosin in that study [1]. However, another trial in 19 scleroderma patients found prazosin reduced both how often and how severe Raynaud’s attacks were, compared with placebo [2]. Overall, prazosin can help Raynaud’s, including in scleroderma, but benefits are moderate and individual response varies. Side effects need careful medical supervision.

- **Rationale**: The question likely refers to prazosin, a blood‑pressure medicine tested for Raynaud’s phenomenon (cold fingers) caused by autoimmune or scarring skin diseases. One small trial showed poor benefit in scleroderma, while another, larger trial in scleroderma patients found clear improvement. In non-scleroderma autoimmune conditions, prazosin worked well. So it is not “very ineffective,” but its benefit is uncertain and varies by disease type and person.

- **Full Answer**:  
Doctors sometimes use prazosin, a blood‑pressure medicine, to treat cold, painful fingers caused by Raynaud’s phenomenon from autoimmune skin diseases.

In one small study of 14 people, prazosin clearly helped most patients whose Raynaud’s was linked to lupus, mixed connective tissue disease, or occurred on its own: 8 out of 9 reported fewer attacks, and blood‑flow tests showed better opening of finger arteries [1]. In contrast, among 5 people with scleroderma (a scarring skin disease), only 1 reported benefit and blood‑flow did not measurably improve, suggesting limited effectiveness in that group [1].

However, a separate, larger trial in 19 patients with scleroderma‑related Raynaud’s found that prazosin significantly reduced how often attacks happened and how severe they felt compared with placebo [2].

Overall, prazosin is not reliably effective, but it can meaningfully reduce cold‑finger attacks for many patients, though responses vary between individuals; results are especially mixed in scleroderma‑related disease [1][2].

- **Rationale**:  
Two studies tested prazosin, a blood pressure drug, for Raynaud’s phenomenon, which causes cold, pale fingers from narrowed blood vessels. In people whose Raynaud’s was linked to lupus, mixed connective tissue disease, or no known cause, most had fewer attacks and better blood flow on prazosin than on placebo [1]. In those with scleroderma (a skin–tightening autoimmune disease), one study found little benefit [1], but another, larger study in scleroderma patients showed prazosin clearly reduced how often and how badly their finger attacks occurred [2].  

- **Full Answer**:  
Some blood pressure medicines, such as prazosin, can help cold fingers caused by Raynaud’s. Studies show prazosin can reduce the frequency and severity of Raynaud’s attacks and improve finger blood flow [1][2]. However, not everyone with skin disease responds, and research is based on small groups [1][2]. Prazosin can lower blood pressure too much, so it should only be tried under a doctor’s guidance.

- **Rationale**: These trials studied prazosin, a blood-pressure drug, for Raynaud’s. One found little effect in scleroderma, another found clear benefit, suggesting it can work but not always.

- **Full Answer**:  
Prazosin is a medicine that has been tested for Raynaud’s phenomenon, which causes cold, pale fingers and can occur with connective tissue diseases such as progressive systemic sclerosis (scleroderma) [1][2]. In one small study, people whose Raynaud’s was linked to lupus, mixed connective tissue disease, or no known cause had fewer Raynaud’s attacks and better finger blood flow on prazosin, but people with scleroderma mostly did not improve [1]. In another study that only included people with scleroderma, prazosin clearly reduced how often attacks happened and how severe they were, compared with placebo [2]. Overall, a medicine like prazosin that is also used for blood pressure can help cold fingers related to conditions affecting the skin and connective tissue in some patients, but the benefit is not consistent for everyone [1][2].

- **Rationale**:  
The articles describe several types of community safety programs for burns: broad injury-prevention programs, burn-specific educational campaigns, and intensive local community interventions. Some programs improved safety knowledge and practices but did not clearly lower burn rates. Others, especially long-term, locally tailored programs with strong community involvement and environmental changes, showed substantial reductions in burn injuries or burn-related hospitalizations in young children. Effects were strongest for infants and toddlers and in higher socioeconomic areas, and weaker or absent for school-aged children. Media or school-only education was least effective.

- **Full Answer**:  
Overall, community safety programs for burns show mixed but sometimes meaningful benefits. Large, long‑term, community‑based efforts that combine data‑driven local hazard analysis, practical home changes, and strong community participation have cut burn injuries in young children by about half compared with a similar city without the program [4]. Other programs linked with burn prevention activities reduced hospitalizations for burns in infants and toddlers, especially in middle‑ and high‑income areas [3]. However, some broad or short‑term educational campaigns did not clearly reduce burn incidence or severity, even when they improved parents’ safety knowledge and behaviors [1,2]. School‑only programs and media campaigns alone appear least effective, and in one case burn rates even rose during a regional media campaign [2]. In summary, well‑designed, locally tailored, multi‑component community programs can substantially reduce burn injuries in small children, but simple or poorly targeted education alone is much less effective [1–4] for serious burns overall.

- **Rationale**:  
The articles show mixed results. Some broad community safety programs improved parents’ knowledge and safety behaviors but did not clearly reduce burn injuries. One large Boston burn-prevention campaign failed to lower burn incidence or severity and any small drop seen likely reflected chance, not the program. In contrast, more focused or intensive programs in specific settings did reduce serious burns: in Israel, hospitalizations fell among infants and toddlers in areas with programs, and in Norway, a long-term, locally driven program cut small‑child burns by about half. These findings suggest community burn‑safety programs are not simply “ineffective,” but their success depends on how intensive, targeted, and sustained they are, and effects are often modest or limited to younger children.

- **Full Answer**:  
Across studies, community burn‑safety programs are not useless, but their impact is often limited and uneven. A large multi‑injury program in Massachusetts improved parents’ burn‑safety knowledge and behaviors but did not measurably cut burn injuries themselves [1]. In Boston, a media and school‑based burn campaign did not lower burn rates or severity; a small drop seen in one community arm was likely just random year‑to‑year fluctuation [2]. In Israel, areas with burn‑prevention activities saw a modest fall in hospitalizations for burns in babies and toddlers (from about 1.4 to 1.0 per 1,000 each year), but there was no benefit for school‑aged children [3]. One Norwegian city with an intensive, long‑term local program cut small‑child burn injuries by about half, while a similar city without the program saw no improvement [4]. Overall, community programs often change knowledge more than outcomes, and strong, sustained, well‑targeted efforts seem required to significantly reduce burns.

- **Rationale**:  
Evidence about community safety programs for burns is mixed. A large multi-injury program improved parents’ burn safety knowledge and behavior but did **not** clearly reduce burn injuries themselves [1]. A Boston burn education project showed no consistent, reliable drop in burn incidence; an apparent small reduction was likely due to chance [2]. In contrast, a Norwegian community program with sustained local engagement cut burn injuries in young children by about half over seven years compared with a reference city [4]. An Israeli review found that communities with burn-prevention programs had fewer hospitalizations for burns in infants and toddlers—especially in better-off areas—but no benefit for school-aged children [3]. Overall, programs that are local, long-term, and actively involve the community seem more effective than short-term or media-only efforts.

- **Full Answer**:  
Community safety programs **can** work for skin burns, but success depends on how they are designed and delivered.  

A broad injury-prevention program improved parents’ burn safety knowledge and behaviors, yet did not clearly lower actual burn injury rates [1]. Another burn education campaign in Boston showed no consistent, reliable reduction in burn incidence; a small apparent benefit was likely due to random variation [2].  

However, a long-term, community-driven program in Norway cut burn injuries in young children by about 50% compared with a similar city without the program [4]. In Israel, towns with child burn-prevention activities had fewer burn-related hospitalizations among infants and toddlers, especially in middle- and higher-income communities, although no benefit was seen for older children [3].  

Taken together, community safety programs are most likely to reduce burn injuries when they are sustained over time, tailored to local risks, and actively involve families and community organizations, rather than relying only on short-term or media-only campaigns [1–4].

- **Rationale**:  
The studies show mixed results. A large U.S. injury‑prevention program did not clearly reduce burn injuries, though it improved burn‑safety behaviors [1]. A Boston burn‑education project mostly failed to lower burn incidence; an apparent small benefit from a community effort was likely due to chance [2]. However, other research shows clear benefits: Israeli communities with burn‑prevention programs saw fewer burn‑related hospitalizations in infants and toddlers, especially in higher‑income areas [3], and a Norwegian community program cut burn injuries in young children by about half compared with a similar town [4]. Thus, effectiveness depends on age group, setting, and program design.

- **Full Answer**:  
Community safety programs for burns do not always work, but some clearly can.

In one large U.S. program, coordinated community activities did not measurably reduce burn injuries, although families exposed to the program did use safer burn-prevention behaviors at home [1]. Another Boston project found that school‑based lessons did not lower burn rates, and a community initiative showed at best a short, uncertain reduction [2].

In contrast, an Israeli study found that communities with burn‑prevention programs had a significant drop in burn‑related hospitalizations for infants and toddlers, especially in middle‑ and high‑income areas [3]. A Norwegian community program cut burn injuries in children under five by about half compared with a similar town without the program [4].

Overall, community burn‑safety programs sometimes reduce serious burns in young children, but results are mixed and depend on program quality, intensity, and how well they are tailored to local risks and families [1–4].

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks about “weak muscles disease,” which matches myasthenia gravis, a condition where the immune system attacks the communication between nerves and muscles, causing muscle weakness. All the summarized articles are randomized or controlled studies of medicines used to treat myasthenia gravis. Across these trials, drugs that dampen the immune system (cyclosporine, azathioprine, prednisone, tacrolimus/FK506, cyclophosphamide, mycophenolate) were compared with placebo or with each other. The main outcomes were muscle strength, relapses, and ability to lower steroid doses. Most studies showed better strength, fewer relapses, or reduced need for steroids in the active‑treatment groups, with manageable but sometimes serious side effects (for example, kidney problems with cyclosporine and typical immune‑suppressive risks). Because the data come from controlled clinical trials, they provide reasonably strong evidence that these medicines are effective overall, though responses differ between individuals and careful monitoring is required.

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicines for myasthenia gravis (a disease causing weak muscles) are generally quite effective at improving strength and daily function, especially when used as immune‑suppressing therapy.  

In two randomized trials, cyclosporine led to significantly greater muscle strength than placebo and fewer treatment failures over 6–12 months [1][4]. Prednisolone plus azathioprine produced longer remissions, fewer relapses, and allowed most patients to taper or stop steroids over several years compared with steroids alone [3][7]. Low‑dose tacrolimus (FK506) shortened hospital treatment and reduced steroid needs without major side effects in new patients [5]. Monthly intravenous cyclophosphamide improved eye, chewing, and swallowing muscles and let some patients stop steroids and pyridostigmine [6]. A small study of mycophenolate showed more improvement than placebo, though results were preliminary [2].  

Overall, many patients experience meaningful, sometimes long‑lasting improvement, but benefits vary and all these drugs can have important side effects, so close medical monitoring is essential for safety.

- **Rationale**:  
The question seems to ask whether medicines for myasthenia gravis (a “weak muscles” autoimmune disease) work poorly. The provided trials all test immune‑suppressing drugs versus placebo or standard treatment. Across these studies, active drugs consistently improve muscle strength, reduce relapses, and allow lower steroid doses, with fewer treatment failures than placebo or single‑drug therapy. However, they are not perfect: some patients still deteriorate or stop drugs because of side effects (for example, kidney problems), so treatment is helpful but not 100% effective or risk‑free.

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicines for myasthenia gravis (a common “weak muscles” disease) are generally effective, not ineffective.  

In several trials, people taking immune‑suppressing drugs such as cyclosporine, azathioprine, tacrolimus (FK506), mycophenolate, or cyclophosphamide had better muscle strength and fewer serious relapses than those on placebo or steroids alone [1][2][3][4][5][6][7]. For example, in one 6‑month cyclosporine trial, there were no treatment failures in the cyclosporine group, but three in the placebo group [1]. In a 12‑month trial in newly worsening patients, again no cyclosporine‑treated patients had treatment failure, versus three on placebo [4]. Adding azathioprine to prednisone led to fewer relapses, longer remissions, and lower steroid doses [3][7]. Cyclophosphamide and tacrolimus also improved strength and allowed steroid reduction without major added side effects in most patients [5][6].  

However, medicines are not perfect: some patients still worsen, and up to about one‑third may stop a drug because of side effects like kidney problems [1][7] sometimes.

- **Rationale**:  
The articles all study myasthenia gravis, a disease where the immune system interferes with nerve‑to‑muscle communication, causing weak muscles. Several randomized, placebo‑controlled trials test different medicines. Across studies, patients on active drugs usually gained more strength and needed less steroid medicine than those on placebo, meaning the treatments were effective. Some drugs caused side effects, so benefits must be balanced against risks and monitored by specialists.

- **Full Answer**:  
“Yes” – for myasthenia gravis (a common “weak muscles disease”), several medicines have been shown to work.

Cyclosporine improved muscle strength and reduced abnormal antibodies compared with placebo in two randomized trials [1][4]. Mycophenolate mofetil also led to greater improvement than placebo in a pilot study [2]. Prednisolone (a steroid) helps, and adding azathioprine led to fewer relapses, longer remissions, and allowed lower steroid doses than prednisolone alone [3][7]. Low‑dose tacrolimus (FK506) reduced hospital treatment and daily steroid needs without major side effects [5]. Intravenous cyclophosphamide improved strength and let many patients taper or stop steroids [6].

These medicines can meaningfully improve weakness in many people with myasthenia gravis, but they require careful specialist supervision because of possible side effects, such as kidney problems with cyclosporine [1][4].

- **Rationale**:  
The summaries describe several clinical trials in people with myasthenia gravis, a disease that causes weak muscles. Across different studies, multiple medicines improved muscle strength, reduced relapses, or allowed lower steroid doses. Some patients did not respond or had side effects, so treatment must be individualized and monitored by specialists.

- **Full Answer**:  
It is not true that medicine “does not work” for the weak‑muscle disease myasthenia gravis. Several medicines have been shown to improve strength and disease control in many patients.  

Cyclosporine improved muscle strength and lowered harmful antibodies compared with placebo in two randomized trials [1][4]. Adding azathioprine to prednisone led to fewer relapses, longer remissions, and lower steroid doses than prednisone alone [3], and azathioprine alone performed at least as well as prednisone in another trial, with combination therapy helping severe cases that failed single drugs [7].  

Other immune‑suppressing drugs—mycophenolate mofetil [2], low‑dose tacrolimus (FK506) [5], and intravenous cyclophosphamide [6]—also showed better strength or reduced need for steroids or rescue treatments.  

These medicines are not cures, and they can cause side effects, so they must be tailored and monitored. But overall, the evidence shows that medicines often do work to improve weak muscles in myasthenia gravis [1–7].

- **Rationale**: The articles studied buflomedil, a drug for poor leg circulation causing pain when walking (intermittent claudication). In 40 people with type 2 diabetes, those taking buflomedil for 6 months increased the distance they could walk before pain by about 71%, and their maximum walking distance by about 68%, while placebo patients did not improve [1]. In 93 patients with artery blockages, buflomedil also led to clearly larger gains in pain‑free and total walking distance than placebo over 12 weeks [2].

- **Full Answer**: In people with leg pain from poor circulation, buflomedil can meaningfully increase how far they walk before pain starts and their total walking distance. Diabetic patients on buflomedil walked about two‑thirds farther after 6 months, while those on placebo did not improve [1]. In a larger study, buflomedil outperformed placebo over 12 weeks [2]. These results suggest moderate, not dramatic, benefit; other treatments and exercise remain important.

- **Rationale**:  
Two studies tested buflomedil, a medicine for leg pain from poor circulation (intermittent claudication). In 40 people with type 2 diabetes, those taking buflomedil for six months could walk about 70% farther before pain than at the start, while the placebo group did not show this improvement [1]. In a larger study with 93 people with blocked arteries, both the medicine and placebo groups improved, but the increase in pain‑free and total walking distance was significantly greater with buflomedil than with placebo [2]. These results show the drug is not useless: it offers a meaningful, though not dramatic, benefit. People can walk farther before pain, but it does not cure the condition, and improvements vary between individuals.

- **Full Answer**:  
Buflomedil is not highly ineffective; it provides a moderate benefit. In one study, treated patients walked about 70% farther before pain after six months, unlike placebo [1]. Another study showed significantly better walking distances with buflomedil than placebo [2].

- **Rationale**:  
The question is about leg pain when walking, which in these articles is due to poor blood flow in the leg arteries (intermittent claudication). Both studies tested the drug buflomedil versus placebo. In diabetic patients, buflomedil clearly increased the distance they could walk before pain started and their total walking distance, while placebo did not show such large gains [1]. In a larger general arterial disease group, walking distances improved in both groups (likely from general care and training), but the buflomedil group improved significantly more than placebo [2]. This shows the medicine can reduce symptoms and increase walking distance, though it is not a cure and other measures remain important.

- **Full Answer**:  
Yes, at least one medicine has been shown to help some people with leg pain when walking due to blocked leg arteries.  

In people with type 2 diabetes and intermittent claudication, six months of buflomedil increased the distance they could walk before pain began by about 71%, and their maximum walking distance by about 68%, compared with placebo [1].  

In another 12‑week trial in people with chronic arterial occlusive disease, both buflomedil and placebo groups improved, but pain‑free and total walking distances increased significantly more with buflomedil than with placebo [2].  

These studies suggest that buflomedil can lessen leg pain on walking and help patients walk farther, although results vary between individuals and it should be combined with other treatments such as exercise, risk‑factor control, and, if needed, procedures to improve blood flow [1][2].

- **Rationale**:  
The studies looked at people whose leg pain when walking was caused by poor blood flow, called intermittent claudication or arterial occlusive disease. In both trials, patients were randomly given either buflomedil (a circulation medicine) or a placebo (dummy pill), so researchers could see if the medicine truly helped. In the first study with people who had type 2 diabetes, only those taking buflomedil showed a large increase—about 70%—in how far they could walk before pain started and in total walking distance [1]. In the second, larger study, walking distance without pain and total distance improved in both groups, but the improvement was significantly greater in those taking buflomedil than in those on placebo [2]. This shows that, at least for this condition and medicine, drugs can reduce leg pain with walking and increase walking distance, though they don’t cure the disease and may not help everyone.

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicine can work for leg pain that appears when walking, especially when the pain is due to poor circulation (intermittent claudication).  

In one study of people with type 2 diabetes and leg pain from intermittent claudication, those taking buflomedil were able to walk about 70% farther before pain started and in total distance, while the placebo group did not show this benefit [1].  

In a larger study of patients with chronic arterial circulation problems, both the medicine and placebo groups improved somewhat, but the increase in pain-free and total walking distance was much greater with buflomedil than with placebo, and the difference was statistically significant [2].  

These results show that medicine can meaningfully improve walking distance and reduce leg pain in this condition, although it does not cure the underlying disease and may not work equally well for everyone [1][2].

- **Rationale**:  
The studies looked at repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS), a non‑invasive brain treatment applied over the motor cortex in people with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), a spinal motor neuron disease. In a 6‑month trial, patients getting real stimulation showed only a modest slowing of worsening compared with sham; both groups still deteriorated, and the small sample makes the result uncertain [3]. In a short 2‑week study, rTMS temporarily improved muscle strength and quality of life, but these benefits disappeared within two weeks after treatment stopped [2]. A larger, year‑long trial in more advanced ALS found no meaningful difference in disease progression between active and sham stimulation, despite good tolerability [1]. Together, these data suggest that while brain stimulation may offer brief, mild benefit for symptoms, it has not been shown to meaningfully alter the course of spinal motor neuron disease.

- **Full Answer**:  
Current evidence shows brain stimulation is only weakly and inconsistently effective for spinal motor neuron disease like ALS. In a 6‑month study, repetitive brain stimulation slightly slowed functional decline compared with sham treatment, but all patients still got worse, and the small number of participants means the result is uncertain [3]. A short 2‑week trial found temporary improvements in muscle strength and quality of life, yet these gains faded within two weeks of stopping treatment [2]. In a larger, year‑long, placebo‑controlled study in more advanced ALS, there was no meaningful difference in disease progression between active brain stimulation and placebo; the functional scores declined at a similar rate in both groups [1]. The treatment appeared safe in these studies [1,3], but overall it has not been proven to significantly change the course of the disease and is not an established, effective long‑term therapy.

- **Rationale**:  
Studies of brain stimulation (rTMS/cTBS) for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), a disease that weakens spinal muscles, show little real impact on the disease course. A year‑long, well‑designed trial found no difference in disability progression between real and sham stimulation; both groups declined similarly, leading researchers to say a larger confirmatory trial was not justified in advanced disease [1]. A smaller 6‑month trial suggested only a modest slowing of decline, and even that result was uncertain because of the small number of patients [3]. Another small study found brief improvements in strength and quality of life, but these gains disappeared within two weeks of stopping treatment [2]. Overall, these treatments do not stop or clearly slow ALS and any benefits appear small and short‑lived.

- **Full Answer**:  
Brain stimulation for ALS‑related spinal muscle weakness has, at best, modest and temporary effects and does not meaningfully change long‑term disease progression [1][2][3].

- **Rationale**:  
The question is about whether “brain treatment” (here, non-invasive brain stimulation of the motor cortex with repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation, rTMS) helps in spinal muscle disease, specifically ALS. One small study found a modest slowing of decline, but another, larger and longer study showed no benefit. A different rTMS approach gave short-lived improvements in strength and quality of life that quickly faded. All trials were small and experimental. Overall, evidence does not show a clear, lasting disease-modifying effect.

- **Full Answer**:  
Studies have used non‑invasive brain stimulation (repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation, rTMS) over the motor cortex in people with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), a disease that affects the nerves controlling spinal muscles.  

A small 6‑month study of one rTMS pattern (continuous theta‑burst stimulation, cTBS) found a modest slowing of functional decline compared with sham treatment, but all patients still got worse and the study was too small to be sure the effect was real [3]. A larger, year‑long trial of the same technique showed no difference between real and sham stimulation, so the authors felt further large trials in advanced ALS were not justified [1].  

Another small trial using a different rTMS pattern reported short‑term improvements in muscle strength and quality of life, which disappeared within two weeks after stopping treatment [2].  

Overall, brain stimulation appears safe but has not been proven to slow spinal muscle disease, and remains experimental [1–3].

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks whether “brain treatment” works for spinal muscle disease, here meaning amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS). The articles all test brain stimulation methods (rTMS, cTBS) targeting the motor cortex. One small 6‑month study found a modest slowing of decline, but everyone still got worse and the trial was underpowered [3]. A separate short trial found temporary gains in strength and quality of life that faded within two weeks of stopping treatment [2]. A longer 12‑month trial in more advanced ALS showed no difference between real and sham stimulation [1]. All studies report that the procedures are generally safe [1–3]. Overall, the evidence does not show a robust, lasting benefit on disease progression.

- **Full Answer**:  
Brain stimulation treatments that act on the motor cortex, such as repetitive transcranial magnetic stimulation (rTMS) and continuous theta‑burst stimulation (cTBS), have been tested in people with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), a motor neuron (“spinal muscle”) disease. In a 6‑month pilot study, cTBS slightly slowed functional decline compared with sham treatment, but all patients still worsened and the study was small [3]. A short 2‑week rTMS course produced temporary improvements in strength and quality of life that disappeared within two weeks after stopping treatment [2]. A larger, year‑long, placebo‑controlled study of repeated cTBS found no meaningful difference in disease progression between real and sham stimulation [1]. Across these studies, the procedures were generally safe but did not provide lasting or clearly clinically important benefits [1–3]. Therefore, brain stimulation cannot currently be considered an effective treatment for ALS, though research continues and it may help some symptoms temporarily [1–3] for patients.

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks about “movement stimulation,” which in these studies includes vertical pulsating surfaces, oscillating water beds, and rocking beds for premature babies with apnea (pauses in breathing). I looked at how each approach affected the number and severity of apnea episodes, oxygen levels, heart rate, and need for intervention. All three trials are small, controlled studies where each baby serves as their own control, so the evidence is promising but limited in size and duration.

- **Full Answer**:  
Several studies suggest that gentle movement of the baby or mattress can reduce apnea (pauses in breathing) in premature infants.

Vertical pulsating stimulation reduced the number of central and mixed apnea episodes, though it did not help obstructive apnea [1]. Because central apnea is the most common type in these babies, this may give benefit [1].

An oscillating water bed significantly cut the total number of apnea spells, especially the longest episodes and those linked with drops in heart rate. It improved central, obstructive, and mixed apnea to a similar degree [2].

A rocking bed also reduced apnea in almost all babies studied, decreased episodes associated with low oxygen levels, and lowered the need for interventions [3].

Overall, movement stimulation appears moderately effective, non‑invasive support for apnea of prematurity, but evidence comes from small, short‑term studies, so it should be considered an add‑on, not a replacement for standard care [1–3].

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks “how ineffective” movement stimulation is, but the articles show it is actually somewhat effective for many, though not all, breathing problems in premature babies. Different types of movement (vertical pulsation, oscillating water beds, rocking beds) were tested. All three studies report meaningful reductions in apnea episodes, especially more serious ones, but with limits: some types of apnea (like obstructive apnea or brief pauses) do not consistently improve. This means movement stimulation is not useless; it provides partial benefit and often reduces the need for emergency intervention, but it does not fully solve the problem.

- **Full Answer**:  
Movement stimulation is generally helpful, not ineffective, for breathing problems (apnea) in premature babies. In one study, vertical pulsating stimulation reduced the number of central and mixed apnea episodes, though it did not improve obstructive apnea [1]. Another study found that gently oscillating water beds significantly reduced apnea, especially the longest events and those linked with dangerous slow heart rate; this benefit was seen for central, obstructive, and mixed apneas, but not for brief pauses or periodic breathing [2]. A third study showed that 11 of 12 preterm infants had fewer apnea episodes when on a rocking bed, with fewer drops in oxygen levels and fewer needed interventions [3]. Overall, movement-based methods appear to provide meaningful but incomplete benefit: they reduce many serious apnea events but do not eliminate all breathing problems or help every type of apnea, so additional treatments may still be needed in many vulnerable infants [1][2][3].

- **Rationale**:  
Three small studies tested different types of movement stimulation in premature babies with apnea (breathing pauses). A vertical pulsating surface reduced central and mixed apnea (pauses due to the brain “forgetting” to breathe), but not obstructive apnea (pauses from blocked airways) [1]. Gently oscillating water beds reduced total apnea, especially long and severe episodes, across central, obstructive, and mixed types [2]. A rocking bed also lowered apnea frequency, oxygen drops, and staff interventions [3]. All methods were non‑invasive, but none eliminated all events, and studies were small and short. More research is needed before this becomes routine care.

- **Full Answer**:  
Overall, gentle movement stimulation can help with many breathing pauses (apnea) in premature babies, but it is not a complete solution.  

Vertical pulsating stimulation reduced the number of central and mixed apnea episodes, which are the most common types, but did not help obstructive apnea [1]. Gently oscillating water beds cut down total apnea episodes, especially the longest ones and those linked to dangerous slowing of the heart rate; they improved central, obstructive, and mixed events by similar amounts [2]. A rocking bed also decreased apnea episodes, reduced drops in oxygen levels, and lowered the number of times staff had to step in [3].  

These methods are non‑invasive and appear safe and easy to use [1][2][3]. However, they did not eliminate all breathing events [1][2], and short pauses were unchanged in one study [2]. Larger, longer studies are needed before movement stimulation becomes standard care in all neonatal units [1][2][3].

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks if movement stimulation “does not work” for breathing problems in premature babies. The articles focus on apnea of prematurity—pauses in breathing. Vertical pulsating stimulation (VPS) reduced central and mixed apneas (caused by immature brain control of breathing) but not obstructive apneas (caused by blocked airflow) [1]. Oscillating water beds significantly reduced apnea overall, including central, obstructive, and mixed types, especially the longest and most serious episodes, though very short pauses were not much changed [2]. A rocking bed also reduced apnea in nearly all infants, with fewer oxygen drops and fewer needed interventions [3]. These methods are non‑invasive and can be helpful, but they don’t completely eliminate all breathing problems and may be less effective for some types of apnea.

- **Full Answer**:  
Movement stimulation does work for many breathing problems (apnea) in premature babies, but not for all types or all episodes.

Vertical pulsating stimulation clearly reduced central and mixed apnea episodes, though it did not help obstructive apnea [1]. Gently oscillating water beds significantly reduced central, obstructive, and mixed apneas, especially the longest and most dangerous ones with slow heart rate, but had little effect on brief breathing pauses and periodic breathing [2]. A rocking bed also reduced apnea in almost all treated infants, with fewer oxygen drops and fewer emergency interventions needed [3].

These findings show that gentle motion can be a useful, non‑drug aid for apnea of prematurity, particularly for central and mixed apneas, but it is not a complete solution and may not fully control obstructive apnea or very short pauses [1–3].

- **Rationale**:  
The question is about how well bone graft surgery works to replace missing bone in the upper jaw/roof of the mouth where a tooth should be (often an “alveolar cleft”). The summaries describe outcomes of two improved techniques for this surgery: adding bone morphogenetic protein-2 (BMP‑2) and adding fibrin glue to bone grafts.

- **Full Answer**:  
Bone graft surgery to fill a gap in the upper jaw (roof of the mouth) where bone is missing for a tooth is generally effective, especially with newer techniques. In older patients with an alveolar cleft, using a collagen sponge plus BMP‑2 filled about 95% of the bony defect, compared with 63% using traditional hip bone grafts, and had fewer complications (11% vs 50%), less pain at the donor site, shorter hospital stay, and lower cost [1].  

Another study found that adding fibrin glue to the bone graft significantly reduced how much graft bone was lost (about 30% resorption vs 62% without glue) and resulted in larger bone volume and better-quality bone, supporting better graft integration [2].  

Overall, modern bone graft approaches can successfully rebuild bone in the gap and improve healing, though individual results and risks vary and require specialist evaluation.

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks how “ineffective” bone surgery is for filling a bony gap in the roof of the mouth, usually due to a cleft or missing tooth area. The studies describe standard bone grafts and newer techniques. Traditional iliac crest bone grafts do work but can lose a lot of bone and have more complications. Newer methods (BMP‑2 and fibrin glue) make bone healing more reliable, so the surgery is better described as imperfect but generally effective, especially with modern techniques.

- **Full Answer**:  
Bone graft surgery to replace missing bone in the upper jaw or roof of the mouth is not usually “ineffective,” but traditional methods have meaningful limitations. In older patients with a cleft-related gap, standard iliac crest bone grafts filled about 63% of the defect and had complications such as poor healing or graft exposure in about half of patients [1]. Another study of conventional grafting found that, on average, 62% of the grafted bone was resorbed (lost) within three months, meaning less than half the original graft volume remained [2]. These issues can reduce support for teeth or implants over time.  

However, adding newer materials can greatly improve results. Using bone morphogenetic protein‑2 on a collagen sponge filled about 95% of the defect with fewer complications (11%) and less pain [1]. Adding fibrin glue to traditional grafts cut bone loss roughly in half and improved graft volume and quality [2].

- **Rationale**:  
These studies examine surgery to fill bone gaps in the upper jaw (alveolar clefts), similar to gaps near the roof of the mouth where teeth are missing. In older patients, using a bone‑regenerating protein (BMP‑2) on a collagen sponge filled about 95% of the defect with new bone and caused fewer complications than traditional hip bone grafts [1]. Using fibrin glue with bone grafts reduced graft shrinkage (resorption) and improved graft volume and quality [2].

- **Full Answer**:  
Bone graft surgery can work well to close a bone gap in the upper jaw near the roof of the mouth, such as an alveolar cleft. In older patients, a method using BMP‑2 on a collagen matrix filled most of the gap with new bone (around 95%), with fewer wound problems and less pain than taking bone from the hip [1]. Adding fibrin glue can further reduce bone loss and improve how well the graft joins the existing bone [2].

- **Rationale**:  
The studies describe surgery to repair an “alveolar cleft” – a gap in the bone of the upper jaw where teeth sit, which is part of the front of the roof of the mouth. In older patients, a method using a collagen sponge plus BMP‑2 grew new bone that filled about 95% of the defect and had fewer complications than a standard hip‑bone graft [1]. Another study showed adding fibrin glue to traditional bone grafting reduced how much graft bone dissolved and improved graft volume and quality [2].
