- **Full Answer**: The malaria vaccine tested in these articles, called SPf66, does not work well enough to be a reliable way to prevent malaria. In some semi‑immune South American communities, it reduced malaria attacks by roughly 30–35%, and possibly more in a few age groups [2][3][7]. But in many other places—including African infants and children, Thai children, and Brazilian adults—there was no meaningful protection compared with placebo [1][4][6][8][9][10]. Overall, experts concluded SPf66 has no useful role in malaria control [1][8] in practice.

- **Rationale**:  
The articles all evaluate one older experimental malaria vaccine, SPf66. Across many trials in different countries and age groups, they consistently report on how well this specific vaccine worked (efficacy), its safety, and whether it reduced malaria illness. Results show SPf66 was generally safe but gave only weak or inconsistent protection, especially in young children and in some regions. A few studies in South America showed moderate benefit, but others in Africa, Asia, and Brazil did not. Researchers therefore concluded that SPf66, in its tested form, is not suitable for broad malaria control. This evidence answers the question for this particular vaccine and shows that “a malaria vaccine” can fail, without saying that all malaria vaccines are ineffective.

- **Full Answer**:  
These articles discuss one specific experimental malaria vaccine called SPf66, not all malaria vaccines. Overall, SPf66 was safe but its protection against malaria was weak and inconsistent. In several African and Asian trials, it showed little or no ability to prevent clinical malaria episodes in infants and children, with efficacy estimates near zero or even negative, meaning no benefit over placebo [1][4][6][8][9][10]. Some studies in South American adults and older children did find moderate reductions (about 30–60%) in falciparum malaria episodes [2][3][7], but results varied and confidence intervals were wide [7]. Because protection was unreliable, short‑lived, and often absent in the youngest children—those at highest risk—researchers concluded that SPf66 in its tested form “does not appear to have a role in malaria control” and that further efficacy trials were not warranted [1][8]. So, this particular malaria vaccine did not work well enough, but this does not rule out malaria vaccines.

- **Rationale**: Evidence comes from dietary and caries-prevention trials in dental care.

- **Full Answer**: In people getting new full dentures, two tailored nutrition counseling visits in the dental clinic led to a large change: fruit and vegetable intake rose by about 209 grams per day versus only 26 grams in those given usual dental care, and more people moved into an “action” stage of healthy eating change [2]. When frequent sugar‑eaters were advised to avoid sugary foods between meals and cut sugar at meals, levels of decay‑causing bacteria in saliva and plaque fell during the 6‑week restriction, then partly rebounded after advice stopped, showing at least short‑term changes in sugar use [3]. In schoolchildren at high risk of cavities, a multi‑year, individualized program that included dietary counseling as part of dental care cut new cavities by about 44% compared with standard care, implying meaningful, lasting changes in everyday habits including diet [5].

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks whether diet advice given in a dental context is ineffective at changing what people actually eat. The provided studies show that when dietary counseling is structured, tailored, and linked to dental treatment, it can significantly change food choices and related health outcomes. One randomized trial in denture-wearers showed a large increase in fruit and vegetable intake with nutrition counseling compared to standard dental care alone. Another study showed that asking people to restrict sugar led to measurable biological improvements, although these partly faded after the restriction ended. A comprehensive prevention program including dietary counseling and other measures clearly reduced new tooth decay in children. Together, these results indicate that diet advice associated with dental care is not “ineffective.” It can work, but impact is greatest when it is individualized, reinforced, and part of a broader preventive program, and may lessen once active support stops.

- **Full Answer**:  
Studies do not show that diet advice linked to dental care is completely useless; in fact, it can noticeably change what people eat.  

In denture-wearing adults, two brief, tailored nutrition counseling sessions given during dental treatment increased fruit and vegetable intake by about 209 grams per day over six weeks, while usual care without special diet advice increased it by only 26 grams per day [2]. More people in the counseling group also moved into “taking action” to improve their diets [2].  

When people with very frequent sugar intake were specifically asked to avoid between-meal sugars for six weeks, levels of cavity-causing bacteria in saliva and plaque dropped, but rose again after the restriction period ended, though they stayed below their starting levels [3].  

Comprehensive preventive programs that include dietary counseling, along with fluoride and hygiene support, substantially reduced new tooth decay in children compared with standard dental care [5].

- **Rationale**:  
Research in dental settings shows that when diet advice is structured, personalized, and linked to dental treatment, people do change what they eat. In denture-wearers, nutrition counseling substantially increased daily fruit and vegetable intake compared with usual care, proving that targeted advice can improve diet quality [2]. When people were specifically asked to cut down sugar, mouth bacteria that cause decay dropped, suggesting they did reduce sugary foods, although some effect faded after the advice period ended [3]. In children with early tooth decay, an intensive, patient-centered program that included dietary counseling, alongside other preventive steps, led to far fewer new cavities over several years, implying better everyday habits, including eating patterns [5]. Overall, the evidence supports that diet advice delivered in dental care can meaningfully influence what people eat, especially when it is tailored, reinforced, and combined with other support, rather than just brief or one-time instructions.

- **Full Answer**:  
Yes. When diet advice is personalized and given as part of dental care, it can change what people eat. In a randomized trial of denture-wearers, two sessions of tailored nutrition counseling in the dental setting led to a large increase in fruit and vegetable intake (about +209 g per day) compared with almost no change in those getting usual care (+26 g per day) [2].  

In another study, people asked to avoid sugar between meals and cut sugar at main meals for six weeks showed reductions in cavity-causing bacteria in saliva and plaque, indicating they likely reduced sugary foods; levels rose again after stopping, suggesting continued advice or support may be needed [3].  

For children with active early decay, a comprehensive program including dietary counseling and other preventive measures almost halved new cavities over 3.4 years, consistent with sustained improvements in everyday behaviours, including diet [5].

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks if diet advice from a dentist “does not work” for changing what people eat. To answer, we look for studies where dietary counseling was delivered in a dental or oral health context and then see if people’s actual eating behavior changed. One study in denture wearers tested tailored nutrition counseling versus standard dental care and measured fruit and vegetable intake. Another study asked frequent sugar-eaters to restrict sugar and then measured changes in cavity-causing bacteria—a good sign of real sugar reduction. A large trial in children combined dietary counseling with other preventive methods and measured new cavities over several years. Across these studies, people who received structured, personalized dietary advice linked to dental care did change their eating patterns or had outcomes that strongly suggest better diet. Therefore, the claim that diet advice from dentists “does not work” is not supported by this evidence.

- **Full Answer**:  
Overall, diet advice given in dental settings can work and can change what people eat.  

In one trial, denture-wearing adults who received two tailored nutrition-counseling sessions as part of their dental care increased their fruit and vegetable intake by about 209 grams per day, while those getting standard dental care without special diet advice increased by only 26 grams per day [2]. This shows that structured, personalized diet advice linked to dental treatment can meaningfully improve eating habits.  

Another study asked people with high sugar intake to avoid sugary foods between meals and cut sugar at main meals. During the 6‑week advice period, levels of cavity‑causing bacteria in their saliva and dental plaque fell, and stayed lower than before even 6 weeks after stopping the strict diet [3], implying real changes in sugar use.  

In children, combining dietary and hygiene counseling with other preventive measures significantly reduced new cavities [5].

- **Rationale**:  
The articles all evaluate mupirocin, an antibiotic ointment used mainly inside the nose to target Staphylococcus aureus. Across many trials, mupirocin reliably reduces or temporarily eliminates nasal carriage of this bacterium, often much better than placebo or other ointments. In some high‑risk groups (hemodialysis and peritoneal dialysis patients, and surgical patients known to be carriers), this decolonization clearly translates into fewer S. aureus infections. However, in broader hospital populations and many surgical settings, lowering nasal carriage does not consistently reduce overall surgical‑site or hospital infections, likely because other bacteria and other sources of infection are also important. Mupirocin is only modestly effective when MRSA is present at several body sites, and recolonization over time is common. Overall, the evidence supports targeted, not routine, use: it works well to clear bacteria in the nose and can reduce certain infections in carefully selected high‑risk carriers.

- **Full Answer**:  
Antibiotic nasal ointments like mupirocin are very good at killing Staphylococcus aureus bacteria in the nose. In several studies, they cleared nasal carriage in about 80–100% of treated patients, far more than placebo or other ointments [1,2,3,5,7,9]. In some high‑risk groups, this clearly led to fewer infections: hemodialysis patients had 1 vs 6 S. aureus infections with mupirocin [1], and peritoneal dialysis patients had many fewer catheter exit‑site infections (14 vs 44 episodes) [2]. In general surgery patients who were known carriers, overall S. aureus hospital infections dropped from 7.7% to 4.0% [8].  

However, for many surgical and nonsurgical patients, reducing nasal bacteria did not significantly lower total surgical‑site or hospital infections [3,4,5,7]. Mupirocin is modestly effective when MRSA (a drug‑resistant S. aureus) is present at several body sites [6]. Overall, mupirocin ointment is effective for decolonizing the nose and can reduce specific S. aureus infections in selected high‑risk carriers.

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks “how ineffective” antibiotic ointment is, so the focus is on its limits. Across many trials, intranasal mupirocin reliably reduces Staphylococcus aureus in the nose but often fails to lower the overall number of surgical or hospital infections. It does help certain high‑risk groups and specific infection types, but recolonization and modest effect sizes show it cannot be a stand‑alone solution for preventing bacterial infections.

- **Full Answer**:  
Antibiotic nasal ointments like mupirocin are quite good at temporarily clearing Staphylococcus aureus from the nose, but much less effective at actually preventing most infections.

In several large hospital trials, using mupirocin did not meaningfully reduce overall surgical-site or hospital-acquired infections: infection rates were almost identical between mupirocin and placebo in cardiac surgery patients (13.5% vs 9.1%) [3], orthopedic surgery [5], and nonsurgical inpatients (2.6% vs 2.8%) [4], and total postoperative infections in cardiovascular surgery were unchanged [7]. For widespread MRSA carriage, full-body eradication was low and similar to placebo (25% vs 18%) [6].

Mupirocin did significantly lower certain S. aureus infections in high‑risk carriers, such as dialysis catheter exit‑site infections and some nosocomial infections [1][2][8], but recolonization was common, requiring repeated treatment [9].

Overall, antibiotic ointment alone is often only modestly helpful and should not be relied on as the main way to prevent bacterial infections in practice [3][4][5][6][7].

- **Rationale**:  
The articles all study mupirocin, a prescription antibiotic ointment used mainly inside the nose. They show that it is very effective at reducing or temporarily clearing Staphylococcus aureus from the nose [1][2][3][5][7][8][9]. In certain high‑risk groups (hemodialysis, peritoneal dialysis, some surgical patients), this reduction in nasal bacteria leads to fewer S. aureus infections, especially at catheter sites or overall S. aureus hospital infections [1][2][8][9]. However, several large randomized trials found that even when nasal bacteria were cleared, the *overall* rate of surgical site or hospital infections did not consistently fall [3][4][5][7]. One study also noted that resistance to mupirocin can develop and is linked to treatment failure [6][9].  

- **Full Answer**:  
Antibiotic ointments can work for certain bacterial infections, but their benefit is limited and specific. In these studies, the prescription ointment mupirocin, used inside the nose, greatly reduced Staphylococcus aureus bacteria living there [1][2][3][5][7][8][9]. In dialysis patients and some surgical patients who carried this germ, using mupirocin cut the number of S. aureus infections in places like catheter exit sites or overall hospital infections from this bacterium [1][2][8][9]. However, several large trials found that even though mupirocin cleared bacteria from the nose, it did not clearly reduce the total number of surgical wound infections or all hospital-acquired infections [3][4][5][7].  

This means antibiotic ointment can be very useful for targeted problems—such as decolonizing the nose in high‑risk patients or treating small, superficial skin infections—but it is not a cure‑all, and it cannot replace oral or intravenous antibiotics for deeper or widespread infections. Overuse may also promote resistance in some bacteria [6][9].

- **Rationale**:  
Across many studies, the antibiotic ointment mupirocin clearly kills or suppresses Staphylococcus aureus bacteria in the nose and on skin, but its ability to *prevent or treat* deeper infections is limited and depends on the situation. Antibiotic ointments act only where they are applied and are mainly useful for minor skin/nasal problems or for reducing carriage of certain bacteria; they cannot replace full‑body (oral or IV) antibiotics for serious infections.

- **Full Answer**:  
Antibiotic ointments like mupirocin *do* work against bacteria, especially Staphylococcus aureus, on the skin and inside the nose. Multiple trials show mupirocin greatly reduces or clears nasal carriage of S. aureus [1][2][5][7][9]. In some high‑risk groups, this also lowers certain related infections, such as exit‑site infections in peritoneal dialysis patients and some hospital S. aureus infections in carriers [1][2][8].  

However, other studies found that while mupirocin removes bacteria from the nose, it doesn’t always reduce overall surgical‑site or hospital infections [3][4][5][7], and it is only marginally effective when MRSA is present at many body sites [6].  

So antibiotic ointment works locally against bacteria, but it cannot reliably prevent or treat all bacterial infections, especially deeper or widespread ones.

- **Rationale**:  
The two studies focus on how vitamin A affects general measles complications, not specifically blindness. In hospitalized children with severe measles, WHO‑recommended vitamin A greatly reduced overall illness and improved weight gain, suggesting it helps correct vitamin A deficiency, a major cause of measles‑related eye damage, but eye outcomes were not reported [1]. In milder, non‑hospitalized measles cases, a single 200,000 IU oil‑based vitamin A dose showed only modest and uncertain benefit for respiratory problems, and children with obvious eye disease (“non‑xerophthalmic”) were specifically included, so prevention of eye damage or blindness was not evaluated [2]. Thus, these trials support vitamin A for reducing measles severity, especially in deficient children, but do not directly measure prevention or treatment of measles‑related blindness.

- **Full Answer**:  
The articles do not directly measure how well vitamin A pills prevent or treat blindness from measles.  

In very sick, hospitalized children with measles, WHO‑recommended vitamin A doses significantly reduced overall illness scores by 61–85% and improved weight gain, showing strong benefit on general measles severity and likely correcting widespread vitamin A deficiency (90% had low vitamin A) [1]. However, eye outcomes or blindness were not reported [1].  

In milder, non‑hospitalized measles, a single 200,000 IU oil‑based vitamin A dose showed only limited and statistically uncertain benefit for respiratory complications, and patients with obvious eye disease were not the focus; again, eye damage and blindness were not assessed [2].  

Overall, these studies support vitamin A to lessen measles complications, particularly in deficient children, but they do not provide clear evidence on how effective vitamin A pills are specifically for preventing or reversing measles‑related blindness [1][2].

- **Rationale**: The provided studies tested vitamin A for general measles complications, not specifically blindness. In hospitalized African children, two recommended doses of vitamin A greatly reduced overall illness and respiratory infections, and improved weight gain, suggesting a strong clinical benefit in severe measles [1]. In milder, non-hospitalized Zambian children, a single oil-based dose showed only modest or uncertain benefit for cough and pneumonia, and the authors questioned the effectiveness of that particular regimen [2]. Eye outcomes or blindness were not measured in either trial, so we cannot directly quantify how (in)effective vitamin A pills are for preventing measles-related blindness based on these summaries alone.

- **Full Answer**: Based on these studies, we cannot say vitamin A pills are ineffective for measles‑related blindness, because eye problems were not studied [1][2]. Vitamin A clearly improved severe measles illness [1], while a single oil‑based dose gave only limited, uncertain benefit in milder cases [2].

- **Rationale**:  
The studies looked at vitamin A supplements in children with measles and measured how sick they became and how fast they recovered, mainly focusing on infections like pneumonia, diarrhea, and general illness, not on blindness. In very sick, hospitalized children, high-dose vitamin A greatly reduced overall illness and improved weight gain, and most had low vitamin A levels, suggesting deficiency is common in measles [1]. In milder, non-hospitalized cases, a single dose showed only modest or uncertain benefits in preventing complications [2]. Neither trial directly measured eye damage or blindness from measles. Because vitamin A deficiency is known to harm the eyes, doctors use it to lower the risk of eye problems in measles, but these specific studies do not prove it cures or reverses measles-related blindness.

- **Full Answer**:  
Based on these studies, vitamin A pills help children with measles be less sick overall, especially with serious infections, and improve recovery [1].  

In very ill, hospitalized children, high-dose vitamin A clearly reduced complications and improved weight gain [1]. In less severe, non-hospitalized measles, a single large dose gave at best modest, uncertain protection against complications like pneumonia [2].  

However, neither study directly tested whether vitamin A prevents or treats blindness from measles.  

Because vitamin A deficiency is common in measles and is known to damage the eyes, the WHO recommends vitamin A during measles [1]. But these particular trials do not show whether it can actually prevent or reverse measles-related blindness.

- **Rationale**:  
Both studies looked at whether high‑dose vitamin A given during measles changes how sick children become, not whether it reverses measles‑related blindness. In very ill, hospitalized children, WHO‑recommended vitamin A doses greatly reduced overall complications and improved weight gain [1]. In less‑ill outpatients, a single oil‑based dose had only modest or uncertain benefit, and two water‑miscible doses appear more effective [2]. Neither trial evaluated eye problems directly, so they cannot prove whether vitamin A reverses established blindness.  

- **Full Answer**:  
Vitamin A given during measles does help reduce how severe the illness is, especially in very sick children, and this supports current WHO advice to use high‑dose vitamin A when treating measles [1]. However, these studies did not measure night blindness, corneal ulcers, or permanent vision loss [1,2]. Because corneal scarring from measles is usually irreversible, vitamin A is most likely to prevent or limit eye damage, not restore sight that is already permanently lost.

- **Rationale**:  
Research on ear (auricular) acupuncture for cocaine addiction includes several randomized controlled trials. One small study in methadone patients showed more cocaine‑negative urine tests with true ear acupuncture than with relaxation or a needle control [1]. Another crack-cocaine study showed lower cocaine levels in urine with true ear acupuncture than placebo acupuncture for those who stayed in treatment longer, but no difference in self‑reported use [6]. However, the largest, high‑quality trial in 620 patients found no difference between true ear acupuncture, sham acupuncture, or relaxation in cocaine use or treatment retention [3]. Other trials likewise found no clear benefit of real versus sham acupuncture, no advantage of higher “doses,” and no direct effect on drug use outcomes when added to standard care [2][4][5][7]. Overall, benefits seen are small, inconsistent, and often similar to placebo or usual care.

- **Full Answer**:  
Overall, ear acupuncture has not been proven clearly effective for cocaine addiction.

One smaller study in people on methadone found that those getting ear acupuncture were more likely to have cocaine‑free urine tests than those getting relaxation or a needle control treatment [1]. Another study in crack users found lower cocaine levels in urine with ear acupuncture compared with placebo sites among patients who stayed more than two weeks [6].

However, the largest, well‑designed trial in 620 patients found that real ear acupuncture worked no better than sham acupuncture or relaxation for reducing cocaine use or keeping people in treatment [3]. Other randomized studies also found no important differences between real ear acupuncture, sham acupuncture, or usual psychosocial care, and no benefit of more sessions [2][4][5][7].

Taken together, the evidence does not support ear acupuncture as a reliable stand‑alone treatment for cocaine addiction; at most, it may be a limited optional add‑on within a full treatment program.

- **Rationale**: Most controlled trials find ear acupuncture works no better than sham acupuncture or simple relaxation for cocaine addiction. The largest study in 620 people saw similar reductions in cocaine use and completion rates across real acupuncture and control groups [3]. Other randomized trials also show no meaningful differences between true ear acupuncture, sham needling, or standard care in drug use, craving, or retention [2][4][5][6][7]. A small earlier study that suggested benefit has not been reproduced [1].

- **Full Answer**: Overall, ear acupuncture appears largely ineffective as a specific treatment for cocaine addiction. In the biggest study, people getting real ear acupuncture did no better in cocaine‑free urine tests or staying in treatment than those getting fake acupuncture or simple relaxation [3]. Other trials also find that true and sham acupuncture, or standard counseling alone, lead to similar drops in cocaine use and craving [2][4][5][6][7]. Any extra benefit, if present, is really too small and unreliable to depend on.

- **Rationale**: Research is mixed. One small study in methadone patients found fewer cocaine‑positive urine tests with ear acupuncture [1]. But larger, better‑designed trials in various treatment settings generally showed no meaningful advantage over sham acupuncture or relaxation for cocaine use or treatment retention [2][3][4][7].

- **Full Answer**: Based on current studies, ear (auricular) acupuncture is not a reliably effective treatment for cocaine addiction. The largest trial, with 620 patients, found no difference between true ear acupuncture, sham acupuncture, or relaxation in reducing cocaine use or keeping people in treatment [3]. Other randomized trials likewise failed to show clear benefits of acupuncture over control conditions [2][4][7]. A few small studies reported modest improvements in urine tests or craving, but results were mixed and not confirmed in larger research [1][5][6]. Ear acupuncture should not replace proven treatments like counseling and behavioral therapies; at most, it may be considered an optional add‑on with limited expected benefit [2][3][4].

- **Rationale**:  
Research on ear (auricular) acupuncture for cocaine addiction has mixed results. A few small studies found that people getting true ear acupuncture had more cocaine‑negative urine tests than relaxation or sham acupuncture groups, suggesting a possible benefit [1,6]. However, most larger and better-designed trials did *not* find that ear acupuncture worked better than sham acupuncture, relaxation, or standard treatment alone for reducing cocaine use, preventing relapse, or keeping people in treatment [3,4,5,7]. One study of several “add‑on” methods (including acupuncture) found they were linked to staying longer in treatment, but none clearly improved drug use outcomes by themselves [2]. Overall, the pattern is that any improvements usually occur in all groups, including placebo/sham, which means the specific effect of ear acupuncture is uncertain.

- **Full Answer**:  
Current evidence does **not** show that ear acupuncture is a reliably effective, stand‑alone treatment for cocaine addiction.  

A few small studies suggest it might modestly reduce cocaine use compared with placebo or relaxation [1,6]. However, the largest and most rigorous trials found no meaningful differences between real ear acupuncture, sham acupuncture, or relaxation in reducing cocaine use, cravings, or improving treatment retention [3,4,5,7].  

One study of adjunct therapies found that receiving *any* extra therapy (including acupuncture) was associated with staying in treatment longer, but acupuncture itself did not clearly improve cocaine use outcomes [2].  

Taken together, these results mean ear acupuncture **cannot be considered a proven or primary treatment** for cocaine addiction. At best, it may serve as an optional add‑on, but it should not replace evidence‑based treatments such as counseling, behavioral therapies, and appropriate medical care [3,4].

- **Rationale**:  
Randomized research comparing day (outpatient) cataract surgery with hospital (inpatient) surgery found no meaningful differences in vision or overall health outcomes several months after surgery [1]. Visual acuity and patients’ perceived visual function were similar in both groups, meaning the operation worked just as well whether patients stayed overnight or went home the same day [1]. Outpatients did have more minor complications in the first 24 hours (such as discomfort or transient issues), but by four months, complication rates were the same, so these early problems did not affect long‑term results [1]. Another study of age‑matched patients also found that doing cataract surgery entirely on an ambulatory basis did not reduce the success of the procedure, showing hospital admission is usually not medically necessary [2]. Day surgery was clearly cheaper than inpatient care, making it more cost‑effective while remaining safe and effective when proper follow‑up is arranged [1][2].

- **Full Answer**:  
Day (outpatient) cataract surgery is highly effective and generally as safe as staying in hospital overnight. In a large randomized trial, patients who went home the same day had similar vision, perceived visual function, and overall health status compared with those admitted to hospital, when checked several months after surgery [1]. Early (first 24‑hour) complications were somewhat more common in outpatients, but these did not lead to worse outcomes at four months; complication rates then were the same in both groups [1]. Another study of age‑matched patients confirmed that doing cataract surgery entirely on an ambulatory basis, with immediate walking and follow‑up outside the hospital, did not reduce the procedure’s success [2]. Importantly, day surgery costs significantly less than inpatient surgery, making it more cost‑effective while maintaining good clinical results [1]. Overall, for suitable patients with appropriate post‑operative support, day surgery for cataract is a safe, effective, and economical option [1][2].

- **Rationale**: Day (ambulatory) cataract surgery was directly compared with inpatient surgery in randomized and observational studies, which showed similar long‑term vision and safety, with only a small rise in short‑term minor complications.

- **Full Answer**: Day (outpatient) cataract surgery is not ineffective compared with staying in hospital. In a large randomized trial of over 900 patients, people having outpatient surgery had the same vision and overall eye health as inpatients four months after surgery, with no difference in final complication rates or perceived quality of life [1]. The only drawback was more minor problems in the first 24 hours (64 vs 43 people; about a 60% higher relative risk), which did not affect later outcomes [1]. Another study of 250 patients likewise found that doing cataract surgery entirely on an ambulatory basis did not reduce the chance of success [2]. Overall, day surgery works just as well as inpatient care, while costing less [1].

- **Rationale**:  
The key issue is whether patients need to stay in hospital for cataract surgery or if they can safely go home the same day (day/ambulatory surgery). One randomized trial compared day surgery with inpatient care and looked at vision, complications, patient-reported health, and costs. It found no difference in vision or long-term complications, though minor problems were a bit more common in the first 24 hours for day-surgery patients, without lasting effects. Another study of age‑matched patients found that doing all aftercare on an outpatient basis did not reduce the success of surgery. Both studies also point out cost savings for outpatient care.

- **Full Answer**:  
Yes. Day (ambulatory) surgery for cataract works as well as inpatient surgery for most suitable patients. A large randomized trial found no meaningful differences in vision, long‑term complications, or patients’ perceived health between those treated as outpatients and those kept in hospital [1]. Outpatients had more minor complications in the first 24 hours (like discomfort or temporary issues), but by four months there was no difference in overall complication rates or visual results [1]. Another study of 250 cataract patients showed that immediate walking after surgery and complete outpatient follow‑up did not reduce the success of the operation, indicating hospitalization is not medically necessary for most cases [2].  

Outpatient cataract surgery was also cheaper than inpatient care, making it more cost‑effective while maintaining similar safety and outcomes [1].

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks whether cataract “day surgery” (outpatient/ambulatory surgery, where you go home the same day) is ineffective. The first trial directly compares outpatient with inpatient cataract surgery. It shows no important differences in vision, long‑term complications, or patient‑reported health, although there are slightly more minor early (first 24 hours) complications in outpatients, which do not affect outcomes at four months. Outpatient surgery is also cheaper. The second study confirms that hospitalization is not required: cataract surgery can be done safely with early walking and follow‑up care outside the hospital. Together, these show that day surgery is effective and safe for appropriate patients and is more cost‑effective than staying in hospital.

- **Full Answer**:  
Day (ambulatory) surgery for cataract generally works very well and is considered safe and effective.  

In a large randomized trial comparing outpatient and inpatient cataract surgery, vision after surgery, longer‑term complications, and patients’ own ratings of vision and overall health were the same in both groups [1]. Outpatients had more minor complications in the first 24 hours (such as temporary discomfort or pressure) than inpatients, but by four months there was no difference in complication rates [1]. Outpatient surgery also cost significantly less than hospital admission [1].  

Another study of 250 patients found that cataract extraction did not require hospitalization when patients could walk soon after surgery and receive all follow‑up care on an outpatient basis in a non‑hospital setting [2].  

Overall, current evidence shows that day surgery for cataract does work: it provides similar visual results and safety to inpatient care, at a lower cost for most patients [1][2].

- **Rationale**:

- **Full Answer**:  
In premature infants with serious early breathing problems, erythromycin was not very effective at preventing or improving long‑lasting lung disease.

In one trial, giving erythromycin for 7 days from birth did not reduce how often chronic lung disease occurred, how severe it was, or markers of inflammation in the lungs [1].

In another study, erythromycin successfully cleared Ureaplasma bacteria from most colonized babies’ airways, but this did not reduce how long they needed extra oxygen or improve longer‑term respiratory outcomes [2].

Instead, long‑term lung problems were more strongly linked to how premature the babies were and other complications such as late infections and heart vessel (PDA) issues [2].

Overall, in these studies, antibiotics helped clear certain germs but did not meaningfully improve chronic lung disease in high‑risk preterm infants [1][2].

The question asks how ineffective antibiotics are for a long‑lasting lung problem, based on the provided studies. Both articles tested erythromycin in very premature, ventilated infants at high risk for chronic lung disease. One trial gave erythromycin from birth to all such babies; the other treated only those colonized with Ureaplasma. In both, the antibiotic did not improve the key long‑term lung outcome: needing oxygen weeks later. Although erythromycin reduced bacterial colonization, it did not change how long babies needed oxygen or how often chronic lung disease developed. This shows that, in this context, antibiotics have little to no effect on chronic lung damage, which is mainly driven by prematurity and other complications.

- **Full Answer**:  
Two good-quality studies in very premature, ventilated babies show that the antibiotic erythromycin does not generally meaningfully prevent chronic lung problems.

In the first trial, babies were randomly assigned to receive erythromycin for 7 days after birth or no antibiotic. The rates and severity of chronic lung disease (defined as needing oxygen at 36 weeks’ gestation) were the same in both groups, and markers of lung inflammation did not improve with erythromycin [1]. In other words, treating all high‑risk babies with this antibiotic did not reduce long‑term lung damage.

In the second study, erythromycin successfully cleared Ureaplasma infection from the airways in 86% of treated infants, but this did not shorten the time they needed extra oxygen or lower the chance of needing oxygen at 36 weeks [2]. Long‑term lung outcomes were instead driven mainly by how premature the baby was and other complications, not by antibiotic use [2] overall.

One group of babies was given the antibiotic erythromycin; another group was not. Erythromycin did kill or reduce the germ in many babies [1][2]. However, the key question is whether this improved the babies’ long‑term lung condition. Studies in premature babies tested whether the antibiotic erythromycin could prevent or lessen chronic lung disease. Although the drug often cleared Ureaplasma bacteria from the lungs, it did not reduce inflammation, the severity of lung damage, or the long‑term need for oxygen.

- **Full Answer**:  
In the studies summarized, antibiotics did not meaningfully improve long‑lasting lung problems in premature infants.

In one trial, babies under 30 weeks’ gestation who needed breathing machines were randomly given erythromycin for 7 days or no antibiotic. The antibiotic made no difference in lung inflammation or in how many babies developed chronic lung disease, defined as needing oxygen at 36 weeks’ gestation [1].

In a larger group, erythromycin cleared Ureaplasma bacteria from most colonized infants, but treated and untreated babies needed oxygen for just as long, and oxygen use at 36 weeks was unchanged [2].

So, in these cases, antibiotics did not help the chronic lung problem [1][2].

The question is broad (“rash on genitals”), but the articles focus on a specific chronic inflammatory condition of the vulva and foreskin called lichen sclerosus/balanitis xerotica obliterans. These data give the best evidence about how well topical creams work on that type of genital rash. Across several randomized, controlled trials, very strong steroid creams (like clobetasol or mometasone) clearly reduce symptoms (itching, pain, tightness) and improve the skin’s appearance under the microscope, whereas hormone creams (testosterone, dihydrotestosterone) are no better than plain ointment and may even worsen symptoms. One non‑steroid immune cream (pimecrolimus) also helps but is somewhat less effective than clobetasol. Simple emollient creams help comfort but don’t reverse disease. Because many other causes of genital rash exist (including infections and STIs), and steroids can be harmful in the wrong setting, any topical steroid should be used only after a proper medical diagnosis.

- **Full Answer**:  
Topical creams can be very effective for certain chronic inflammatory genital rashes, but results depend strongly on the type of cream and the exact diagnosis.

For vulvar lichen sclerosus (a common chronic rash of the female genitals), very strong steroid creams such as clobetasol 0.05% relieved itching and pain in about 75% of women and also improved the skin under the microscope, clearly outperforming testosterone, progesterone, or plain cream [5]. Another trial confirmed clobetasol worked better than the non‑steroid pimecrolimus, though both reduced symptoms and inflammation and were well tolerated [7]. In boys with a related foreskin condition (balanitis xerotica obliterans), a potent steroid (mometasone) improved tightness in many, especially when used early, and slowed worsening compared with placebo [4].

By contrast, hormone creams (testosterone or dihydrotestosterone) generally did no better than plain ointment and sometimes worsened symptoms [1–3,6].

Because genital rashes have many causes, any steroid cream should be used only under medical supervision.

- **Rationale**:  
The articles mainly study a specific genital skin condition, lichen sclerosus (and the related balanitis xerotica obliterans in boys), not every possible “genital rash.” Several trials show that many non‑steroid creams (especially hormone creams like testosterone) are no better than plain moisturizer, and sometimes worse. In contrast, strong steroid creams clearly improve symptoms and skin appearance. So the “ineffectiveness” really depends on what cream is used and what the diagnosis is.

- **Full Answer**:  
For some genital rashes like lichen sclerosus, many topical creams are quite ineffective, especially hormone creams:

- A large trial found 2% testosterone ointment worked no better than plain petrolatum; symptom relief was 66.6% with testosterone vs 75% with placebo, and neither improved the underlying skin damage [3].  
- In another study, clobetasol (a very strong steroid) relieved symptoms in 75% of patients, compared with only 20% for testosterone, 10% for progesterone, and 10% for a simple cream base [5].  
- After successful clobetasol treatment, switching to testosterone actually worsened symptoms, while plain emollient cream maintained control [6].  
- Dihydrotestosterone improved skin appearance but did not improve itching or pain [1].  

In contrast, potent topical steroids like clobetasol and mometasone significantly improved disease signs and symptoms in both women and boys [4][5][7].

- **Rationale**:  
Studies of specific genital skin conditions show that some prescription creams help, but not all creams are effective. High‑potency steroid creams clearly improve vulvar lichen sclerosus and early balanitis xerotica obliterans, while hormone creams (testosterone or dihydrotestosterone) are inconsistent and often no better than plain moisturizer. [1][2][3][4][5][6][7]

Yes, certain topical prescription creams can work for some rashes on the genitals, but it depends on the exact diagnosis and product.  
For vulvar lichen sclerosus, a very strong steroid cream such as 0.05% clobetasol relieved symptoms in about 75% of women and produced clear tissue improvement, outperforming testosterone, progesterone, and placebo creams. [5][7]  
On the penis, a potent steroid (0.05% mometasone) improved early and intermediate balanitis xerotica obliterans and may slow worsening in later disease. [4]  
In contrast, testosterone or dihydrotestosterone creams often did not improve symptoms, and simple ointment worked just as well or better. [1][2][3][6]

Very strong steroid creams (like clobetasol 0.05%) clearly improve symptoms and skin changes in most people with vulvar lichen sclerosus and are considered the treatment of choice [5][7]. In boys with balanitis xerotica obliterans, a potent steroid (mometasone 0.05%) improved early and intermediate disease and may slow worsening in late disease [4]. In contrast, hormone creams such as testosterone or dihydrotestosterone sometimes improve the appearance of the skin but often do not reliably relieve itching or pain, and in some cases work no better than plain ointment or can worsen symptoms after steroid treatment [1][2][3][6].  

- **Full Answer**:  
Topical cream can work for some genital rashes, but it must be the *right* cream for the *right* diagnosis.  

For vulvar lichen sclerosus (a chronic inflammatory condition of the vulva), very strong prescription steroid creams such as clobetasol 0.05% relieve symptoms (itching, pain) and improve skin changes in most patients and are considered the preferred treatment [5][7].  

In boys with a related condition on the penis (balanitis xerotica obliterans), a potent steroid cream (mometasone 0.05%) improved narrowing of the foreskin in many, especially when the disease was in early or intermediate stages, and may help prevent worsening even in late stages [4].  

However, hormone creams like testosterone or dihydrotestosterone show mixed results: they may improve the look of the skin but often do not reliably relieve symptoms and can be no better than simple ointment; testosterone can even worsen symptoms after good response to steroids [1][2][3][6].  

So if an over‑the‑counter or non‑steroid cream is not helping your genital rash, it does not mean that all topical treatments are useless. It likely means you need a precise diagnosis and an appropriate prescription cream—often a potent steroid—under a clinician’s guidance.

Research has tested thyroid hormones as a treatment for short‑term, serious kidney injury, because animal studies suggested they might speed healing. In a high‑quality trial in people with acute kidney failure, adding thyroxine did not improve kidney recovery, dialysis needs, or survival, and deaths were actually higher in the thyroid‑treated group [2]. In kidney transplants with delayed function, triiodothyronine likewise did not improve recovery or long‑term graft performance [1].

- **Full Answer**:  
Based on current human studies, thyroid medicine is not an effective treatment for kidney damage by itself. In acute kidney failure, giving high‑dose thyroxine did not reduce the need for dialysis, did not speed kidney recovery, and did not lower death rates; mortality was actually higher and linked to stronger thyroid‑stimulating hormone (TSH) suppression [2]. In kidney transplant patients with delayed graft function, triiodothyronine also showed no benefit in recovery time, dialysis needs, or 1‑year kidney function [1].

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks how ineffective thyroid medicine is for kidney damage. The provided articles report two well‑designed clinical trials in humans: one using triiodothyronine (T3) after kidney transplant and one using thyroxine (T4) in acute renal failure. Both showed no kidney benefit, and the thyroxine trial suggested possible harm. I will summarize these results in simple terms and directly answer how ineffective thyroid medicine is in this context.

- **Full Answer**:  
Clinical trials show standard thyroid hormones do not help people with serious kidney injury.  

In kidney-transplant patients with delayed graft function from acute tubular necrosis, triiodothyronine (T3) did not reduce the need for dialysis, speed kidney recovery, shorten hospital stay, or improve 1‑year graft survival or creatinine levels compared with placebo [1]. Outcomes were essentially the same with or without T3 [1].  

In a separate trial of thyroxine in acute renal failure, thyroxine suppressed thyroid‑stimulating hormone as expected but did not change kidney recovery, dialysis needs, or overall severity of kidney failure [2]. Deaths were actually more common with thyroxine: 43% in the treated group versus 13% in the placebo group, and higher mortality was linked to stronger TSH suppression [2].  

Overall, these studies suggest thyroid hormone treatment is ineffective for improving kidney damage and may even worsen outcomes in critically ill patients with acute kidney failure in practice [1][2].

- **Rationale**: Two good-quality trials tested thyroid hormones in people with serious kidney injury. One gave triiodothyronine (T3) to kidney transplant patients with delayed graft function; the other gave thyroxine to patients with acute renal failure. Neither study showed better kidney recovery or less need for dialysis, and the thyroxine group had higher death rates. So thyroid hormone did not help kidneys and might be harmful.

- **Full Answer**: Based on current research, thyroid medicine is generally not an effective treatment for kidney damage. In transplant patients whose new kidneys were slow to start working, T3 did not reduce the need for dialysis, speed recovery, shorten hospital stay, or improve kidney function at one year [1]. In people with acute renal failure, thyroxine did not improve recovery or survival, and deaths were higher in those who received it [2]. Thyroid hormone should only be used when true thyroid disease is clearly present [2].

- **Rationale**:  
Two clinical trials tested thyroid hormone as a treatment for short‑term kidney injury. In people with delayed kidney function after a transplant, extra triiodothyronine (T3) did not improve recovery, dialysis needs, hospital stay, or one‑year kidney function compared with placebo [1]. In patients with acute renal failure, thyroxine also failed to improve kidney recovery, dialysis needs, or survival, and deaths were actually higher in the thyroxine group [2]. These studies were in very ill patients whose thyroid tests already reflected “sick‑euthyroid” changes, not true thyroid disease.

- **Full Answer**:  
Current research shows thyroid medicine should not be given to try to heal kidney damage in people whose thyroid is normal. In transplant‑related delayed kidney function, adding T3 did not speed recovery or improve long‑term kidney performance [1]. In acute kidney failure, thyroxine gave no kidney benefit and was linked to higher death rates [2]. Thyroid drugs are only appropriate for thyroid hormone deficiency.

- **Full Answer**:  
Studies of adenoid surgery in children show it is only slightly helpful, and often no better than not operating. In a trial of young children with repeated ear infections, adenoidectomy did not reduce treatment failures, ear infection episodes, doctor visits, antibiotic use, or days with respiratory symptoms compared with placebo or long‑term antibiotics; risk differences were small (about 10–18%) and statistically uncertain [1]. Overall, for chronic “nose problems” from enlarged adenoids, surgery offers at best modest short‑term relief and no clear long‑term advantage over simply waiting for children to grow naturally [1][2].

Another study followed children with long‑standing nasal blockage: both those who had surgery and those who did not got better over time, with only a small, short‑lasting extra improvement from surgery. In toddlers with repeated ear infections, adenoid surgery did not prevent new infections or reduce symptoms compared with medicine or placebo [1]. - **Full Answer**:  
Based on these studies, adenoid surgery does not reliably solve long‑lasting nose and ear problems for most young children. For kids under 2 with repeated ear infections, adenoidectomy did not prevent new infections or reduce symptoms compared with non‑surgical treatment [1]. One large study that specifically included womb (endometrial) cancer found that being treated in a high‑volume or teaching hospital did *not* clearly improve long‑term survival once age, stage, and other factors were taken into account [1]. Short‑term (30–90 day) survival looked better in high‑volume centers at first, but this benefit disappeared after proper statistical adjustment [1]. For ovarian cancer, several studies show that specialist cancer centers and specialist gynecologic surgeons achieve better staging, more complete tumor removal, fewer repeat operations, and better survival [2–5]. This suggests that specialist skills and organized cancer teams can improve care, particularly for more complex gynecologic cancers, even if the effect is less obvious for womb cancer specifically.

- **Full Answer**:  
For womb (endometrial) cancer, a large study comparing high‑volume/teaching hospitals with other hospitals did **not** find a clear long‑term survival advantage after accounting for age, cancer stage, and other factors [1]. However, for ovarian cancer, treatment in specialist or high‑volume hospitals and by gynecologic cancer specialists leads to more accurate staging, better tumor removal, fewer repeat operations, and improved survival [2–5]. These findings indicate that specialized cancer teams and experienced surgeons can improve outcomes for complex gynecologic cancers.  

So, for womb cancer specifically, current evidence does **not** show a strong overall survival benefit from being in a special or high‑volume hospital [1]. Still, specialist centers may offer advantages for more complicated cases, combined treatments, and access to expert teams, as seen clearly in ovarian cancer care [2–5].

Survival depended much more on how advanced the cancer was and whether women received surgery and chemotherapy [1]. Thus, treatment in special hospitals is not shown to be less effective for womb cancer.

- **Full Answer**:  
Research looking at gynecologic cancers, including womb (endometrial) cancer, compared outcomes for women treated in large, specialized or teaching hospitals with those treated in other hospitals. For endometrial cancer, women at high‑volume centers were a bit more likely to survive the first 30–90 days after treatment, but when researchers adjusted for age, stage of disease, and other factors, there was no meaningful long‑term survival difference between specialized/high‑volume hospitals and other hospitals [1]. In other words, treatment in special hospitals was not less effective, but it was also not clearly more effective overall for womb cancer [1]. Outcomes were driven much more by how advanced the cancer was, the woman’s age and general health, and whether she actually received surgery and chemotherapy, which were both linked to better survival [1]. So current data do not show that specialized hospitals give markedly worse or better long‑term results for womb cancer overall outcomes.

Other studies show benefits of specialist centers for ovarian cancer, but not directly for womb cancer [2][3][4][5].

- **Full Answer**:  
Based on these studies, treatment in special hospitals does not clearly improve survival for womb (endometrial) cancer. In the only study that included endometrial cancer specifically, women treated in teaching or high‑volume hospitals did not live longer than those treated elsewhere after other important factors were taken into account [1]. What mattered more was how advanced the cancer was and whether women received proper surgery and, when needed, chemotherapy [1]. - **Rationale**: Studies that directly include womb (endometrial) cancer found that being treated in a high‑volume or teaching hospital did not clearly improve long‑term survival once age, stage of disease, and treatments received were taken into account [1]. However, these specialized centers did show slightly better short‑term (30–90 day) survival for womb cancer patients [1]. Other studies in ovarian cancer suggest benefits from specialized teams [2–5], but they cannot be assumed to apply equally to womb cancer.

- **Full Answer**: Treatment for womb cancer does work, and the main things that affect survival are how advanced the cancer is, your age, and whether you receive recommended surgery and any needed chemotherapy [1]. One large study found no clear overall survival advantage for womb cancer patients treated in higher‑volume or teaching hospitals, after adjusting for these factors [1]. That means specialized hospitals are not useless, but not yet proven to improve long‑term survival for womb cancer.

The articles all examine follicular lymphoma, a common type of blood cancer. They consistently show that modern treatments can put a large proportion of patients into complete remission and keep the disease controlled for years. More intensive approaches (high‑dose chemotherapy with autologous stem‑cell transplant) usually improve the length of time people stay in remission and sometimes survival, especially after relapse. However, using the most intensive treatments right away does not always help people live longer and can increase serious late side effects, such as second cancers. Therefore, these aggressive options are generally reserved for relapsed disease. A very deep response (molecular remission) is strongly linked to better long‑term control.

- **Full Answer**:  
These studies focus on follicular lymphoma, a common slow‑growing blood cancer. Standard chemotherapy with the antibody rituximab puts about 60% of high‑risk patients into complete remission, while more intensive chemotherapy plus stem‑cell transplant can raise this to about 80–85% and roughly double the chance of being free of events at four years (about 61% versus 28%) [1]. In relapsed disease, high‑dose therapy with autologous stem‑cell transplant improves both time without progression and overall survival; four‑year survival was about 71–77% with transplant versus 46% with standard chemotherapy [2]. Over longer follow‑up, nine‑year survival around 76–80% has been reported, although more intensive “first‑line” transplants did not clearly extend life overall and increased the risk of later second cancers [4][5]. Because of this, high‑dose therapy and transplant are usually reserved for relapse, where they remain a powerful option [1][2][5]. Achieving a deep (molecular) remission predicts better control [1], for many patients over years.

- **Rationale**:  
The question is broad (“blood cancer”), but all provided articles focus on follicular lymphoma, a common slow‑growing blood cancer. I therefore base the answer on how effective current treatments are for this disease. I explain that treatments often work well at controlling cancer and extending life, but many patients relapse and cure is uncommon. I use the trial data to show response rates, how long patients stay in remission, survival, and important downsides such as secondary cancers and treatment‑related risks.

- **Full Answer**:  
Modern treatment for follicular lymphoma, a common slow‑growing blood cancer, is not ineffective, but it has limits. Standard chemo plus rituximab puts about 60% of high‑risk patients into complete remission, yet only about 28% remain event‑free at 4 years [1]. More intensive, transplant‑based regimens can raise complete remission rates to 80–85% and roughly double 4‑year event‑free survival or 9‑year progression‑free survival (about 60–64%) [1][4]. In relapsed disease, high‑dose therapy with autologous stem‑cell transplant improves both time without progression and 4‑year survival (about 70–77% versus 46% with standard chemotherapy) [2]. However, using very intensive treatment first‑line usually does not lengthen overall survival compared with good conventional regimens [1][4][5]. Many patients eventually relapse and need further therapy [1][4][5]. High‑dose approaches also carry higher risks, including difficulty collecting stem cells with some drug combinations and more secondary leukemias and solid tumors [3][4]. Overall, treatments can control the disease for many years, but cures are rare and side‑effects can be serious [1][2][4].

- **Rationale**:  
The articles focus on follicular lymphoma, a common blood cancer. Standard chemotherapy plus the antibody drug rituximab puts many people into complete remission, but the disease often returns. More intensive treatment—high‑dose chemotherapy followed by a transplant of the patient’s own stem cells—can deepen remission, keep the cancer away longer, and in some situations improve survival. However, this approach also brings higher risks, including serious side effects and secondary cancers. Because standard treatment works well for many and high‑dose therapy can still be used if the cancer comes back, doctors usually reserve the most aggressive options for relapse.

- **Full Answer**:  
Yes. For follicular lymphoma, a type of blood cancer, standard chemotherapy with rituximab leads to complete remissions in most patients and good disease control, even if overall survival is similar between more and less intensive first treatments [1,5]. When the cancer returns, high‑dose chemotherapy with a patient’s own stem‑cell transplant significantly improves the chance of staying cancer‑free and can improve survival compared with more standard chemotherapy [2]. In newly diagnosed patients, doing a transplant immediately can keep the disease away longer and may cure a subgroup, but it does not clearly extend overall survival and increases the risk of serious second cancers [4,5]. Because of this risk–benefit balance, high‑dose therapy and transplant are generally reserved for relapse rather than used for everyone upfront [1,4,5].

The articles all study follicular lymphoma, a type of blood cancer. Standard chemo‑immunotherapy often puts patients into complete remission [1]. More intensive regimens and high‑dose chemotherapy with autologous stem‑cell transplant can deepen remission, keep the disease away longer, and in some settings extend overall survival [1][2][3]. Long‑term follow‑up shows many patients alive 9 years later and a subgroup that may be effectively cured, although intensive therapy raises the risk of later second cancers [4][5]. So treatment clearly works for many people, but is not perfect or risk‑free.

- **Full Answer**:  
Blood cancers like follicular lymphoma can often be treated successfully, but cure is not guaranteed for everyone. In newly diagnosed high‑risk follicular lymphoma, standard chemo plus rituximab put most patients into complete remission, and a more intensive regimen produced even more complete and molecular remissions and much longer periods without the disease getting worse [1]. When the cancer came back after standard treatment, many patients responded very well to high‑dose chemotherapy with their own stem cells, with high complete remission rates and several years of disease control [1]. In people whose lymphoma had relapsed, high‑dose therapy with stem‑cell transplant clearly improved how long they lived without progression and also improved overall survival compared with more chemotherapy alone [2]. Other long‑term studies show many patients are alive 9 years after treatment and some may even be cured, for life, though intensive treatments can raise the risk of later second cancers [4][5].
