The agility/core group returned to sport sooner on average and, more importantly, had far fewer re-injuries—none in the first 2 weeks and only about 8% within a year, versus over 50% and 70% in the stretching/strength-only group [2]. This indicates that well-designed, progressive exercises are effective for healing and for preventing hamstring re-injury.

- **Full Answer**:  
Exercise is an effective part of healing hamstring injuries, especially when it is specific and well-planned. In athletes with grade 2 hamstring strains, adding more intensive stretching to usual rehab shortened the time to regain normal knee movement (about 5.6 vs 7.3 days) and slightly reduced total rehab time (about 13.3 vs 15.1 days) [1]. Another trial found that a program based on progressive agility and core (trunk) stabilization helped athletes return to sport in about 22 days on average, compared with about 37 days for a program focused mainly on stretching and isolated hamstring strengthening, although this time difference was not statistically certain [2]. However, the agility/core program dramatically reduced re-injury: 0% vs 54.5% within 2 weeks, and 7.7% vs 70% within a year [2]. Overall, progressive, supervised exercise—especially including core and movement training—is highly effective for recovery and for preventing future hamstring problems.

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks whether exercise is ineffective for healing hamstring muscle injuries. The articles actually show that exercise-based rehabilitation is helpful, but some types of exercise programs work much better than others. More intensive stretching speeds recovery, and a program focused on movement control (agility and trunk stabilization) greatly reduces reinjury compared with simple stretching and isolated strengthening.

- **Full Answer**:  
Exercise is not ineffective for healing hamstring injuries; it is actually an important part of recovery, but the type of program matters. In one study of 80 athletes with hamstring strains, those doing a more intensive stretching program regained normal knee motion about 2 days faster (5.6 vs 7.3 days) and returned to full training about 2 days sooner (13.3 vs 15.1 days) than those with less stretching, showing clear benefit [1].  

In another study, both groups did exercise, but the group doing progressive agility and trunk (core) stabilization had far fewer repeat injuries than the group doing mostly stretching and isolated hamstring strengthening (0% vs 55% reinjury at 2 weeks; 8% vs 70% at 1 year) [2].  

So, exercise is effective, but simple stretching/strengthening alone is less protective than a well-designed, whole-body rehabilitation program.

- **Rationale**:  
Two clinical studies followed athletes with hamstring strains who did structured exercise programs. One found that more intensive stretching shortened recovery time. Another found that a program focused on whole‑body control (agility and core/trunk exercises), plus ice, led to quicker return to sport and far fewer repeat hamstring injuries than a program focused only on hamstring stretching and strengthening. Together, they show that exercise‑based rehab helps healing and that the *type* of exercise matters.

- **Full Answer**:  
Yes. Exercise‑based rehabilitation does help hamstring injuries heal and can speed safe return to activity.  
In one study of 80 athletes with hamstring strains, those who did a more intensive stretching program regained normal knee movement and completed rehabilitation about 2 days faster than those with a milder program [1].  
Another study of 24 athletes found that a program using progressive agility and core/trunk stabilization exercises plus icing led to faster return to sport and dramatically fewer re‑injuries than a program focused only on hamstring stretching and strengthening plus icing [2].  
These results suggest that guided stretching plus functional exercises for the hips, core, and legs work better than rest alone or simple stretching to heal hamstring injuries and prevent them from coming back [1][2].

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks if “exercise to heal muscles” doesn’t work for hamstring injuries. The articles show that targeted, well‑planned exercise (especially stretching and functional training) can *improve* recovery and reduce reinjury. One study shows that more intensive stretching speeds up regaining motion and overall rehab time. Another shows that a rehab program focused on agility and core stability leads to fewer repeat hamstring injuries than a program focused only on stretching and hamstring strengthening. So, exercise does work, but the *type* of exercise program is crucial.

- **Full Answer**:  
Exercise-based rehab does work for hamstring injuries, but the kind of exercise matters. In one study of 80 athletes with moderate hamstring strains, those who followed a more intensive stretching program regained normal knee movement in about 5.6 days and were fully rehabilitated in about 13 days, versus 7.3 and 15 days with a milder program, showing that properly dosed stretching sped recovery as part of rehab [1]. Another trial compared two exercise programs after acute hamstring strain. Both used ice, but one focused on stretching and isolated hamstring strengthening, while the other emphasized progressive agility and trunk (core) stabilization exercises [2]. Athletes in the agility/core group returned to sport sooner and, more importantly, had far fewer reinjuries: about 8% in a year versus 70% in the stretching/strengthening group [2]. Overall, structured, progressive rehab exercise is helpful for hamstring healing; it just needs to be designed and supervised [1][2].

- **Rationale**:  
The summaries focus on one medicine, cyclosporine, for a specific chronic liver disease called primary biliary cirrhosis (PBC). In large and small trials, cyclosporine improved blood tests of liver damage and bile blockage, eased symptoms like itching and tiredness, and appeared to slow scarring seen on liver biopsy [1][2][3]. One long-term study suggested it could delay death or the need for liver transplant by up to about 50%, but simpler survival analyses did not confirm a clear life‑saving effect [1]. Importantly, cyclosporine often harmed the kidneys and raised blood pressure, sometimes forcing dose reduction or stopping the drug [1][2][3]. Overall, the medicine helps some aspects of liver disease but is not a cure, and its benefits must be weighed against significant side effects.

- **Full Answer**:  
For primary biliary cirrhosis, cyclosporine can improve liver enzyme tests and slow worsening of liver damage on biopsy, especially in earlier stages of disease [2][3]. Patients taking cyclosporine often report more stable or improved fatigue and itching than those on placebo [3]. In a large 6‑year study, time to death or liver transplant was prolonged by up to about 50% in statistical models, and liver‑related deaths were lower, although simpler survival comparisons did not show a clear difference [1]. Despite these benefits, cyclosporine frequently causes kidney problems and high blood pressure, sometimes requiring dose reduction or stopping the drug [1][2][3]. Because of these safety concerns and the lack of definite proof that it extends life, cyclosporine is considered to have limited but real effectiveness, not a cure, and must be used with very close medical monitoring [1][2][3].

- **Rationale**:  
The articles all study cyclosporine for primary biliary cirrhosis, a chronic liver disease. They show some slowing of disease progression and symptom relief, but no clear, consistent survival benefit and many side effects. So, medicine here is not useless, but only moderately effective and limited by toxicity.

- **Full Answer**:  
Based on these studies in primary biliary cirrhosis, medicine is partly effective but far from a cure. Cyclosporine can slow worsening of liver blood tests and symptoms such as fatigue and itching compared with placebo [2][3]. In early-stage disease, it greatly reduced microscopic progression of liver damage over two years (1 of 13 on cyclosporine vs 5 of 7 on placebo) [3]. In a large 6‑year trial, statistical modeling suggested up to a 50% longer time before death or liver transplant and lower liver‑related deaths, although simple survival comparisons did not clearly show a benefit [1]. However, key markers of liver function and survival often changed little, and all trials showed frequent kidney damage, high blood pressure, and other side effects, sometimes forcing permanent drug withdrawal [1][2][3]. Because benefits are modest and risks are significant, current medicines for this liver disease are only moderately effective overall [1][2][3].

- **Rationale**:  
The articles all study one medicine, cyclosporine, in a specific autoimmune liver disease called primary biliary cirrhosis (PBC). Across several trials, cyclosporine improved blood tests of liver damage, symptoms like itching and tiredness, and in one large study may have delayed death or need for transplant. However, benefits were modest and not always clearly seen in simple survival comparisons. All studies reported important side effects, especially kidney damage and high blood pressure. Overall, medicine can help in this particular liver disease, but it is not a cure and carries significant risks, so careful monitoring is needed and more research is warranted.

- **Full Answer**:  
For the specific autoimmune liver disease primary biliary cirrhosis (PBC), the medicine cyclosporine shows some benefit but is not a cure.

In a large 6‑year trial, cyclosporine slowed worsening of liver tests and, by more complex analysis, appeared to prolong time before death or liver transplant, though simple survival comparisons were not clearly better [1].  

Smaller studies found cyclosporine improved abnormal liver enzymes, itching, fatigue, and slowed scarring on liver biopsy in some patients [2][3].  

However, kidney damage (rising creatinine) and high blood pressure were common, sometimes forcing dose reduction or stopping the drug [1][2][3]. Many side effects improved after the medicine was stopped [2].  

So, medicine (cyclosporine) can help certain patients with PBC by improving tests and symptoms and possibly slowing disease, but its benefits are limited and balanced by significant risks, requiring close monitoring and specialist care [1][2][3].

- **Rationale**:  
The question is broad (“liver disease” in general), but the articles focus on one autoimmune liver condition: primary biliary cirrhosis (PBC). Across three controlled trials, cyclosporine A showed consistent biological and symptom improvements, and in one large trial it may have delayed death or need for transplant. However, important side effects (kidney injury, high blood pressure, other adverse effects) limit its use and mean it is not a cure. So the accurate lay answer is that medicines can help some liver diseases (including PBC), but they do not always cure them and must be closely monitored.

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicines can help some liver diseases, but they do not always cure them, and benefits must be balanced with side effects. In primary biliary cirrhosis, an autoimmune liver disease, cyclosporine A improved blood tests that reflect bile flow and liver inflammation, and slowed worsening of these tests compared with placebo [1][2][3]. In several trials, patients on cyclosporine had less fatigue and itch, and some had slower or less scarring seen on liver biopsies than those on placebo [2][3]. One large study suggested cyclosporine might lower liver-related deaths or need for transplant, although survival benefits were not clearly proven by all analyses [1]. However, cyclosporine frequently caused kidney problems and high blood pressure, and sometimes had to be stopped or reduced [1][2][3]. Overall, medicines can work to slow progression and improve symptoms in certain liver diseases, but they require careful monitoring and are not guaranteed cures for every patient or disease.

- **Rationale**:  
Across multiple randomized trials, diabetes education for people with type 2 diabetes consistently improves what patients know about their disease and how they care for themselves, and often improves blood sugar and heart‑risk factors. Both group and one‑to‑one education work similarly well, with group sessions sometimes giving slightly better blood sugar control and being more efficient. However, benefits can fade if education is short and not reinforced over time. Some counseling programs mainly improved attitudes and self‑care rather than lab results, suggesting that intensity, content, and follow‑up matter.

- **Full Answer**:  
Education for type 2 diabetes is generally effective. Structured education—whether group or individual—improves knowledge, self‑monitoring, diet skills, and other self‑care behaviors [1,3,7,8]. Many studies show meaningful drops in HbA1c (about 1–2 percentage points), weight/BMI, blood pressure, and cholesterol after education or counseling programs [1,2,3,4,6,8]. Group education works at least as well as one‑to‑one and may slightly improve blood sugar more, while using fewer resources [1,8]. Nurse‑led and culturally tailored programs also improve understanding and self‑management, though not always long‑term HbA1c [5,7,9]. Benefits tend to lessen once education stops, so ongoing reinforcement is important [4,6].

- **Rationale**:  
Across these randomized trials, diabetes education for type 2 diabetes usually improves what patients know and how they care for themselves. Many studies also show better blood sugar and some improvement in weight, blood pressure, or cholesterol. However, not all programs change clinical outcomes, and benefits can fade when education stops. Ongoing, structured, behavior‑focused education works better than brief or one‑off sessions. Some interventions mainly improve satisfaction or distress without extra blood sugar benefit over usual care.

- **Full Answer**:  
Diabetes education for type 2 diabetes is generally helpful rather than ineffective. Multiple randomized trials show that structured education, whether in groups or one‑to‑one, improves patients’ knowledge and self‑care behaviors [1][3][6][7][8]. Many programs also lead to meaningful short‑term drops in blood sugar (HbA1c reductions around 1–2.5 percentage points) and modest improvements in weight, blood pressure, or cholesterol [1][2][3][4][6][8]. However, benefits are not guaranteed. Some intensive counseling produced better understanding and behavior but no clear improvement in HbA1c, weight, or quality of life over one year [5], and gains in blood sugar control faded once education stopped [4]. Added “coaching” after education improved satisfaction and distress but not HbA1c beyond standard care [9]. Overall, education is moderately effective for managing type 2 diabetes, especially when structured, ongoing, and behavior‑focused; it is least effective when brief, one‑time, or not reinforced in routine care [4][5][9], and when healthcare teams support agreed action plans.

- **Rationale**:  
Across many randomized studies in adults with type 2 diabetes, structured education—whether one‑to‑one, in groups, or with nurse coaching—consistently improves what people know about diabetes and how they look after themselves. [1][3][7][8][9] In several trials, this better self‑care led to lower long‑term blood sugar (HbA1c), sometimes by 0.7–2 percentage points, and small improvements in weight, blood pressure, and cholesterol. [1][2][3][4][6][8] Benefits are seen in community settings, primary care, and hospital clinics, and in higher‑risk or underserved groups. [2][4][6][7][9] Group and individual formats usually work equally well, with group sessions sometimes slightly better or more efficient. [1][3][8] However, when education is brief and then stops, improvements in blood sugar can fade within about a year, showing that ongoing reinforcement is important. [4][5] Some lifestyle‑counseling programs mainly change attitudes and behaviors without clear lab changes over one year. [5] Overall, evidence supports diabetes education as an effective part of managing type 2 diabetes.

- **Full Answer**:  
Yes. Well‑designed education programs for people with type 2 diabetes clearly “work,” especially in the short to medium term.  

Randomized studies show that both group and individual education improve patients’ knowledge about diabetes and their self‑care behaviors, such as diet, glucose monitoring, foot care, and medication use. [1][3][7][8][9] Many trials also report better blood sugar control (lower HbA1c), often by about 0.7–2 percentage points, and modest improvements in weight, blood pressure, and cholesterol or other cardiovascular risk factors. [1][2][3][4][6][8] These benefits appear in community programs, primary care, and specialist clinics, and among higher‑risk or underserved groups. [2][4][6][7][9]  

Group and individual formats are generally equally effective, with group sessions sometimes giving slightly greater HbA1c reductions and being more efficient. [1][3][8] However, when education is given only for a short period and then stopped, improvements in blood sugar tend to fade within a year, so ongoing reinforcement is important. [4][5]

- **Rationale**:  
Across many randomized studies in people with type 2 diabetes, education consistently improves knowledge, self‑care behaviors, and often blood sugar and heart‑risk factors. Both group and one‑to‑one teaching lowered average blood sugar (HbA1c), weight/BMI, and blood pressure in several trials [1][2][3][6][8]. Pictorial and culturally tailored teaching improved knowledge and self‑monitoring and slightly improved HbA1c [7]. Nurse‑led programs sometimes show the strongest gains early, which can fade if education stops, suggesting benefits depend on ongoing reinforcement [4]. One trial of monthly nurse lifestyle counseling did not change HbA1c in one year but still improved patients’ thinking and behavior about diabetes [5]. Another nurse‑coaching program improved diet, distress, and satisfaction, even though A1c changes were similar to usual care [9]. Overall, education works, but it is not a cure and must be continuous and combined with medical treatment.

- **Full Answer**:  
Diabetes education does work for people with type 2 diabetes, but its effects and strength can vary.  

Group and individual education programs both improved diabetes knowledge and lowered average blood sugar (HbA1c), weight, cholesterol, and blood pressure, with no clear disadvantage for either format [1][3][8]. Community programs with individual counseling also improved HbA1c, cholesterol, and blood pressure [2]. Nurse‑led education for people on maximal tablets produced a large short‑term drop in HbA1c, but this benefit faded after the education stopped, showing the need for ongoing reinforcement [4]. Regular structured education in Chinese patients improved waist size, blood pressure, HbA1c, and cholesterol beyond usual care [6]. Pictorial, one‑to‑one teaching for British Asians improved knowledge, self‑testing, and modestly improved HbA1c [7].  

One lifestyle‑counseling study did not change HbA1c over a year but still improved patients’ diabetes‑related thinking and behaviors [5], and nurse‑coaching improved diet, distress, and satisfaction [9].  

Overall, education helps type 2 diabetes management, especially when continued over time and combined with good medical care.

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks how effective a supplement is for age‑related eye disease. The articles both study Ginkgo biloba extract (EGb 761) in people with “dry” senile/age‑related macular degeneration. One is a relatively larger, 6‑month trial (99 people) comparing two doses; the other is a very small (10 people) placebo‑controlled trial [1][2]. Both report statistically significant or clearly favorable changes in visual acuity with Ginkgo, with a dose–response trend in the larger study [1]. However, the total number of patients is small, follow‑up is short, and the research is older, so confidence in the size and durability of benefit is limited [1][2].  

- **Full Answer**:  
Two small clinical studies looked at Ginkgo biloba extract (EGb 761) for the “dry” form of age‑related macular degeneration, a common cause of vision loss in older adults. In a 99‑person, 6‑month trial, both a high dose (240 mg/day) and a low dose (60 mg/day) produced modest improvements in vision; on average, visual acuity in the worse eye improved slightly more with the higher dose (about 0.13 versus 0.10) [1]. Nearly twice as many people on the higher dose had a more noticeable gain in vision (an improvement of at least 0.2) [1]. A much smaller 10‑person trial also found statistically significant improvement in distance vision with Ginkgo compared with placebo [2]. Side effects were minimal in these studies [1]. Overall, Ginkgo extract may offer small vision benefits for dry age‑related macular degeneration, but evidence is limited to short‑term, older, small trials, so certainty is low for this supplement [1][2].

- **Rationale**:  
The question seems to ask whether the supplement (Ginkgo biloba extract) is *ineffective* for age‑related eye disease, specifically dry age‑related macular degeneration. Two small, older studies looked at Ginkgo biloba extract (EGb 761). In a 99‑person trial, people taking Ginkgo for 6 months had modest improvement in visual acuity (sharpness of sight) in the worse eye; the higher dose (240 mg/day) helped a bit more than the lower dose (60 mg/day), and many patients noticed everyday life benefits [1]. A second, much smaller trial with 10 patients found that Ginkgo worked better than placebo in improving distance vision [2]. However, these studies are small, short term, and do not show that Ginkgo prevents long‑term vision loss or blindness. So the supplement cannot be called clearly “ineffective,” but the evidence is limited and not strong enough to rely on it as a main treatment.

- **Full Answer**:  
Based on these studies, Ginkgo biloba extract is **not clearly ineffective**, but its benefits appear **modest and uncertain**.

In a 6‑month study of 99 people with dry age‑related macular degeneration, both low and high doses of Ginkgo biloba slightly improved vision sharpness in the more damaged eye; the higher dose helped a bit more, and nearly twice as many people on the higher dose had a meaningful vision gain (though this difference was only borderline statistically significant) [1]. A tiny 10‑person study also found better distance vision with Ginkgo compared with placebo [2].

However, these trials are small, short, and do not prove that Ginkgo prevents serious vision loss or blindness over time [1][2]. They also do not compare Ginkgo with today’s standard eye‑health vitamin formulas.

Overall, Ginkgo may offer **small short‑term improvements**, but evidence is too limited to rely on it as a primary treatment for age‑related macular degeneration [1][2].

- **Rationale**:  
Both articles studied a Ginkgo biloba extract (EGb 761) in people with “senile” or age‑related dry macular degeneration, a common cause of vision loss in older adults. In a 99‑person, 6‑month trial, both low and high doses led to modest average improvements in visual sharpness, with slightly better gains at the higher dose and good tolerability [1]. A much smaller 10‑person placebo‑controlled study also found a statistically significant improvement in distance vision with Ginkgo extract [2]. However, the total number of patients is small, follow‑up is short, and the studies are old. They suggest possible benefit but are not strong enough to prove that Ginkgo reliably prevents or slows age‑related macular degeneration, nor do they address other age‑related eye diseases.

- **Full Answer**:  
Ginkgo biloba extract showed modest short‑term improvement in vision in small studies of age‑related dry macular degeneration, but evidence is limited and not definitive [1][2].

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks if a supplement “does not work” for age-related eye disease. The articles both study a Ginkgo biloba extract (EGb 761) in people with the “dry” form of age-related macular degeneration. In one larger, 6‑month double‑blind trial, both low and high doses improved visual acuity, with more benefit at the higher dose [1]. A smaller placebo‑controlled study also found better distance vision with Ginkgo than with placebo [2]. However, the studies are relatively small, effects are modest, and they address only dry (not wet) macular degeneration. So we can say the supplement is not useless, but evidence is limited and it should not replace standard eye care.

- **Full Answer**:  
Research on a specific Ginkgo biloba supplement (EGb 761) suggests it may help, at least modestly, for the “dry” form of age‑related macular degeneration, an eye disease that occurs with aging. In a six‑month, double‑blind study of 99 people, both high and low doses of EGb 761 improved visual acuity; the higher dose showed somewhat larger gains and more people had meaningful vision improvement [1]. Another small, placebo‑controlled study of 10 patients also found a statistically significant improvement in distance vision with Ginkgo extract compared with placebo [2]. These studies indicate that this supplement is not completely ineffective, but the benefits were moderate, and the total number of people studied was small [1][2]. The research does not prove it prevents vision loss or works for the “wet” form of macular degeneration. It should not replace regular eye care; discuss any supplement use with your eye specialist to ensure safe use.

- **Rationale**:  
The question is about how well medicines work to reduce self-injury, mostly in people with severe intellectual disability or autism. One small, controlled study of the antidepressant clomipramine showed a large drop in self-injury in most participants, but also some serious side effects. Several small studies of the opioid blocker naltrexone found that some patients had clear reductions in self-injury. However, a larger, better-controlled trial in 33 adults found no benefit and even some worsening of other repetitive behaviors. Overall, these medicines can help some individuals but do not work reliably for everyone and can cause side effects. They should be used cautiously, under specialist care, and as part of a broader treatment plan that includes behavioral approaches.

- **Full Answer**:  
In people with severe intellectual disability, one small trial of the antidepressant clomipramine found that 6 of 8 patients (75%) had their self-injury cut by at least half, but 2 had serious side effects like seizure and agitation [1].  

Several small studies of the opioid blocker naltrexone showed fewer and less severe episodes of head-banging and self-biting in many participants [2–4]. For example, all 4 patients in one trial reduced self-injury, with larger doses often helping more [3], and 3 of 4 adults in another study had meaningful reductions [4].  

However, a larger study in 33 adults found that naltrexone did not improve self-injury or autism symptoms and actually increased other repetitive behaviors; staff rated patients as worse on naltrexone than on placebo [5].  

Overall, medicines can sometimes reduce self-injury but are not reliably effective and can cause important side effects [1–5].

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks how *ineffective* medicines are for self-harm (self‑injurious behavior) based on the provided studies. The evidence is mixed and depends on the specific drug and study size. Small trials of clomipramine and naltrexone show meaningful benefit for some individuals, but a larger, better‑powered naltrexone study found no overall benefit and even some worsening. This suggests medicines are not reliably effective across broad groups, may help a subset of patients, and can cause side effects. I synthesized these findings into clear, lay language within 150 words.

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicines to reduce self‑injurious behavior (hurting oneself on purpose) help some people but are far from reliably effective.  

In a small study of 8 people with severe intellectual disability, the antidepressant clomipramine led to a large (50% or more) drop in self‑injury in 6 people, but 2 had serious side effects like seizure and heart‑related agitation [1]. Several small studies of the opioid‑blocking drug naltrexone in a few adults found noticeable reductions in self‑injury for most participants [2][3][4].  

However, a much larger, better‑controlled study in 33 adults with intellectual disability and autism found that naltrexone did not improve self‑injury or autism symptoms; in some people it actually worsened repetitive behaviors, and staff rated 50 mg per day as worse than placebo overall [5].  

Overall, medicines may substantially help a minority of carefully selected individuals, but they can also cause side effects and are not broadly effective for self‑injury in general.

- **Rationale**:  
The articles describe medicines tested in people with severe intellectual (learning) disabilities who repeatedly harm themselves. One antidepressant (clomipramine) clearly reduced self‑injury for most participants in a small trial, but caused serious side effects in some. Several small studies of the opioid‑blocking drug naltrexone showed reduced self‑injury, yet a larger, better‑controlled study found no benefit and some worsening of behavior, with staff rating it worse than placebo. Overall, results are mixed: some individuals improve, others do not, and side effects can be significant. The evidence is limited to specific groups and does not cover all types of self‑harm (such as self‑harm linked to depression or emotional distress in the general population). Medicines should therefore be considered only as one part of a broader treatment plan, combined with behavioral therapies, safety planning, and support, and always prescribed and monitored by a qualified clinician.

- **Full Answer**:  
Research has mostly looked at medicines for people with severe learning disabilities who repeatedly hurt themselves (self‑injurious behavior), not for all kinds of self‑harm. An antidepressant called clomipramine reduced self‑injury by at least half in 6 of 8 people in one small trial, but two had serious side effects like seizure and heart rhythm problems [1]. An opioid‑blocking drug, naltrexone, reduced self‑injury in several very small studies [2–4], but another, larger study in 33 people found no benefit and even some worsening of repetitive behaviors, with staff rating naltrexone as worse than placebo [5]. Overall, medicines can sometimes reduce self‑injury in specific groups, but results are mixed and side effects can be significant [1–5]. They are not a cure and should only be used under close medical supervision, together with behavioral treatments, safety planning, and support to address emotional, medical, or environmental causes of self‑harm and other mental health issues.

- **Rationale**:  
Research has mainly looked at medicines for people who hurt themselves repeatedly (self‑injurious behavior) and who also have intellectual disability or autism. Some small studies show benefit from certain medicines, but another larger study does not. This means medicines are not useless, but they are also not a sure fix, and they can cause side effects. Treatment usually needs to combine behavior strategies, environmental support, and sometimes medication, under close medical supervision.

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicines can sometimes help reduce self‑injuring behavior, but they do not work for everyone and are not a guaranteed solution.

In one small study, most people taking the antidepressant clomipramine had at least a 50% drop in how often they hurt themselves, though some had serious side effects like seizures and heart problems [1].  

Several small studies of the opioid blocker naltrexone reported clear decreases in self‑injury for many patients [2][3][4].  

However, a larger, well‑controlled study in 33 adults found that naltrexone did **not** improve self‑injury or autism symptoms and in some cases made stereotyped behaviors worse [5].  

Overall, medicines may help some individuals but not others, and they can sometimes worsen behavior. Any use of medication for self‑harm should be carefully managed by a specialist and combined with behavioral and environmental supports.

- **Rationale**:  
Two good-quality studies tested sildenafil (Viagra) for erection problems in men with multiple sclerosis (MS). They reached different conclusions: one saw only small benefits over placebo, the other found large improvements in erections and quality of life. Together, they suggest sildenafil can help many men with MS, but it does not work for everyone and side effects occur.

- **Full Answer**:  
Research on sildenafil (Viagra) for erection problems in men with MS shows mixed but generally positive results.  

In one trial of 203 men, sildenafil led to only modest improvements: about 33% reported better erections versus 18% on placebo, and successful intercourse attempts rose only slightly; side effects were more common, so the authors did *not* recommend routine use [1].  

In another trial of 217 men, about 89–95% of those taking sildenafil reported improved erections versus 24–26% on placebo, with clear gains in ability to get and keep erections and better quality of life; side effects were mostly mild [2].  

Overall, sildenafil can be effective for many men with MS-related erection problems, but response varies and medical review is essential.

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks how ineffective medicine is for sexual problems in multiple sclerosis (MS), using two sildenafil (Viagra) trials. One study found only a small advantage over placebo, while another found a very large benefit. I compare the percentage of men who improved in each group to show how often the drug helps versus a dummy pill, and explain that results are inconsistent rather than clearly “ineffective,” in plain, non-technical language.

- **Full Answer**:  
Research in men with multiple sclerosis (MS) and erection problems shows mixed results.  

In one trial, about 33% of men taking sildenafil (Viagra) reported better erections, compared with 18% on placebo. This means only around 1 extra man in 6 clearly benefited from the drug, and the authors judged the overall effect “little” and not strong enough for routine use [1].  

However, another similar trial found much larger benefits: 89% of men on sildenafil reported improved erections versus 24% on placebo; quality of life scores also improved far more with the drug [2].  

Side effects were more common than with placebo (about 24% versus 9% in one study), but were usually mild [1][2].  

So, medicines like sildenafil are not useless for MS‑related sexual problems, but they do not work for everyone. Some trials suggest only modest benefit, while others show substantial improvement [1][2]. Discuss options with a neurologist or urologist.

- **Rationale**:  
Two good-quality studies tested sildenafil (Viagra) for erection problems in men with multiple sclerosis (MS). One found only small benefits over placebo and more side effects, and concluded it should not be used routinely [1]. The other, larger study found clearly better erections and quality of life with sildenafil than with placebo, with mostly mild side effects [2]. Both studies looked only at erection problems in men, not other sexual issues or women with MS. So, medicine can help many men with MS-related erection problems, but results may vary, and it does not fix all sexual difficulties.

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicines like sildenafil (Viagra) can help some men with MS-related erection problems, but results are mixed.  

In one study, improved erections were seen in 33% on sildenafil vs 18% on placebo, a small advantage, and side effects were more common (24% vs 9%) [1].  

In another, larger study, 89% of men on sildenafil reported better erections compared with 24% on placebo, and overall quality of life improved much more (43% vs 13%) [2]. Side effects were generally mild, and no one stopped treatment because of them [2].  

These medicines mainly help with getting and keeping an erection; they may not fully address other MS-related sexual problems like low desire, spasticity, or fatigue.  

Talk with a doctor familiar with MS to see if an erectile medicine is appropriate and safe in your case.

- **Rationale**:  
The question is whether medicines “don’t work” for sexual (erectile) problems in multiple sclerosis (MS). The articles both study sildenafil (Viagra). One trial shows only small benefits over placebo and more side effects, leading the authors to say it should not be used routinely. Another, similarly designed trial finds very strong benefits for erections and quality of life, with mostly mild side effects. Together, this means the medicine can help many men with MS, but not all, and responses are variable, likely because MS affects nerves in complex ways. So the fair answer is that medicine can work, but it is not guaranteed and needs individual trial and medical supervision.

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicines for sexual problems in multiple sclerosis (MS), especially sildenafil (Viagra), can help many men, but not everyone.

One study in 203 men with MS found only small improvements in erections and successful intercourse with sildenafil compared with placebo, and more side effects; the authors concluded it should not be routinely recommended [1]. However, a second, similar trial in 217 men showed much larger benefits: 89–95% reported better erections versus 24–26% on placebo, with quality of life improving by 43% versus 13% and mostly mild side effects [2].

These mixed results suggest that MS-related nerve damage may make erectile problems harder to treat and that individual response varies [1][2].

So, medicine does work for sexual (erection) problems in many men with MS, but it is not guaranteed. A trial under medical supervision is reasonable, and other treatments or counseling may still be needed [1][2].

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks about “cancer treatment in the womb” for advanced “womb cancer.” The articles describe intracavitary brachytherapy—radiation given through applicators placed in the uterus/cervix—for invasive cervical cancer (often called “womb cancer” in lay language). All trials include many women with Stage II–III (advanced) disease and report survival, pelvic (local) control, and complications, comparing low‑dose‑rate (LDR) and high‑dose‑rate (HDR) brachytherapy. Across studies, LDR and HDR give very similar results in survival and local control, with generally low rates of serious bowel/bladder damage. However, many failures in advanced disease are due to cancer spreading outside the pelvis, and one study notes that adding systemic chemotherapy could reduce these distant recurrences, which reflects current standard practice. I therefore summarize effectiveness as good pelvic control and moderate long‑term survival, but not a guaranteed cure, especially once disease is advanced.

- **Full Answer**:  
Internal radiotherapy given through the womb (intracavitary brachytherapy), together with external beam radiotherapy, can control many cases of advanced cervical (“womb”) cancer but is not a guaranteed cure.

Across several large trials in women with Stage II–III disease, about 50–70% were alive 3–5 years after treatment, depending on stage and study [1–4]. Control of the tumour in the pelvis was better: roughly 75–90% had no return of cancer in the treated area [1,2,4]. Outcomes were similar whether a low‑dose‑rate or high‑dose‑rate radiation schedule was used [1–4].

Serious long‑term bowel or bladder side effects were uncommon, generally affecting only a few percent of patients, although milder rectal problems were more frequent with some regimens [1–4].

Many failures in advanced stages were due to spread of cancer to distant sites, suggesting that adding systemic treatments like chemotherapy (which is standard in modern care) may further improve results for certain high‑risk patients [4].

- **Rationale**:  
The articles describe women with moderately to very advanced cancer of the cervix (neck of the womb), treated mainly with internal radiotherapy (brachytherapy) plus external beam radiotherapy. For stages II–III, 5‑year survival is typically around 50–75%, meaning 25–50% of women still die from their cancer within five years despite treatment [1–3]. Local (pelvic) control is better—about 70–90%—so most failures in advanced stages are due to spread elsewhere in the body (distant metastases), not regrowth in the womb area [1,2,4]. This shows treatment is quite effective at controlling the tumor in the pelvis but only partly effective at preventing death and distant spread in advanced disease.

- **Full Answer**:  
For advanced “womb” (cervical) cancer, internal radiotherapy plus external radiotherapy is only partly effective.

Across several trials, 5‑year survival for stage II–III disease is typically 50–75%, so roughly 1 in 4 to 1 in 2 women still die of their cancer within five years despite treatment [1–3].  

Control of the tumor in the pelvis is better: about 70–90% have no return of cancer in the womb/pelvic area (pelvic control), meaning local failure occurs in roughly 10–30% [1,2,4].  

The main problem in advanced stages is cancer spreading elsewhere in the body. In one study, most recurrences in stage IIB–IIIB patients were distant metastases, not regrowth in the pelvis [4].  

So, treatment in the womb is reasonably good at local control but remains quite limited in preventing death and distant spread for advanced cancer.

- **Rationale**:  
The articles all study women with locally advanced cervical cancer (cancer of the neck of the womb) treated by placing radiation sources inside the womb/cervix (intracavitary brachytherapy), usually combined with external beam radiation [1–4]. Across several large randomized trials, both low‑dose‑rate and high‑dose‑rate brachytherapy gave similar results, with roughly half to two‑thirds of women alive at 5 years and good control of the tumor in the pelvis [1–3]. Pelvic control rates were high, but many recurrences were due to cancer spreading to distant organs, especially in more advanced stages, suggesting that adding whole‑body treatments like chemotherapy may improve outcomes [4]. Moderate to severe side effects involving nearby organs (rectum, bladder) occurred in a minority of patients [1–4]. Overall, these data show that “radiation treatment in the womb” is an important and effective part of treatment for advanced cervical (womb‑neck) cancer, though it does not cure everyone.

- **Full Answer**:  
Treatment that puts radiation directly inside the womb and cervix (intracavitary brachytherapy) does help many people with locally advanced cervical (womb‑neck) cancer, but it is not a guaranteed cure.

Across several large trials of stage II–III disease, combining external beam radiation with brachytherapy gave 5‑year survival around 50–70% [1–3] and local (pelvic) tumor control in roughly 75–90% of patients [1,2,4]. High‑dose‑rate and low‑dose‑rate brachytherapy worked about equally well at controlling the cancer and achieving long‑term survival [1–4].

However, a substantial number of patients still developed cancer spread to distant organs, especially in more advanced stages [4]. Because of this, researchers suggest adding treatments that work throughout the body, such as chemotherapy, to improve results in advanced cases [4].

Radiation inside the womb and cervix can also cause side effects in nearby organs (rectum, bladder), with moderate to severe complications in about 3–10% of patients, depending on dose and technique [1–4].

- **Rationale**:  
The articles describe “intracavitary brachytherapy” – a form of radiotherapy given inside the womb/cervix area – for cervical cancer. They include many patients with more advanced disease (Stage II and III). Across several large trials, this treatment, combined with external beam radiotherapy, controlled the tumor in the pelvis for most women and kept around half of women with Stage III disease alive at 5 years. However, cure rates are clearly lower in more advanced stages, and many failures are due to cancer spreading elsewhere in the body, which local womb treatment alone cannot fully prevent.  

- **Full Answer**:  
Treatment “inside the womb” (intracavitary brachytherapy) for cervical cancer does work, even in more advanced stages, but it does not cure everyone.  

In several large studies of Stage II–III cervical cancer, about half of women with Stage III disease were alive 5 years after treatment with brachytherapy plus external radiotherapy [1–3]. Pelvic tumor control rates were high, around 70–90% depending on stage and technique [1–4].  

The main problem in advanced stages is not that the womb treatment fails locally, but that the cancer is more likely to have spread elsewhere; many recurrences are distant metastases, especially in Stage IIB–IIIB [4]. Because of this, experts suggest adding treatments like chemotherapy to improve outcomes in advanced disease [4].  

So, cancer treatment in the womb can still be effective for advanced cervical cancer, but the chances of cure are lower than in earlier stages, and additional treatments are often needed [1–4].

- **Rationale**:  
Research on newborn screening for cystic fibrosis, a genetic disease that damages lungs and digestion, shows timing benefits. In Wales and the West Midlands, screening using a blood test found affected babies much earlier than waiting for symptoms. Screened babies spent less time in hospital in the first year of life, but through age 4 their growth and lung measures were similar to children diagnosed later. Some infants were missed by screening, showing the test is not perfect.

- **Full Answer**:  
Newborn screening for cystic fibrosis (a major cause of lifelong lung and digestive problems) is moderately effective, with clear but limited proven benefits. In a large regional program, screening with a blood test led to diagnosis at a much younger age than waiting for symptoms to appear [1]. Babies identified by screening spent significantly less time in hospital during their first year of life, suggesting fewer or milder early problems [1]. However, up to age 4, there were no clear differences in growth, lung health, or other clinical measures between screened children and those diagnosed later based on symptoms [1]. The test also missed some babies who were later found to have cystic fibrosis, so it is helpful but not completely reliable [1].

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks how ineffective birth screening is for cystic fibrosis (a condition causing lung and digestion problems from birth). The main evidence comes from a study of a regional newborn screening program using a blood test (immunoreactive trypsin). I focus on how many cases were missed and whether early detection improved health, as both indicate effectiveness.

- **Full Answer**:  
Newborn screening for cystic fibrosis looks for a blood marker (immunoreactive trypsin) shortly after birth. In the five‑year program from Wales and the West Midlands, screening clearly was not perfect: nine children who actually had cystic fibrosis had a negative newborn screen and were only diagnosed later when they developed symptoms [1]. Among all children eventually known to have cystic fibrosis in that study, this represents a meaningful minority, showing that screening can miss babies with the disease [1]. For those who were picked up by screening, diagnosis happened earlier and they spent less time in hospital in the first year of life, which is an important benefit [1]. However, by around age four, their growth, lung findings, and blood tests were similar to children who were diagnosed later based on symptoms alone [1]. This suggests that, at least in that setting, early screening gave limited long‑term health advantage [1].

- **Rationale**:  
The main study looks at a newborn blood test for cystic fibrosis (CF), a condition that causes lung infections and digestive problems. It compares babies found by screening with those diagnosed later when they develop symptoms. Screening clearly leads to earlier diagnosis and fewer days in hospital in the first year of life. However, by age 4, there weren’t clear differences in lung health or growth. Another article explains that CF severity varies by gene changes, which also affects long‑term lung outcomes, independent of screening. So screening helps mainly by finding the disease early and reducing early illness burden.

- **Full Answer**:  
Newborn screening for cystic fibrosis (CF) looks for high levels of immunoreactive trypsin in a heel‑prick blood sample. In a large study from Wales and the West Midlands, this screening found babies with CF much earlier than waiting for symptoms such as persistent cough, poor weight gain, or bowel blockage to appear [1]. Children found by screening spent significantly less time in hospital in their first year of life (after excluding time for diagnostic tests) than those diagnosed later because of symptoms [1]. However, up to age 4 years, lung health, growth, and blood tests were similar in screened and unscreened children, so clear long‑term health advantages were not yet shown [1]. CF itself has many genetic forms that influence how severe lung disease becomes, which also affects outcomes beyond screening alone [2]. Overall, birth screening “works” mainly by giving an earlier diagnosis and reducing early hospital stays for families.

- **Rationale**:  
The key lung and digestion disease present from birth in these articles is cystic fibrosis (CF). A large study of newborn screening in Wales and the West Midlands used a blood test (immunoreactive trypsin) to find babies with CF soon after birth [1]. Screening found babies much earlier than waiting for symptoms and led to fewer days in hospital in the first year of life [1]. However, up to age 4, growth, lung, and lab measures were similar between screened children and those diagnosed later by symptoms [1]. Some babies with CF were missed by screening and diagnosed only after they became sick, showing that screening is not perfect [1]. Another study shows that lung severity in CF varies by gene type and cannot be fully predicted from early clinical tests alone [2].  

- **Full Answer**:  
Newborn screening for cystic fibrosis does “work” in several important ways, but it has limits.

In the Welsh/West Midlands program, screening identified most affected babies much earlier than waiting for lung or digestion symptoms to appear [1]. Those detected by screening spent less time in hospital during their first year of life, even after excluding hospital stays just for testing [1]. This suggests that early diagnosis and treatment can reduce early complications.

However, screening did not clearly improve growth, lung status, or other clinical measures compared with children diagnosed later, at least up to age 4 [1]. Also, some children with cystic fibrosis had negative newborn screens and were only diagnosed when symptoms developed, so the test can miss cases [1].

Lung disease severity in cystic fibrosis also depends on the specific gene mutations, and this cannot be fully predicted from early tests alone [2].

So, newborn screening is helpful but not perfect for this lung and digestion problem.

- **Rationale**:  
The question is about how effective it is when a specialist (usually a psychiatrist) writes a letter to the family doctor with advice on managing patients who have ongoing physical symptoms that don’t have a clear medical cause (often called “somatizing” or medically unexplained symptoms). Several randomized trials tested exactly this. They looked at whether such letters changed patients’ symptoms, functioning, and health‑care use and costs. Overall, these letters mainly helped doctors organize care better and reduced unnecessary tests and hospital stays, sometimes improving physical functioning a bit, but they did not dramatically reduce the symptoms themselves.

- **Full Answer**:  
Specialist letters to the family doctor that give concrete advice on managing “medically unexplained” body symptoms have modest but real benefits.  

In patients with several unexplained symptoms, a single psychiatric consultation plus a management letter led to better physical functioning that stayed improved for at least a year, and cut annual medical costs by about one‑third, mainly by avoiding hospital care [4]. In people meeting full criteria for somatization disorder, a similar letter reduced health‑care costs by 12–50% over a year, again largely from fewer hospitalizations, without harming physical or mental health or satisfaction [3,5]. However, these letters did not clearly reduce symptom distress itself [3,5].  

In a broader group of distressed, high‑use patients, psychiatric consultations changed doctors’ prescribing (more antidepressants) but did not improve symptoms, functioning, or service use [6].  

Overall, letters help doctors manage care more efficiently and may slightly improve functioning, but they are not a cure.

- **Rationale**:  
The articles describe “letters from doctors” as psychiatric consultation letters sent to primary‑care doctors caring for people with many physical symptoms but no clear medical cause. These letters give structured advice on how to manage such patients. Across studies, the letters reliably reduced medical costs and sometimes improved physical functioning, but had limited impact on the symptoms themselves or on emotional distress. In contrast, more intensive, face‑to‑face collaborative care produced larger improvements in symptoms and functioning. So, the letters are not useless, but they are a modest, mainly cost‑saving intervention rather than a cure.

- **Full Answer**:  
Psychiatric consultation letters to primary‑care doctors for patients with multiple unexplained physical symptoms are **modestly effective**, not completely ineffective.  

In classic somatization disorder, such consultation reduced quarterly or annual health‑care costs by about 12–53%, mainly by cutting hospitalizations, without worsening physical or mental health, though symptoms themselves did not clearly improve [3][5].  

In patients with several unexplained symptoms (but not full disorder), a standardized consultation letter improved physical functioning and reduced annual medical costs by about one‑third [4].  

However, in distressed high‑utilizing patients more generally, consultation increased antidepressant use but did **not** improve distress, functioning, or overall service use [6].  

More intensive collaborative care with in‑person psychiatric involvement produced larger gains, cutting symptom severity by 58% and improving social functioning while reducing health‑care use [1].  

So, letters alone offer small-to-moderate benefits (mainly cost savings and sometimes better functioning), but they are not a strong treatment for the symptoms themselves.

- **Rationale**:  
Research mainly looks at “somatization” or “medically unexplained symptoms” – physical complaints without a clear medical cause. When psychiatrists give primary care doctors a structured consultation letter with concrete management advice, two consistent effects are seen. Several studies show lower medical costs, mostly from fewer tests and hospital stays, without harming patients’ health or satisfaction [3][5]. One trial in patients with multiple unexplained symptoms also found better physical functioning that stayed improved over a year, alongside about a one‑third drop in yearly medical costs [4]. Another, more intensive collaborative model where a psychiatrist saw patients together with the GP led to a 58% reduction in symptom severity, better social functioning, and less health care use [1]. However, not every trial found improvements in distress or disability [3][6]. Overall, letters help doctors manage more efficiently and can modestly improve functioning, but they are not a cure‑all.

- **Full Answer**:  
Yes—under certain conditions, letters from specialists to family doctors can help with body symptoms that have no clear medical cause.  

When psychiatrists assessed these patients and sent the primary doctor a structured letter with specific management tips, patients’ overall medical costs fell by about 12–50%, mainly because there were fewer hospitalizations and tests, and health status did not worsen [3][5].  

In one study of people with several unexplained symptoms, this kind of consultation letter both improved patients’ physical functioning and cut yearly medical costs by about one‑third [4].  

A more intensive approach, where the psychiatrist met patients together with the GP, led to a 58% drop in main symptom severity, better social functioning, and lower health care use [1].  

However, other trials found no clear change in distress or disability, even though prescribing of antidepressants increased [6].  

So, these letters can improve care efficiency and sometimes functioning, but they are not a guaranteed fix for all symptoms.

- **Rationale**:  
Studies looked at people with many physical symptoms that doctors couldn’t explain medically (often called somatization or medically unexplained symptoms). In several trials, psychiatrists sent a structured consultation letter to the patient’s primary‑care doctor, giving clear management advice. These letters did not dramatically cure symptoms, but they often improved physical functioning and reduced health‑care costs [3–5]. One study in a broader group of distressed, high‑use patients showed more antidepressant prescribing but no clear improvement in distress, function, or service use [6]. Overall, letters help doctors manage care more effectively, but are not a complete solution.

- **Full Answer**:  
Letters from psychiatrists to primary‑care doctors about how to manage patients with physical symptoms that lack a clear medical cause do show benefits, but they are not a cure‑all. In somatizing patients, a standardized consultation letter with management advice led to better physical functioning that stayed improved for at least a year, and cut medical costs by about one‑third [4]. In another study of patients with a more strictly defined somatization disorder, a similar letter reduced health‑care costs by about 12% without worsening physical or mental health [5]. Earlier work with in‑person psychiatric consultation (not just letters) also showed large drops in medical costs, again without major changes in symptoms [3]. However, in distressed “high‑utilizer” patients more broadly, psychiatric consultation did not clearly improve distress, functioning, or use of services [6]. Overall, letters can help doctors manage these symptoms more efficiently, but usually don’t make them disappear for most people.

- **Rationale**:  
The studies look at people with moderate to severe piles (grades III–IV) who had surgery to remove them. Across trials, surgery reliably removed the hemorrhoids and gave good long‑term symptom control, with no or very low recurrence in follow‑up up to three years [3][10][11]. The main downside is short‑term pain after the operation, which can be quite significant with standard techniques [2][6][8]. A newer method using a device called LigaSure removes the piles just as completely but usually causes less early pain, needs fewer painkillers, involves less bleeding, and lets people heal and return to normal activities sooner [1][2][3][5][6][7][12][14]. Overall complication rates and long‑term bowel control (continence) appear similar between techniques, and serious problems are uncommon [1][2][4][6][7][8][9][10][11][12][13][15].

- **Full Answer**:  
Surgery for painful, advanced piles is highly effective at removing hemorrhoids and giving lasting relief, with low recurrence over months to years [3][10][11].  

Traditional operations work well but usually cause substantial pain for at least several days, and recovery can take a couple of weeks [2][6][8].  

Techniques using a sealing device called LigaSure remove the piles just as completely, but:  

- Shorten the operation and reduce bleeding [1][5][7][12][13][15].  
- Lower pain scores and the need for painkillers in the first days after surgery [1][2][5][6][7][12][14].  
- Speed wound healing and return to work or normal activities [2][3][6][7][12][14].  

Overall complication rates and long‑term bowel control are similar between LigaSure and standard surgery, and serious complications are uncommon [1][2][4][6][7][8][9][10][11][12][13][15].

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks “how ineffective” surgery is for painful piles. The randomized trials provided actually show that excisional surgery (including newer LigaSure methods) works well for severe haemorrhoids, with low recurrence, high satisfaction, and acceptable complication rates. The main limitation is short‑term pain, which is reduced (but not eliminated) by newer techniques. So the best lay answer is that surgery is generally effective, with relatively few failures.

- **Full Answer**:  
Surgery for painful piles (haemorrhoids) is generally effective rather than ineffective. In several randomized trials including hundreds of patients with severe (grade III–IV) haemorrhoids, excision surgery reliably removed the piles and controlled bleeding and prolapse [1–3,6,7,10–13]. Recurrence was rare in the short term; one study reported no recurrences at six months [3], and longer‑term follow‑up (about three years) showed good symptom control with either traditional or LigaSure techniques [10]. Around 80–85% of patients were satisfied with results at one year [9].  

The main drawback is short‑term pain. Many studies found that using newer LigaSure equipment reduces pain and pain‑killer needs in the first days to week after surgery compared with older methods [1,2,5–7,12,13,15], although a few trials showed similar pain levels [3,4,8,11,14]. Complication rates, continence problems, and long‑term outcomes were low and similar between techniques [1–4,6–11,13–15]. Overall, surgery is effective, with relatively few failures for most patients in practice.

- **Rationale**:  
Surgery for piles removes the swollen veins, so it often gives strong relief from pain, bleeding and prolapse when simpler treatments fail [3][8][12]. Trials show it works well long term, with high satisfaction and no extra risk of lasting leakage or poor bowel control compared with older methods [8][9][10]. The main trade‑off is recovery: most people have notable anal pain for days to weeks after surgery and need painkillers, especially when passing stool [2][3][6][7][8][13][15]. Newer energy devices such as LigaSure do the same job but usually make surgery quicker, with less bleeding, slightly less pain and faster healing and return to work than traditional techniques [1][2][3][5][6][7][11][12][13][14][15].

- **Full Answer**:  
Yes. For moderate to severe painful piles that keep coming back, excision surgery reliably removes the haemorrhoids and usually stops pain, bleeding and prolapse [3][8][12]. Expect a painful recovery period, but modern techniques like LigaSure can slightly reduce pain and speed healing overall [1][2][3][5][6][7][11][13][14][15].

- **Rationale**:  
The studies summarized all looked at people with moderate to severe hemorrhoids (grades III–IV), the kind that often cause significant pain, bleeding, or prolapse. Across many randomized trials, both traditional hemorrhoidectomy and newer tools like the LigaSure device removed the hemorrhoids effectively, with good symptom control and low recurrence over months to years. Pain after surgery is common, but newer techniques often reduce pain, need for painkillers, and recovery time. Complication rates and bowel control are generally similar between methods. This shows that surgery does work for painful piles, especially when simpler treatments have failed, but it is not pain‑free and is usually reserved for more severe disease.

- **Full Answer**:  
Surgery *does* work for painful piles, especially when they are large or keep prolapsing (grades III–IV). In multiple randomized trials, surgical removal of hemorrhoids gave good symptom control and very low early recurrence [1][3][6][10][11][13]. Longer‑term follow‑up (1–3 years) shows ongoing symptom relief and high patient satisfaction, with similar bowel control to standard surgery [9][10].  

All forms of hemorrhoidectomy cause short‑term pain, but newer techniques using the LigaSure device often reduce pain scores, painkiller use, and time off work compared with traditional methods [1][2][5][6][7][11][12][13]. Operating time and blood loss are also lower, and day‑case surgery is often possible [1][6][11][14].  

Complication rates (bleeding, wound problems, continence issues) are generally similar between techniques [1][2][4][6][7][8][9][10][12][13][14].  

So, surgery is an effective option for painful, severe piles, but it carries expected short‑term pain and recovery time.

- **Rationale**:  
When a periampullary cancer (near the pancreas and bile duct) cannot be fully removed, surgeons can still do “bypass” operations to prevent or relieve blockage of the stomach outlet and bile duct. Two randomized trials compared doing a double bypass (including a stomach‑to‑small‑intestine connection called a gastrojejunostomy) versus a single bypass (bile duct only). These studies show how well this surgery works for symptom control, complications, and survival.

- **Full Answer**:  
For periampullary cancers that cannot be completely removed, surgery is used to ease symptoms, not to cure the cancer or prolong life. Adding a prophylactic gastrojejunostomy (a surgical bypass from the stomach to the small intestine) during palliative surgery greatly reduces later stomach blockage: 0% vs 19% in one trial [1] and 5.5% vs 41.4% in another [2]. It also cuts the need for another operation; about 1 in 6 patients benefit by avoiding reoperation [2]. These extra bypass procedures do not increase death rates or overall complication rates, and hospital stay is similar [1][2]. Survival time is unchanged (around 7–8 months in both groups) [1][2], and quality of life returns to baseline within about 4 months, with no meaningful difference between surgical approaches [2].

- **Rationale**:  
In these studies, “unresectable” means the cancer cannot be fully removed, so surgery is done only to relieve blockages, not to cure the cancer. In both trials, adding a stomach bypass (gastrojejunostomy) did **not** lengthen life: average survival stayed around 7–8 months whether or not the extra bypass was done [1][2]. However, the extra bypass clearly reduced later stomach blockage. Without it, about 19–41% of patients developed gastric outlet obstruction and many needed another procedure [1][2]. With the bypass, this fell to 0–6%, and reoperations dropped from about 21% to 3% [1][2]. Complication rates, hospital stay, and overall quality of life were similar with or without the extra bypass [1][2].  

- **Full Answer**:  
For cancers that cannot be removed, surgery is largely **ineffective for cure or longer survival**—median survival stays about 7–8 months regardless of the operation [1][2].  

However, surgery can be **effective for symptom control**. Adding a bypass between the stomach and small intestine prevents future stomach blockage in most patients: obstruction falls from about 19–41% without the bypass to 0–6% with it, and the need for another operation drops from about 21% to 3% [1][2].  

Importantly, this added bypass does **not** significantly increase surgical complications, hospital stay, or worsen overall quality of life; quality of life usually returns to baseline within four months after surgery [1][2].  

So, while surgery for unresectable cancer is **ineffective as a cure**, it can be **quite effective** at preventing distressing blockages and further operations.

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks whether surgery can still “work” when the cancer itself cannot be completely removed. The articles describe patients whose periampullary cancers were unresectable at surgery, yet they still had operations to bypass blockages. These studies looked at whether such palliative surgeries prevent later complications, affect survival, and change quality of life. I use these data to explain that surgery can help with symptoms and complications even when it cannot cure the cancer, and clarify what “work” means in this context.

- **Full Answer**:  
Even when a cancer can’t be fully removed, surgery can still “work” by easing problems the tumor causes, but it usually does not prolong life or cure the disease. In people with periampullary cancers (around the pancreas and bile duct) that turned out to be unresectable during surgery, doctors often create bypasses so food and bile can flow around the blocked area. One trial showed that adding a preventive stomach‑to‑small‑intestine bypass (gastrojejunostomy) at the time of palliative surgery completely prevented later gastric outlet obstruction, while 19% without it later developed blockage needing another procedure [1]. A larger multicenter trial found similar results: only 5.5% with the double bypass developed obstruction versus 41.4% with biliary bypass alone, and reoperations were much less common [2]. Importantly, complication rates, hospital stay, survival (about 7–8 months), and quality of life were similar with or without the extra bypass for most of these patients [1,2].

- **Rationale**:  
The studies looked at people whose periampullary cancers were found during surgery to be “unresectable,” meaning the tumor could not be safely or completely removed. Surgeons then used operations not to cure the cancer, but to bypass blocked areas of the digestive tract so patients could eat and avoid distressing complications.

- **Full Answer**:  
Even when a cancer cannot be removed, surgery can still “work” in an important way. In these studies, patients with unresectable periampullary cancer had bypass operations to prevent or treat blockage of the stomach outlet (gastric outlet obstruction). Doing an extra bypass connection between the stomach and small intestine (a gastrojejunostomy) at the time of surgery greatly reduced later blockage and the need for another operation, without increasing complications or hospital stay [1][2]. However, this kind of surgery did not lengthen survival; people lived about the same amount of time whether or not they had the extra bypass [1][2]. So, for cancers that can’t be removed, surgery may not cure the cancer but can significantly improve comfort and avoid later emergencies.

- **Rationale**:  
The articles all evaluate “light therapy” as photodynamic therapy (PDT) with verteporfin for age‑related macular degeneration (AMD) with abnormal blood vessels under the retina. I focused on how much PDT reduces the chance of moderate-to-severe vision loss compared with placebo, across different lesion types (predominantly classic, minimally classic, and occult). I also considered safety data. Overall, PDT consistently slows, but does not stop, vision loss in certain well-defined groups, with smaller or more “classic” lesions benefiting most, and mixed results for purely occult lesions.

- **Full Answer**:  
Light therapy in these studies means photodynamic therapy (PDT) with verteporfin for age‑related macular degeneration, where new blood vessels grow under the central retina. [3][4] PDT rarely improves sight but can slow how quickly vision is lost in some patients. [3][4][5]

In predominantly classic lesions, about 61% of PDT‑treated eyes avoided losing 3 or more lines of vision at 1 year, versus 46% with placebo. [4] For small, minimally classic lesions, about 72–86% of treated eyes avoided this degree of loss at 1 year, versus 53% with placebo, with some benefit to 2 years. [5] For lesions that were entirely occult, results were mixed: one large trial showed little overall benefit, while another found a modest benefit in selected patients. [2][3]

Side effects were usually mild, such as temporary visual disturbances and light‑sensitive skin reactions. [2][4][5] Overall, PDT modestly reduces—but does not eliminate—the risk of vision loss. [3][4][5]

- **Rationale**:  
The question is about how *ineffective* light‑activated verteporfin treatment (photodynamic therapy) is for age‑related macular degeneration (AMD), a leading cause of vision loss. The trials compare verteporfin to placebo and different light regimens. For “occult” (hard‑to‑see) abnormal vessels, verteporfin performs only slightly better than placebo and often not significantly better. For “classic” or “minimally classic” vessels, it clearly slows vision loss, but many patients still lose substantial vision. Changing timing, dose, or retreatment schedule of the light does not meaningfully change this.

- **Full Answer**:  
Light‑activated verteporfin treatment (photodynamic “light therapy”) for age‑related macular degeneration is only modestly helpful and, in some common situations, not better than sham treatment.

In patients whose abnormal vessels are “occult” (hard to see on dye testing), 37–47% of treated eyes versus 45–53% of placebo eyes lost at least three lines of vision over two years, with no meaningful difference between groups [2]. Another trial in similar patients showed a statistically better result, but still 54% of treated eyes lost at least three lines, compared with 67% of placebo eyes, and 30% vs 47% lost six or more lines [3].

For eyes with more visible (“classic” or “minimally classic”) new vessels, light therapy reduced the chance of losing three or more lines of vision, but 14–39% of treated patients still lost that much vision within one year [4][5].

Altering light dose, timing, or retreatment intensity did not improve outcomes [1][5][6].

- **Rationale**:  
The articles evaluate “light therapy” called photodynamic therapy (PDT) using a drug (verteporfin) plus a special laser for age‑related macular degeneration (AMD), an eye disease that can cause serious vision loss. I looked at whether PDT slows or prevents vision loss, how strong the benefit is, which patients benefit most, and safety.

- **Full Answer**:  
A specific type of light‑based treatment, photodynamic therapy (PDT) with verteporfin, can help some people with age‑related macular degeneration that threatens central vision.  

In patients whose abnormal blood vessels under the retina are “predominantly classic,” PDT clearly reduced the chance of losing 3 or more lines of vision versus placebo over 1–2 years [3][4]. It also helped in many with “minimally classic” lesions [5]. For “occult only” lesions, results were mixed: one large trial found no clear benefit [1], while another showed a modest but statistically significant reduction in moderate and severe vision loss by 2 years, especially in smaller or more advanced lesions [2].  

Changing the timing or intensity of the light did not meaningfully change outcomes [0][6]. Overall, PDT is reasonably safe but mainly slows further loss; it rarely improves vision [1][3][4][5].

- **Rationale**:  
Research on verteporfin “light therapy” for wet age‑related macular degeneration shows mixed results. In some types of abnormal new vessels, especially predominantly classic and small minimally classic lesions, it clearly reduces the chance of moderate or severe vision loss versus placebo [3][4][5][6]. For broader groups with only occult vessels, studies found little or no statistically significant benefit [1][2].

- **Full Answer**:  
Light‑based verteporfin therapy does work for some eye diseases that cause vision loss, particularly certain forms of “wet” age‑related macular degeneration. In eyes with predominantly classic abnormal vessels, treatment significantly reduced the chance of moderate vision loss compared with placebo [4]. It also lowered vision loss risk in small minimally classic and some occult lesions, especially when lesions were small or starting vision was worse [3][5][6]. However, in larger groups of patients whose lesions were only occult, studies showed little or no clear overall benefit [1][2]. It does not restore normal vision or stop the disease in everyone.

- **Rationale**:  
The first study directly measured how much eye bulging (exophthalmos) decreased after two common orbital decompression surgeries in Graves’ disease. Both surgeries significantly reduced eye protrusion, showing that surgery is effective, but one technique caused more side effects like double vision and nerve injury, so the safer method was preferred [1]. The second study focused on patients with threatened vision from thyroid eye disease. It showed that while decompression surgery can improve vision, many patients still needed steroids or additional eye and eyelid surgeries, and immediate surgery was not better than starting with steroid treatment [2]. Together, these show decompression can substantially reduce bulging and protect vision but is major surgery with notable risks, usually reserved for more severe cases.

- **Full Answer**:  
Orbital decompression surgery is generally effective at reducing bulging eyes from thyroid eye disease. In a study of people with Graves’ eye disease whose eyes protruded more than 22 mm, both types of decompression surgery tested led to a significant backward movement of the eyes, meaning the bulging was clearly reduced on measurement [1]. However, one technique (Walsh and Ogura) caused more complications, particularly new double vision and numbness from infraorbital nerve injury, so the lower‑risk Kennedy technique was recommended [1]. 

Another trial looked at surgery mainly for patients with threatened vision. Many who started with surgery alone still needed steroid medicines afterward, and many in both groups later needed additional eyelid or eye‑muscle surgery to fine‑tune eye position and appearance [2]. Overall, decompression works well to reduce bulging and protect vision, but it is major surgery with real risks and may not always be the first treatment tried [1][2].

- **Rationale**:  
The first study shows orbital decompression reliably reduces eye bulging (exophthalmos) in Graves’ disease, though with some risk of double vision and nerve problems, especially with one technique [1]. That indicates surgery is effective for bulging but not risk‑free or cure‑all. The second study focuses on severe, active disease with optic nerve damage; here, surgery used alone as first treatment often failed, with most patients needing additional steroids [2]. Together, this suggests surgery works for bulging eyes but is rarely sufficient as the only treatment.

- **Full Answer**:  
Orbital decompression surgery is generally effective at reducing bulging eyes from thyroid eye disease, but it is not a perfect fix and often requires additional treatment.  

In people with stable Graves’ eye disease and protruding eyes, both common decompression techniques significantly reduced eye bulging (exophthalmos) beyond 22 mm, with no difference in how well they worked [1]. However, one technique caused more double vision and numbness from nerve injury, showing that side effects are an important limitation [1].  

In patients with very active disease and vision loss from optic nerve pressure, using decompression surgery alone as first treatment was often not enough: 5 out of 6 patients (82%) still needed strong steroid treatment afterward [2].
