Versus high‑quality rehabilitation alone, disc replacement gave only a modest extra average benefit, and there were rare but very serious surgical complications. Follow‑up is mostly limited to about two years, so longer‑term durability and late problems are less certain. Effectiveness also depends heavily on choosing the “right” patients.

- **Full Answer**:  
Disc replacement surgery is offered to people with long‑lasting low back pain from one worn disc who have not improved with non‑surgical care. In several randomized trials, patients who had disc replacement reported large drops in disability and pain over two years, similar to or slightly better than those who had fusion surgery to stiffen the spine; about 75–90% had meaningful improvement in daily function [1][2][3][4][6]. Around 30% became completely pain‑free after disc replacement, compared with 15% after fusion [3]. Compared with intensive rehabilitation alone, disc replacement improved disability and pain somewhat more, but the average extra benefit was small and one patient had a leg amputation after a serious complication [7]. Serious problems are uncommon but can be severe [6][7]. Overall, for well‑selected patients, disc replacement is a reasonably effective option, but it is not a cure and long‑term results beyond a few years remain still somewhat uncertain [3][7].

- **Rationale**:  
Most of the articles compare disc replacement surgery with spinal fusion, not with non-surgical care. Those trials show that, in carefully selected patients with single-level degenerative disc disease, disc replacement gives similar or somewhat better pain and function outcomes than fusion over about two years [1–6]. One key study directly compares disc prosthesis surgery with intensive rehabilitation in people with chronic low back pain lasting at least a year [7]. That study found that surgery did help more on average—but the extra benefit over good rehabilitation was modest, and there were also serious surgical risks. So disc replacement is not “ineffective,” but its added benefit over high-quality non-surgical care is limited for many patients.

- **Full Answer**:  
For chronic low back pain from a worn disc, disc replacement surgery is somewhat more effective than intensive rehabilitation, but the difference is modest. At two years, disability scores were on average 8.4 points better (on a 0–100 scale) with surgery, slightly below the 10‑point difference the researchers considered clearly meaningful [7]. More surgery patients were satisfied (63% vs 39%) and reported less pain [7]. However, many rehabilitation patients also improved substantially, and at least one very serious complication (leg amputation) occurred after surgery [7]. Other trials show disc replacement works about as well as or slightly better than spinal fusion in similar patients [1–6]. Overall, disc replacement is not useless, but its extra benefit over good non-surgical care is modest and must be weighed against surgical risks.

- **Rationale**:  
The summaries describe several randomized trials of lumbar disc replacement (“artificial disc”) in adults with chronic low back pain caused by one degenerated disc that did not improve with non‑surgical care. These studies compare disc replacement with spinal fusion or with intensive rehabilitation, using standard measures of pain, disability, function, and satisfaction, and follow patients for about two years. I used them to judge how well disc replacement works, how it compares with other options, the size of benefit, and the known risks and uncertainties.

- **Full Answer**:  
For carefully selected people whose long‑lasting low back pain comes mainly from a single worn disc, disc replacement surgery can reduce pain and disability and improve function for at least two years. In several large trials, artificial disc replacement worked at least as well as standard spinal fusion, and often slightly better, with similar or fewer complications and higher patient satisfaction [1–6]. About three‑quarters of surgical patients achieved meaningful improvement in function [2,4,6]. When disc replacement was compared with an intensive rehabilitation program, surgery led to somewhat greater average improvement in pain and disability, but the difference was modest and many non‑surgical patients also improved [7]. Serious complications are uncommon but can be severe, as shown by a leg amputation after a device problem in one patient [7]. Most studies only followed people for two to three years, so long‑term benefits and risks are still uncertain for many patients [1–7].

- **Rationale**:  
Across several randomized trials in people with chronic low back pain from one degenerative lumbar disc who had not improved with non‑surgical care, disc replacement surgery led to large average reductions in pain and disability over two years [1][2][3][4][5][6]. In most studies it worked at least as well as spinal fusion and was sometimes slightly better, with faster return to work and higher satisfaction [2][3][4][5][6]. A trial comparing disc replacement with intensive rehabilitation found surgery gave somewhat better function and pain scores, but the average advantage was modest and some patients did well without surgery [7]. Serious complications are uncommon but can be severe [7]. Overall, disc replacement can help selected patients, but it does not cure everyone and involves risks.

- **Full Answer**:  
Disc replacement surgery can help some people with long‑lasting low back pain, but it is not a guaranteed cure.  

In several randomized trials of patients with chronic low back pain from one damaged lumbar disc, most people who had disc replacement reported large drops in pain and disability scores over two years [1][2][4][6]. In one study, about 30% of disc‑replacement patients were completely pain‑free at two years, compared with 15% after standard fusion surgery [3]. Disc replacement usually worked at least as well as fusion and sometimes slightly better, and let many patients return to work sooner [2][4][5][6].  

However, many patients still had some pain, and improvement after intensive rehabilitation without surgery was only slightly less than with disc replacement for the average patient [7]. The operation also carries risks, including rare but serious complications [7].  

So disc replacement can work for carefully selected patients, but results vary widely between individuals.

- **Rationale**:  
The question is about how effective it is to get help from a paid worker (such as a support worker or personal care aide) for people with physical and brain-related problems. The articles show what happens when adults with aging, mental health, intellectual disability, or other disabilities receive structured support services, either in small community homes or in consumer-directed personal care programs. Outcomes include quality of life, ability to get needed help, community living, and health and safety.

- **Full Answer**:  
Help from a worker is generally quite effective for people with physical and brain-related problems. In small community care homes with case management, adults who were aging, had mental health conditions, or intellectual disabilities received more of the services they needed, lived in better conditions, were more integrated into the community, and spent fewer days in institutions, especially older adults and those with mental health issues [1].  

In consumer-directed personal care, people who managed an allowance to hire their own helpers were more likely to get paid care, were more satisfied, and had fewer unmet care needs, without increased injuries or health problems [2]. The benefits were substantial when people actually received and used the support funds [2].

- **Rationale**: Research on supportive services for people with physical or brain-related problems (such as mental health conditions) evaluated how case-managed housing and consumer-directed personal care affect quality of life, unmet needs, and safety. The Pennsylvania domiciliary care study compared people in small supervised homes to similar people without the program. The Cash and Counseling trial randomly assigned Medicaid recipients to hire and manage helpers or receive usual agency care, then measured outcomes and harms.

- **Full Answer**: Across these studies, help from workers was effective, not ineffective. In the Pennsylvania program, participants in supervised small homes had better living conditions, more needed services, more community involvement, and fewer days in institutions than similar people without the program [1]. In Cash and Counseling, people who could direct and pay helpers had more paid care, fewer unmet needs, and higher satisfaction, without added health problems or injuries [2]. Only one elderly subgroup saw little change [2].

- **Rationale**:  
Research in these summaries looks at organized help from workers, such as case managers, care home staff, and paid personal assistants. In older adults and people with mental health conditions, this help improved everyday life, living conditions, and use of needed services [1]. For people with physical or other disabilities, programs that let them hire and direct their own helpers led to more paid help, higher satisfaction, and fewer unmet needs, without extra health risks [2]. Effects were weaker for people with intellectual disabilities and for elders who did not actually receive the new type of help [1,2].

- **Full Answer**:  
Overall, help from trained workers or user‑hired helpers does work for both physical and brain‑related problems: it improves quality of life, increases needed services, and reduces unmet care needs, without adding health risks, particularly for older adults, people with mental illness, and those with physical disabilities, though effects differ by group [1,2].

- **Rationale**:  
The question seems to ask whether assistance from a worker (such as personal care aides, case managers, or helpers paid through a program) is ineffective for people with physical and brain-related problems. The first article evaluates a program providing structured support and small community homes for older adults, people with mental health issues, and people with intellectual disabilities. It finds generally positive effects on quality of life, especially for aging and mental health groups, with smaller but still positive effects for intellectual disability [1]. The second article studies a “Cash and Counseling” model, where people with disabilities direct their own paid personal care. It shows more paid help, fewer unmet needs, and greater satisfaction, without increased health risks, for people of all ages with disabilities [2]. Together, these studies indicate that worker support can be beneficial for both physical and cognitive/mental conditions when the support is actually provided and well-organized.

- **Full Answer**:  
Help from workers can be effective for both physical disabilities and brain-related problems.  

In the Pennsylvania Domiciliary Care Program, adults who received organized support in small community homes—such as help with daily tasks, coordination of services, and supervision—had better quality of life than similar people without the program. They got more needed services, lived in better conditions, were more involved in the community, and spent fewer days in institutions, especially older adults and those with mental health problems [1]. Benefits were smaller, but still present, for people with intellectual disabilities (“mental retardation”) [1].  

In the Cash and Counseling program, people of all ages with disabilities who got funds to arrange their own personal care (often hiring aides or workers) had more paid help, fewer unmet needs, and higher satisfaction, without more injuries or health problems [2].  

These findings show that well-organized worker support can help both physical and cognitive conditions.

- **Rationale**:  
Studies of fish oil (omega‑3 / EPA) in people with cancer‑related weight loss show mixed and mostly modest effects. In one trial of advanced cancer patients, high-dose fish oil capsules for 2 weeks did not improve appetite, calorie intake, weight, or well‑being compared with placebo, and many patients struggled to take enough capsules [1]. In another study, a high‑calorie, high‑protein drink enriched with omega‑3s only helped patients gain weight and muscle when they were able to drink enough; overall, it was no better than a similar drink without omega‑3s [2]. When fish oil was compared with the appetite drug megestrol, far fewer fish‑oil patients achieved meaningful weight gain (6% vs 18%) [3]. Short‑term fish oil also did not clearly reduce the body’s breakdown of fat [4]. One trial suggested fish oil might modestly improve immunity and survival, especially in malnourished patients, but did not show clear weight gain [5].  

- **Full Answer**:  
Overall, fish oil medicine alone is not a reliably effective treatment for cancer‑related weight loss. High‑dose fish oil capsules did not significantly improve appetite, calorie intake, weight, or strength compared with placebo in advanced cancer patients, and many could not tolerate the capsule load [1]. A nutrition drink enriched with omega‑3s was, on average, no better than a similar drink without omega‑3s in stopping weight loss, though patients who managed to take enough did gain some weight and muscle [2]. Compared with the standard appetite drug megestrol, fish oil was clearly less effective for meaningful weight gain (6% vs 18% achieving ≥10% weight gain) and did not improve appetite more than megestrol [3]. Short‑term fish oil did not reduce fat breakdown, calling its mechanism into question [4]. One study found immune and survival benefits, particularly in malnourished patients, but not clear reversal of weight loss [5].

- **Rationale**:  
Across several good-quality studies in people with advanced cancer and weight loss, fish oil or EPA supplements generally did **not** meaningfully stop weight loss or improve appetite compared with placebo or standard appetite medicines. In one large trial, far fewer patients on EPA gained substantial weight than on megestrol acetate, a usual drug for cancer‑related weight loss. One enriched nutrition drink with EPA showed possible benefit only in patients who managed to take high enough doses, but this was a post‑hoc (exploratory) finding and not the main study result. Overall, fish oil is at best weak and unreliable for treating cancer‑related weight loss.

- **Full Answer**:  
Fish oil (omega‑3/EPA) has shown **little overall benefit** for cancer‑related weight loss:

- In advanced cancer patients with poor appetite, high‑dose fish oil for 2 weeks did **not** improve appetite, calorie intake, weight, or strength compared with placebo [1].  
- In pancreatic cancer cachexia, an EPA‑enriched drink was **no better** than an identical high‑calorie, high‑protein drink without EPA at stopping weight loss (both groups’ weight loss slowed similarly) [2].  
- In 421 patients, only 6% on EPA alone gained ≥10% of their starting weight, versus 18% on megestrol acetate (a standard appetite stimulant); adding EPA to megestrol did **not** improve results [3].  
- Short‑term EPA did **not** change fat breakdown in weight‑losing cancer patients [4].  

Overall, fish oil is **weak and unreliable** as a medicine for cancer‑related weight loss, and clearly less effective than megestrol acetate [1–4].

- **Rationale**: Trials of fish oil (omega‑3, EPA) for cancer‑related weight loss mostly show no meaningful benefit compared with placebo or standard appetite‑stimulating drugs. One study suggests that when omega‑3s are taken in high enough amounts as part of a calorie‑ and protein‑rich drink, some patients may gain weight and lean tissue, but this was not seen on average and required good intake of the supplement. Overall, evidence does not support using fish oil alone as a primary treatment for cancer‑related weight loss, though it might be considered as an optional add‑on under medical supervision.

- **Full Answer**:  
Fish oil alone is not a reliable treatment for cancer‑related weight loss.

In a 2‑week study, high‑dose fish oil capsules did not improve appetite, food intake, weight, strength, or well‑being versus placebo [1], and did not reduce fat breakdown [4]. In a large trial, an EPA drink led to less weight gain than the standard drug megestrol, and adding EPA to megestrol gave no extra benefit [3].  

A calorie‑ and protein‑dense drink enriched with omega‑3s stopped weight loss, but worked no better overall than a similar non‑fish‑oil drink; only patients who drank enough of the omega‑3 formula seemed to gain weight and lean tissue [2].  

High‑dose omega‑3 plus vitamin E may improve immunity and survival but was not shown to reverse weight loss [5].  

Fish oil should be used, if at all, only as a supportive add‑on under guidance from an oncologist and dietitian.

- **Rationale**: Trials of fish‑oil supplements in advanced cancer show little help for weight.

- **Full Answer**:  
Fish oil is not a reliable treatment to reverse cancer‑related weight loss (cachexia).

In patients with advanced cancer, two weeks of high‑dose fish‑oil capsules did not improve appetite, food intake, weight, or strength compared with placebo [1]. Short‑term EPA (a fish‑oil component) pills also did not change the fat‑breakdown process that drives weight loss [4]. In a large study, an EPA‑enriched nutrition drink was no better overall than a similar high‑calorie drink without EPA, although patients who drank enough EPA tended to gain some weight and muscle [2]. Another trial showed an EPA supplement was less effective than the standard appetite drug megestrol for weight gain [3]. 

Fish oil may have other benefits, such as possible immune effects and slightly longer survival in some patients [5], but it should be considered an optional supplement, not a main treatment, for cancer weight loss.

- **Rationale**:  
The evidence on face masks and wound infection comes from three surgical studies. One very small trial in gynecologic surgery found fewer infections when the whole team wore masks, but it included only nine major abdominal operations, so the result is uncertain [1]. Two much larger, better-powered studies in general and mixed surgery found no statistically meaningful reduction in wound infections when masks were worn. One showed similar infection rates when non-scrubbed staff did or did not wear masks [2]. Another found slightly *more* infections with masks, but the difference was not statistically significant [3]. Overall, these studies suggest that, with a healthy surgical team and other standard sterile practices in place, masks have not been clearly proven to lower wound infection rates, though they may still protect staff from blood and respiratory droplets [3].

- **Full Answer**:  
Research on face masks in the operating room shows mixed and mostly negative evidence for preventing wound infections.

A small gynaecological study found that, after major abdominal surgery, 3 of 5 patients developed wound infections when the surgical team did not wear masks, compared with 0 of 4 when masks were used; more bacteria also settled on the operating table without masks [1]. However, this trial was very small, so its results are uncertain.

Larger controlled studies in general and mixed surgeries found no clear benefit. When non-scrubbed staff did not wear masks, the overall surgical site infection rate did not increase (about 10–12% in both groups) [2]. Another study of over 3,000 operations showed 4.7% infections with masks versus 3.5% without, a difference that was not statistically significant [3].

Taken together, these studies suggest that, in modern operating rooms, face masks worn by staff have not been clearly proven to reduce wound infections in patients, though they may protect staff themselves [3].

- **Rationale**:  
Across studies, masks worn by operating room staff have shown little or no clear benefit in preventing wound infections. One very small trial in gynecologic surgery found fewer infections when the whole team wore masks (0/4 vs 3/5 for major abdominal surgery), but this was based on only nine patients, so the result is uncertain [1]. Larger, better-powered trials did not confirm a protective effect. In a study of more than 3,000 general surgery patients, infection rates were 4.7% with masks and 3.5% without, a small difference that could easily be due to chance [3]. Another trial of 811 patients found 11.5% infections when non‑scrubbed staff wore masks versus 9.0% when they did not, again a non‑significant difference [2]. Overall, if masks reduce wound infections at all, the effect appears small and unproven under modern sterile conditions.

- **Full Answer**:  
Evidence suggests surgical face masks are, at best, only weakly effective for preventing wound infections.

In a tiny study of major gynecologic surgery (only nine patients), none of the masked patients developed infections, compared with 3 of 5 unmasked patients, suggesting a possible benefit but with very low certainty [1].

However, far larger studies do not show a meaningful reduction. In more than 3,000 general surgery cases, 4.7% of patients had wound infections when masks were used versus 3.5% when they were not, a small and statistically non‑significant difference [3]. In another trial of 811 patients, infection rates were 11.5% when non‑scrubbed staff wore masks and 9.0% when they did not, again with no clear evidence that masks helped [2].

Taken together, these studies indicate that routine mask use by a healthy surgical team has not been proven to significantly lower postoperative wound infection rates, and any benefit is likely to be small [1–3].

- **Rationale**: Across the studies, masks worn by operating room staff did not clearly lower patients’ wound infection rates. The largest trial in general surgery found similar infection rates whether the whole team wore masks or not [3]. Another large trial also showed no increase in surgical site infections when non-scrubbed staff stopped wearing masks [2]. Only a small trial in gynecologic surgery suggested fewer infections with masks [1], but numbers were too small for conclusions.

- **Full Answer**: Overall, research does not show that surgical face masks reliably prevent wound infections in surgery. In a large general-surgery study, wound infections occurred at similar rates whether staff wore masks or not [3]. A second large trial found no higher infection rate when non-scrubbed staff did not wear masks [2]. Only a small study in gynecologic surgery suggested fewer infections with masks [1]. Masks still help protect staff from blood and airborne germs [3].

- **Rationale**:  
One small study in gynecological surgery found fewer wound infections when the whole surgical team wore masks (0/4 masked vs 3/5 unmasked after major abdominal surgery), suggesting possible benefit, but the numbers were very small [1]. Larger, better-powered studies in general surgery and mixed operations found no statistically significant difference in wound infection rates whether masks were worn or not [2][3]. One found slightly *more* infections in the masked group, but this difference was not meaningful statistically [3]. Another found that not masking non-scrubbed staff did not increase infections [2]. Overall, these studies suggest that routine use of surgical masks by healthy staff has not been clearly proven to reduce postoperative wound infections, though masks can still protect staff from blood splashes and possibly from respiratory germs [3].

- **Full Answer**:  
Current research does **not** clearly show that surgical face masks reliably prevent wound (surgical site) infections in patients.  

A small trial in gynecologic surgery reported fewer infections when the entire team wore masks, but it included only nine major abdominal cases, so its results are uncertain [1].  

Larger, more robust studies in general and mixed surgeries found no meaningful difference in wound infection rates between operations done with masks and those done without them [2][3]. In one large study, infection rates were 4.7% with masks and 3.5% without masks—this difference was not statistically significant [3]. Another randomized trial showed no increase in infections when non-scrubbed staff did not wear masks [2].  

Overall, masks have not been convincingly proven to protect patients from wound infections when worn by healthy operating room staff, although they remain important for protecting staff from blood splashes and some airborne infections [3].

- **Rationale**:  
The question is broad, but the articles specifically study treatment of a vaginal infection called Trichomonas vaginalis in pregnancy. I focused on how well the medicine clears the infection and whether it improves pregnancy outcomes. The studies show high cure rates with metronidazole-type drugs, but little or no benefit for preventing early birth, and one trial showed a possible increase in preterm delivery. So the answer explains that the medicine is effective at curing the infection but not clearly beneficial for pregnancy outcomes, and that treatment choices should be made with a clinician.

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicines such as metronidazole are good at clearing certain vaginal infections (Trichomonas vaginalis) during pregnancy. In a large trial, over 90% of pregnant women given metronidazole had the infection cleared, compared with about one‑third given placebo [1]. Another study also found a single 2‑gram dose of benzoylmetronidazole to be “highly effective” at curing the infection [2]. However, curing the infection did not improve babies’ outcomes. In the large trial, women who received metronidazole actually had more preterm deliveries than women given placebo (19% vs 11%) [1]. In the other study, birth weight and length of pregnancy were similar in uninfected women, infected women who were treated, and infected women who were not treated [2]. Overall, treatment is effective for getting rid of Trichomonas infection but does not appear to protect against early birth, and in some cases may increase that risk. Treatment decisions should be made with your prenatal provider.

- **Rationale**:  
The studies focus on a specific vaginal infection in pregnancy: *Trichomonas vaginalis*. Metronidazole-type medicines were very successful at clearing the infection itself, but the key concern is whether treatment improves pregnancy outcomes, especially preterm birth. In the larger, more modern trial, clearing the infection did **not** lower the risk of preterm delivery and was actually linked to *more* early births. An earlier study found no clear benefit or harm on birth weight or timing of delivery. So, the medicine is effective at getting rid of the parasite, but largely ineffective— and possibly harmful—if the goal is to prevent early birth in women who have no symptoms.

- **Full Answer**:  
In pregnant women with **asymptomatic** Trichomonas vaginalis, metronidazole cleared the infection in about 93% of women, compared with 35% clearing on placebo [1]. However, despite curing the infection, it did **not** prevent early birth. In fact, preterm delivery occurred in 19.0% of treated women versus 10.7% with placebo (about a 1.8‑fold higher risk) [1].  

An earlier study using a similar drug found it was highly effective at treating the infection, but birth weights and gestational ages were similar whether women were uninfected, treated, or left untreated [2].  

Overall, these medicines are **effective at curing the vaginal infection itself**, but appear **ineffective at improving pregnancy outcomes** such as preterm birth in women without symptoms, and in one good study were associated with **more** preterm deliveries [1][2].

- **Rationale**:  
Both studies looked at Trichomonas vaginalis, a specific vaginal infection in pregnancy. The medicines used (metronidazole/benzoylmetronidazole) were very effective at clearing the infection itself. In the large trial, most treated women had no trichomonas on follow‑up, far more than in the placebo group [1]. The second study also described the single‑dose treatment as “highly effective” [2]. However, when researchers checked whether treatment improved pregnancy outcomes (like birth weight or avoiding preterm birth), they found no benefit. In fact, in one large study, treated women had *more* preterm births than those on placebo [1]. The other study found no differences in birth weight or gestational age between treated, untreated, and uninfected women [2]. So, medicine “works” to cure this infection, but it does not clearly help the baby and may increase early birth risk when used routinely in symptom‑free women, so decisions must be individualized.

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicines such as metronidazole can effectively clear Trichomonas vaginalis, a common vaginal parasite, in pregnancy. In one study, about 93% of treated women had the infection cleared, compared with about 35% given placebo [1]. Another study also found a single dose of benzoylmetronidazole was “highly effective” at curing the infection [2].

However, in women who had no symptoms, treating the infection did not improve the baby’s birth weight or the timing of birth [1][2]. In the larger trial, women given metronidazole actually had more preterm births (before 37 weeks) than those given placebo [1]. Because of this, routine screening and treatment of symptom‑free pregnant women for this specific infection is not recommended just to prevent early delivery [1].

If you are pregnant and have vaginal symptoms or test positive for an infection, your clinician will weigh symptom relief and other risks against potential effects on pregnancy before prescribing treatment.

- **Rationale**:  
Both studies looked at a specific vaginal infection in pregnancy caused by Trichomonas vaginalis and treatment with metronidazole-type drugs. The medicine clearly cleared the infection in most women. However, treating women who had no symptoms did *not* reduce the chance of having a premature baby; in one study, early treatment was linked with *more* preterm births. This means the medicine works for the infection itself, but not necessarily for improving pregnancy outcomes like preterm delivery.

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicine for certain vaginal infections in pregnancy, such as Trichomonas vaginalis, does work to clear the infection. In one study, metronidazole cured the infection in over 90% of treated women, compared with about 35% without treatment [1]. Another study found a single dose of a related drug was “highly effective” at treating the infection [2].  

However, treating women who had no symptoms did **not** prevent early birth and was actually linked to more preterm deliveries in one trial [1]. A second study found no difference in birth weight or timing of delivery between treated, untreated, and uninfected women [2].  

So, the medicine works against the infection, but does not necessarily improve pregnancy outcomes. Decisions about treatment in pregnancy should be made with your healthcare provider.

- **Rationale**:  
The articles all study nitroglycerin (glyceryl trinitrate) patches placed over painful shoulder tendons. Two trials in supraspinatus tendinitis show that patches are better than placebo and better than exercise alone for reducing pain and improving function, including over several months [2][3]. However, another trial in recent-onset rotator cuff tendinitis found nitroglycerin patches clearly less effective than a standard steroid injection [1]. Headache is a frequent side effect and sometimes causes people to stop using the patches [1][2]. Overall, patch treatment can help some types of shoulder tendon pain but is not the strongest option available and has trade‑offs.

- **Full Answer**:  
Several studies have tested nitroglycerin (glyceryl trinitrate) patches placed over the sore shoulder tendon.

In a small study of supraspinatus tendonitis, a 5‑mg nitroglycerin patch worn for 3 days reduced pain clearly more than a placebo patch within 24–48 hours, and benefits lasted at least 15 days [2]. In a larger 6‑month trial of chronic supraspinatus tendinopathy, low‑dose nitroglycerin patches plus exercises improved pain, movement, and strength more than exercises alone; 46% of people using patches were pain‑free in daily activities, versus 24% with exercises only [3].

However, in recent-onset rotator cuff tendonitis that had not improved with anti‑inflammatory pills, a 3‑day 5‑mg nitroglycerin patch worked much worse than a steroid injection: only 10 of 24 patch users improved fully or partly, compared with 22 of 24 in the injection group [1].

Headache is common with nitroglycerin patches and led some patients to stop treatment [1][2].

- **Rationale**:  
The question focuses on “how ineffective” patch medicine is, so I compared nitroglycerin patches with both stronger active treatment (steroid injection) and weaker comparators (placebo, exercise alone). One trial showed patches were much worse than steroid injections and caused frequent headaches, suggesting they are relatively ineffective when better options exist [1]. However, two randomized, placebo-controlled trials showed that patches can meaningfully reduce pain and improve function versus placebo or exercise alone, though benefits were modest and side effects (especially headache) were common [2,3]. Thus, patches are not useless, but many patients either do not improve enough or cannot tolerate them.

- **Full Answer**:  
Patch medicines using nitroglycerin for rotator cuff or supraspinatus tendon shoulder pain are not clearly ineffective, but their benefit is limited and depends on what they’re compared with.  

When nitroglycerin patches were compared with a steroid injection, only 42% of patch users had meaningful pain relief, versus 92% with the injection; treatment failed in 58% on patches versus 8% with injections, and headaches caused many to stop the patches [1]. In a very small 3‑day trial, patches worked better than placebo for short‑term pain, function, and sleep, with 2 of 10 patients getting headaches [2]. Over 6 months, adding a low‑dose patch to exercises made 46% of people pain‑free in daily activities, versus 24% with exercises alone; overall improvements were statistically significant but small in size [3].  

So nitroglycerin patches may help some people, but many get little benefit or troublesome headaches, and injections work better overall on average [1–3].

- **Rationale**:  
The question is about “patch medicine” for shoulder pain. All three studies are about nitroglycerin (glyceryl trinitrate) patches used for rotator cuff/supraspinatus tendon problems, not for all causes of shoulder pain. Two trials showed that nitroglycerin patches reduce pain and improve function versus placebo or exercises alone in supraspinatus tendinitis/tendinopathy, with some patients becoming symptom‑free [2][3]. Another trial in recent rotator cuff tendinitis found nitroglycerin patches much less effective than a steroid injection and causing many headaches, leading to treatment discontinuation [1]. Overall, nitroglycerin patches can work for certain shoulder tendon conditions but are not the best option for everyone and have notable side effects.

- **Full Answer**:  
Research has looked at a specific “patch medicine” for shoulder tendon pain: nitroglycerin (also called glyceryl trinitrate) patches.  

In one small study of people with supraspinatus (rotator cuff) tendonitis, a 5‑mg nitroglycerin patch worn over the painful area for 3 days reduced pain and improved sleep and arm use compared with a placebo patch. Benefits lasted at least 15 days [2]. In a larger 6‑month trial of chronic supraspinatus tendinopathy, low‑dose nitroglycerin patches plus exercises improved pain, motion, and shoulder strength more than exercises alone; about 46% became symptom‑free versus 24% with rehab only [3].  

However, another trial in recent rotator cuff tendonitis found a 3‑day nitroglycerin patch worked much worse than a steroid injection and caused frequent headaches, leading some people to stop treatment [1].  

Overall, nitroglycerin patches can help some shoulder tendon problems, but benefits are modest and headaches are common. They’re considered mainly after standard treatments [1][2][3].

- **Rationale**:  
The articles all studied a specific “patch medicine”: nitroglycerin (also called glyceryl trinitrate) for rotator cuff/supraspinatus tendon problems. One trial in recent rotator cuff tendinitis found these patches worked worse than a steroid injection and caused many headaches, so they were not a good alternative in that setting [1]. However, two other placebo‑controlled trials in supraspinatus tendinitis/tendinopathy (especially chronic cases) showed real benefits in pain, function, and strength compared with placebo or rehab alone, although headaches were still common [2][3]. So the evidence does not say that “patch medicine doesn’t work”; rather, it suggests nitroglycerin patches may help certain chronic tendon‑related shoulder problems but are not as effective as steroid injections in acute cases and can cause side effects.

- **Full Answer**:  
Research on nitroglycerin “patch medicine” for shoulder pain shows mixed results.  

In people with recent rotator cuff tendinitis that did not improve with anti‑inflammatory pills, a 3‑day nitroglycerin patch helped far fewer patients than a steroid injection, and many stopped it because of headaches [1]. So for this type of new-onset shoulder pain, patches were clearly less effective than injections [1].  

However, in small, well‑designed trials of supraspinatus (rotator cuff) tendinitis, nitroglycerin patches did reduce pain, improve sleep and arm use within days, and benefits lasted at least 2 weeks [2]. In a larger 6‑month trial for chronic supraspinatus tendinopathy, patches plus rehab improved pain, movement, and strength more than rehab alone, and about twice as many patients became symptom‑free in daily activities [3].  

So patch medicine can work for some rotator cuff problems, especially chronic tendinopathy, but it is not a cure‑all and often causes troublesome headaches in some people.

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks how well nausea medicines work for vomiting from a “stomach bug” (infectious gastroenteritis). The studies mainly evaluate ondansetron and, separately, dimenhydrinate in children. Across multiple randomized, placebo‑controlled trials, ondansetron consistently reduces vomiting and improves success of oral rehydration, with secondary benefits (less IV fluid use, fewer admissions), but with a bit more diarrhea and, in one study, more return visits. Dimenhydrinate reduces vomiting frequency but does not clearly improve hydration or key clinical outcomes, so its practical benefit is smaller. I focused on clear, patient‑centered outcomes (vomiting, hydration, admission) and expressed effect sizes in simple comparative terms.

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicines for nausea can reduce vomiting from a “stomach bug,” especially ondansetron (Zofran).

In trials in children with gastroenteritis, ondansetron cut vomiting episodes and the chance of any vomiting over the next 8–24 hours by roughly half compared with placebo [1][2][3]. In one study, 14% on ondansetron vomited during rehydration versus 35% on placebo [3]; in another, 58% on ondansetron had no vomiting compared with 17% on placebo [1]. More children could keep fluids down and finish oral rehydration [2][3][4][7].

As a result, ondansetron reduced the need for IV fluids and hospital admission: rates dropped from about one‑third–one‑half of children to around one‑sixth–one‑fifth [3][6][7]. The main downside was a small increase in diarrhea and, in one study, more return visits [1][3][6].

Dimenhydrinate modestly reduced vomiting but did not improve hydration, well‑being, or admission rates, so its overall benefit was limited [5]. Both medicines were generally well tolerated in studies overall [1][3][5].

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks whether nausea medicines are ineffective for vomiting from a stomach bug. The articles mainly study ondansetron and dimenhydrinate in children with viral gastroenteritis. I looked at how much these drugs reduce vomiting and whether they improve important outcomes like avoiding IV fluids, hospital admission, and successful drinking (oral rehydration). Ondansetron consistently shows a clear, short‑term benefit, while dimenhydrinate shows only modest benefit without better overall clinical outcomes. I will summarize how “effective vs. ineffective” they are in plain terms, including approximate effect sizes, and note important trade‑offs (like more diarrhea and no clear shortening of the illness).

- **Full Answer**:  
Nausea medicine is not useless for a stomach bug, but it is not a cure either.  

Ondansetron clearly helps many children vomit less in the short term. In several trials, it cut the chance of vomiting by about half to two‑thirds compared with no medicine and increased the number of children who stopped vomiting altogether [1][2][3][6]. It also made it easier for kids to keep down fluids, so fewer needed IV fluids or hospital admission [3][4][6][7]. However, it did not make the illness shorter, and some studies found more diarrhea afterward [1][3][6].  

Dimenhydrinate reduced vomiting slightly, but it did not improve hydration, hospital admission, or children’s overall condition; researchers judged its overall benefit as low [5].  

So, anti‑nausea medicines—especially ondansetron—are moderately effective for short‑term control of vomiting from a stomach bug, but they do not fix the infection itself and are not always necessary.

- **Rationale**: Trials looked at children with vomiting from viral gastroenteritis (“stomach bug”) who were given nausea medicines versus placebo. Ondansetron consistently cut how many children vomited and how often, and helped more kids keep down oral rehydration fluids, reducing the need for IV fluids and some admissions, though it sometimes caused more diarrhea [1][2][3][4][6][7]. Dimenhydrinate modestly reduced vomiting episodes but did not change hydration, hospital admission, or overall recovery, so its benefit was small [5]. These data support that modern anti‑nausea drugs, especially ondansetron, can help vomiting from a stomach bug, mainly to allow better rehydration, not to cure the infection.

- **Full Answer**: Yes. In children with vomiting from a stomach bug, ondansetron clearly reduces vomiting and helps them keep down oral rehydration, lowering the need for IV fluids and some hospital admissions [1][2][3][4][6][7]. Another drug, dimenhydrinate, reduces vomiting slightly but does not improve hydration or recovery much [5]. Side effects seem mild.

- **Rationale**:  
Studies in children with stomach bugs show that some anti-nausea medicines, especially ondansetron, reduce vomiting and help kids drink, though they don’t cure the illness and can slightly increase diarrhea. Others, like dimenhydrinate, help less and don’t much change overall recovery.

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicines for nausea can work for vomiting from a stomach bug, but benefit depends on the specific drug.  

Several studies in children found that ondansetron, by mouth or vein, cut vomiting episodes and made it more likely that vomiting stopped compared with placebo [1,2,3,4,6,7]. It also helped kids drink more and reduced the need for IV fluids and hospital admission, making oral rehydration easier [2,3,4,6,7]. Dimenhydrinate reduced vomiting somewhat but did not improve hydration, hospital admission, or overall recovery [5]. Ondansetron may cause more diarrhea [1,3,6].  

These medicines treat symptoms only and are usually used when vomiting is bad enough to prevent drinking. A clinician should decide if they’re appropriate.

- **Rationale**:  
The question is about “surgery with small cuts,” meaning laparoscopic (“keyhole”) surgery for Crohn’s disease. Four randomized trials compared laparoscopic ileocolic resection with standard open surgery, looking at recovery, complications, quality of life, costs, and long‑term relapse and reoperation. Across these studies, disease control and long‑term outcomes were similar, while short‑term recovery and some complication rates favored laparoscopic surgery. This allows a clear lay explanation of how effective keyhole surgery is, and what its main pros and cons are, using only results supported by the articles.

- **Full Answer**:  
Laparoscopic (“keyhole”) surgery for Crohn’s involving the end of the small bowel and beginning of the colon works at least as well as traditional open surgery for controlling disease and preventing relapse. Long‑term follow‑up (about 7–11 years) shows similar rates of flare‑ups, need for another bowel resection, and use of Crohn’s medicines between keyhole and open operations [1][4]. Keyhole surgery brings several short‑term advantages: smaller cuts (about 5 cm vs 12 cm) [2], fewer early complications such as wound infections [2][3], faster recovery of breathing and fitness [2], and shorter hospital stays (about 5 days vs 6–7 days) [2][3]. Over the long term, patients having keyhole surgery have fewer problems like incisional hernias and bowel blockages from scar tissue, and are less likely to need multiple further operations [1][4]. Quality of life ends up similar, but patients are more satisfied with their body image and scars after keyhole surgery [1][3].

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks whether laparoscopic (“small cuts”) surgery for Crohn’s disease is less effective than traditional open surgery. The cited randomized trials compare relapse, need for repeat surgery, and long‑term outcomes, as well as recovery and complications. Across several studies with up to 10 years of follow‑up, laparoscopic surgery controls Crohn’s disease at least as well as open surgery, while offering some recovery and cosmetic advantages.

- **Full Answer**:  
“Surgery with small cuts” (laparoscopic ileocolic resection) for Crohn’s disease is **not** more ineffective than standard open surgery.  

In long‑term follow‑up around 7 years, relapse‑free rates and need for repeat bowel resections were very similar between laparoscopic and open surgery [1]. Overall reoperations for Crohn’s or surgical problems were also comparable, with a trend toward fewer reoperations after laparoscopy [1].  

Another trial with about 10 years of follow‑up likewise found no difference in Crohn’s recurrence or need for additional surgery; laparoscopic patients were actually less likely to need multiple later operations [4].  

Short‑term studies show no clinical recurrences during roughly 1.5–4 years of follow‑up [2], and similar quality of life, but with fewer complications, shorter hospital stay, and better body image after laparoscopy [1][2][3].  

Overall, laparoscopic surgery is at least as effective as open surgery for controlling Crohn’s, and often safer and easier to recover from.

- **Rationale**: Several randomized studies compared “keyhole” (laparoscopic) ileocolic surgery with traditional open surgery for Crohn’s disease. They found similar long‑term control of Crohn’s and similar need for repeat surgery, but with some short‑term benefits and fewer wound problems for the small‑cut approach [1][2][3][4].

- **Full Answer**: Yes. For people needing removal of the diseased last part of the small bowel and first part of the colon (ileocolic resection), surgery done through small cuts works as well as the traditional large‑cut operation at controlling Crohn’s in the long run, with similar relapse and reoperation rates up to 10 years [1][4]. In the short term, small‑cut surgery leads to faster lung function recovery, slightly shorter hospital stays, and fewer early complications than standard surgery in selected patients [2][3]. Patients also report better body image and cosmetic results, and have fewer incisional hernias and bowel blockages from scars after laparoscopic surgery [1][3] by experienced Crohn’s surgical teams.

- **Rationale**:  
Across several randomized trials, “small‑cut” (laparoscopic) surgery for Crohn’s disease of the ileum and right colon worked at least as well as traditional open surgery. Long‑term follow‑up (about 7–10 years) shows similar rates of relapse, need for repeat surgery, and overall recurrence between laparoscopic and open ileocolic resection [1][4]. In one trial, 16 of 29 laparoscopic patients and 16 of 26 open patients stayed relapse‑free, and repeat resections were almost the same [1]. Another long‑term study found similar reoperation and recurrence rates, concluding laparoscopic surgery is at least comparable to open surgery [4]. Short‑term, laparoscopic surgery led to fewer complications, shorter hospital stays, faster recovery of lung function, lower costs, and better body image and scar appearance [1][2][3]. Some laparoscopic cases must be converted to open surgery due to severe inflammation or adhesions, but overall effectiveness is not reduced [2][3].

- **Full Answer**:  
No. For suitable Crohn’s patients, surgery with small cuts (laparoscopic ileocolic resection) works just as well as traditional open surgery, with similar long‑term control of disease and need for further operations [1][4]. In long‑term studies, relapse and repeat‑surgery rates were essentially the same between laparoscopic and open groups [1][4].  

In the short term, laparoscopic surgery often has advantages: fewer postoperative complications, shorter hospital stays, faster recovery of breathing function, and lower overall costs [2][3]. Patients also tend to rate their body image and scar appearance more positively after laparoscopic surgery, and open surgery may have more problems like incisional hernias and bowel obstruction from adhesions [1][4].  

A small number of laparoscopic cases must be converted to open surgery if the inflammation or scar tissue is too extensive, but this does not mean the laparoscopic approach “doesn’t work”—overall outcomes are at least as good as open surgery [2][3][4].

- **Rationale**:  
The studies look at people with sudden hearing loss where no cause is found. They compare different medicine combinations and sometimes add breathing carbogen gas. Outcomes are measured as “effective rate” (how many improve) or change in hearing level (decibels). Overall, medicines often help some people, but not everyone, and extra drugs don’t always add clear benefit. The studies are small and short-term, so results are not definitive.

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicines can improve hearing in many people with sudden hearing loss of unknown cause, but results are mixed and not guaranteed.

Standard drug treatments (steroids, circulation‑improving drugs, vitamins) led to hearing improvement in about half of patients; adding carbogen gas increased the improvement rate to about 77% and shortened recovery from 6–23 days to 3–14 days [1].  

In another trial, a blood‑thinning solution improved average hearing, and adding naftidrofuryl improved it further and also reduced tinnitus, without more side effects [2].  

By contrast, adding prostaglandin E1 to steroid treatment did not clearly improve overall hearing, though it modestly helped high‑frequency hearing in some with severe tinnitus [3].

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks whether medicines for sudden hearing loss with no clear cause (idiopathic sudden sensorineural hearing loss) are ineffective. The studies show that some drug treatments are associated with meaningful hearing improvement, but results are mixed and not all patients benefit. One study using a standard drug regimen alone reported improvement in about half of patients, while adding a special gas (carbogen) increased improvement rates, suggesting treatment can help but is not a guaranteed cure [1]. Another trial showed that one drug (naftidrofuryl) added to a blood-thinning infusion improved hearing more than the infusion alone, again indicating partial benefit [2]. However, a different drug (prostaglandin E1) did not show clear overall benefit when added to steroid treatment, except for some high-frequency gains in certain patients [3].

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicines for sudden hearing loss with no known cause are only partly effective, not useless but not a cure-all.

In one study, a standard drug regimen (steroids, vitamins, circulation drugs) improved hearing in about 50% of patients; adding carbogen gas increased improvement to about 77%, and recovery was faster [1]. This suggests common drug treatments help some, but not all, people.

Another trial found that a circulation drug (naftidrofuryl) added to low-molecular-weight dextran improved average hearing more (about 21 dB improvement vs 13 dB with dextran alone) and reduced tinnitus, indicating additional benefit from medication adjustments [2].

However, a study of prostaglandin E1 added to steroid treatment did not show overall better hearing than steroid plus placebo, except for some improvement at high-pitched sounds in certain patients with severe tinnitus [3].

Overall, medicines can improve hearing for many, but a substantial number still have limited recovery despite treatment [1][2][3].

- **Rationale**:  
The studies look at people with sudden hearing loss where no cause was found. Several medicine combinations were tested. Some showed better hearing recovery than others, but results were not consistent across all drugs. This suggests that medicines can help some people, but benefits vary by treatment type and are not guaranteed.

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicines can help some people with sudden hearing loss of unknown cause, but they do not work for everyone and no single treatment is clearly best.

In one study, a standard drug mix (including steroids, vitamins, and circulation‑improving drugs) improved hearing in about half of patients, and adding carbogen gas increased the success rate to about 77% and shortened recovery time [1].  

Another trial found that adding the drug naftidrofuryl to low‑molecular‑weight dextran led to greater hearing improvement and better relief of tinnitus than dextran alone [2].  

However, a study of prostaglandin E1 added to steroid treatment did not show meaningful overall benefit compared with steroids alone [3].  

Overall, medicines may improve recovery, but results are mixed and not guaranteed.

- **Rationale**:  
Studies of sudden sensorineural hearing loss with no known cause (“idiopathic sudden deafness”) show that some medicines can improve hearing, while others do not clearly add benefit. Drug-only treatment helped about half of patients, and adding certain medicines improved results further. However, one medicine (prostaglandin E1) did not clearly improve overall recovery compared with standard steroid treatment, though it may help at specific high pitches in some people. Overall, medicines are not guaranteed to work, but they are not useless and should be started quickly under specialist care.

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicines *can* work for sudden hearing loss with no known cause, but they do not help everyone and not all drugs are equally useful.

Standard drug treatments (such as steroids, circulation‑improving drugs, and vitamins) led to hearing improvement in about 50% of patients, and combining them with carbogen gas improved the success rate to about 77% [1].  

Adding naftidrofuryl to low‑molecular‑weight dextran led to greater hearing recovery than dextran alone, including better tinnitus relief [2].  

Prostaglandin E1, when added to steroid treatment, did not clearly improve overall hearing, though it helped high‑frequency hearing in some patients [3].  

So medicines are often worth trying and may improve hearing, but results vary and quick specialist treatment is important.

- **Rationale**:  
The articles study older medicines for chronic hepatitis C (“long‑lasting liver infection”) in people who also have HIV. These use interferon (standard or pegylated) plus ribavirin. Effectiveness is measured by “sustained virologic response” (SVR), meaning the virus stays undetectable months after treatment stops, which is generally considered a cure.

- **Full Answer**:  
In people with HIV and long‑lasting hepatitis C, standard interferon plus ribavirin cured only about 12–22% of patients overall [3][8][9][10][14]. Newer “pegylated” interferon combined with ribavirin worked better, curing about 27–55% depending on the study and patient mix [1][4][5][6][8][11][13][14].  

A large trial found pegylated interferon plus ribavirin cured 40% overall: about 29% of those with the harder‑to‑treat genotype 1 and 62% with genotypes 2 or 3 [13]. Another study reported cure rates up to 55% with pegylated interferon plus ribavirin [5].  

Side effects were very common and led 10–30% of patients to stop treatment early [1][3][4][5][6][8][11][13][14]. If the virus had not fallen clearly by 12 weeks, the chance of later cure was almost zero, so stopping at that point was recommended [1][3][8][10][11][13].

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks how ineffective medicines are for long-lasting liver infection. The articles describe older treatments for chronic hepatitis C (a long-term liver infection), mostly in people who also have HIV. The key measure is “sustained virologic response” (SVR), meaning the virus stays undetectable after treatment ends—essentially a cure. Across many randomized trials, standard interferon plus ribavirin cured only a small minority of patients. Pegylated interferon improved results but still left many uncured, especially those with harder-to-treat virus types. Side effects were very frequent and often led people to stop treatment early, further reducing effectiveness. Attempts to intensify treatment, extend duration, or add extra drugs generally did not meaningfully improve cure rates. Thus, these older medicines were only modestly effective and quite hard to tolerate for chronic hepatitis C.

- **Full Answer**:  
Doctors used to treat long‑lasting hepatitis C (a chronic liver infection) in people with HIV mainly with interferon and ribavirin. These medicines cleared the virus in only about 12–22 out of 100 patients overall. [9][10][14] Even with the improved “pegylated” interferon, cure rates were only about 27–44 out of 100. [1][5][6][8][13][14] Hard‑to‑treat virus types (genotypes 1 and 4) did especially poorly, with cure in only about 14–30 of 100 patients, while easier types (2 and 3) reached about 50–70 of 100. [1][4][5][6][8][13][14] Side effects were very common (around 90% of patients had some), and 10–30 out of 100 had to stop treatment early because they could not tolerate it. [1][3][4][5][6][7][8][9][11][13][14] Longer or more intensive regimens, or adding a third drug like amantadine, did not meaningfully improve success. [7][10][11] Overall, these older medicines were only modestly effective and often difficult to stay on for chronic liver infection for many patients. [9][13][14]

- **Rationale**:  
The articles describe long‑lasting liver infection caused by chronic hepatitis C, especially in people who also have HIV. They evaluate different antiviral medicines (interferon, pegylated interferon, ribavirin, and sometimes amantadine). Across many trials, standard interferon plus ribavirin clears the virus in a minority of patients, while pegylated interferon plus ribavirin works better, particularly for certain virus types. However, response rates are still limited and side effects are frequent. Studies also show that if the virus has not fallen enough early in treatment, continuing for longer offers almost no chance of cure.

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicines can help many people with long‑lasting liver infection from hepatitis C, but they do not work for everyone and often cause side effects.

In people who also have HIV, older standard treatments using interferon and ribavirin cleared the virus in about 12–27% of patients, depending on the regimen [8],[9],[14]. Newer “pegylated” interferon plus ribavirin worked better, curing about 27–55% overall and up to 60–70% in easier‑to‑treat virus types (genotypes 2 or 3) [1],[4],[5],[6],[8],[13],[14]. Harder‑to‑treat types (genotypes 1 or 4) had lower cure rates, often below 40% [1],[4],[5],[6],[8],[13],[14].

Doctors check early viral response after 8–12 weeks; if the virus has not dropped by at least 100‑fold, the chance of later cure is almost zero, so stopping treatment is usually advised [3],[4],[5],[8],[10],[11],[13],[14]. Side effects (flu‑like symptoms, anemia, low white cells) are very common and lead some patients to stop treatment early [1],[3],[4],[5],[7],[8],[11],[13]. Overall, medicine can work well for some, but results vary.

- **Rationale**:  
I interpreted “liver infection that lasts for a long time” as chronic hepatitis C. The articles summarize trials in people who have both HIV and long‑lasting hepatitis C. They compare older medicines (interferon plus ribavirin) with a stronger form called pegylated interferon plus ribavirin. I focused on how often these treatments clear the virus, how side effects limit treatment, and how early blood tests predict who will benefit.

- **Full Answer**:  
Medicines can work for long‑lasting liver infections such as chronic hepatitis C, but they do not help everyone [3][8][13][14].  

In studies of people with both HIV and chronic hepatitis C, older treatments using interferon plus ribavirin cleared the hepatitis C virus in about 12–27% of patients [3][8][9][14]. When a stronger “pegylated” interferon was combined with ribavirin, cure rates improved to about 27–55%, and were highest (around 60–70%) in some virus types (genotypes 2 or 3) [1][4][5][6][8][13][14]. However, many people had side effects and had to stop treatment early [1][3][4][5][6][8][11][13].  

Doctors can tell by 12 weeks whether treatment is working: if the virus level has not dropped by at least 100‑fold, the chance of later cure is almost zero, so therapy is usually stopped [3][8][10][11].  

Even when the virus is not completely cleared, these medicines can reduce liver damage in some patients [8][14]. Your liver specialist can explain your personal chances.

- **Rationale**:  
The articles study silver *dressings* (foams and alginates), not “silver cream,” but they show how silver works on dirty or locally infected chronic sores. Overall, silver dressings modestly speed healing and improve signs linked to bacterial burden (odor, discharge, maceration), with similar side effects to standard dressings. Evidence is best for chronic leg and pressure ulcers, not for minor everyday cuts. Silver products do not replace proper cleaning, medical assessment, or antibiotics when there is a true infection (e.g., spreading redness, fever).

- **Full Answer**:  
In chronic leg and pressure ulcers that are heavily contaminated but not severely infected, silver-releasing dressings help sores heal faster than standard moist dressings or “local best practice.” Ulcer size fell by about 45–50% with silver foam versus 25–34% with non-silver care over four weeks [1][2]. Silver dressings also reduced wound odor, leakage, maceration (skin softening), slough, and exudate, and improved comfort and ease of care [1][2].  

In locally infected chronic wounds, silver hydroalginate dressings led to faster wound closure and greater improvement in wound severity than standard alginate, with similar tolerance and slightly fewer patients needing antibiotics [3].  

These results apply to specialized silver *dressings* for chronic ulcers, not routine “silver cream” for small cuts. For clearly infected sores (spreading redness, pus, fever), medical evaluation and possible antibiotics are still needed.

- **Rationale**:  
The question asks about “silver cream,” but the studies provided evaluated silver-releasing dressings (foams and hydroalginate), which are closely related ways of applying silver to wounds. Across three trials, silver dressings were consistently *more* effective than similar non-silver dressings for slow-healing, colonised, or locally infected wounds, improving healing rates, odour, leakage, and exudate [1–3]. However, these studies mostly involved chronic ulcers, not grossly dirty wounds or patients with whole-body (systemic) infection, and one trial specifically excluded wounds needing systemic antibiotics [3]. So, the evidence does not support the idea that topical silver is “ineffective” for contaminated or locally infected sores—rather, it has measurable but limited benefits and must be part of broader care (thorough cleaning, possible antibiotics, pressure relief, etc.).

- **Full Answer**:  
Research doesn’t show that silver products are useless for dirty or infected sores; in fact, dressings that slowly release silver can help when wounds are heavily colonised with bacteria.

In people with hard‑to‑heal venous leg ulcers, a silver foam dressing led to about 45% reduction in wound size in four weeks, compared with 25% using a similar dressing without silver, and also reduced smell, leakage, and skin damage around the wound [1]. In a larger “real‑life” study of slow‑healing ulcers, silver foam reduced wound area by 50% versus 34% with usual dressings and improved exudate, odour, and pain [2]. In colonised pressure and venous ulcers with signs of local infection, a silver‑releasing dressing doubled the healing rate and tended to reduce the need for antibiotics [3].

So, silver itself is not ineffective, but it must be combined with good cleaning and proper medical care for seriously infected or dirty wounds.

- **Rationale**:  
The question is about whether “silver cream” works for dirty or infected sores. The articles study silver-containing *dressings* (not basic cream) on chronic wounds that are heavily colonized with germs or at high risk of infection. Across multiple trials, silver dressings improved healing speed and reduced signs related to local infection (odor, discharge, slough), and sometimes reduced antibiotic use. However, they were used under medical supervision and not as the only treatment for serious infection.

- **Full Answer**:  
Research has looked at silver‐releasing dressings, not ordinary creams, on sores that are dirty or starting to get infected.  

In people with venous leg ulcers that were “critically colonised” (heavily contaminated with germs), a silver foam dressing made the ulcers shrink faster and reduced smell, leakage and skin softening compared with a similar foam without silver [1]. In a large “real‑life” study of different slow‑healing wounds, the same silver foam led to a bigger reduction in wound size, less slough (dead tissue), less ooze and odour, and better pain relief than best‑practice dressings, including some other silver products [2]. In chronic wounds at high risk of infection, a silver hydroalginate dressing improved healing rate and may have lowered the need for antibiotics [3].  

So, silver‑containing dressings can help dirty or mildly infected sores, but should be used as part of medical wound care, not to replace treatment for serious infection.

- **Rationale**:  
Studies looked at silver-containing dressings (not creams) used on chronic ulcers that were heavily colonised with bacteria or at high risk of local infection. Compared with similar dressings without silver or with usual care, the silver dressings led to faster healing and better control of fluid, odour, and tissue damage, and fewer wounds progressed to needing antibiotics [1–3].

- **Full Answer**:  
Silver products are used specifically because they kill bacteria in wounds. In studies of chronic leg and pressure ulcers with heavy bacterial colonisation or early local infection, silver dressings helped ulcers shrink faster and reduced odour, leakage, slough, and maceration compared with similar non‑silver dressings or usual care [1,2]. They also reduced how many wounds later needed antibiotics and improved overall wound severity scores [3]. However, they were always used on wounds that were properly cleaned and assessed. Silver will not “fix” a very dirty or severely infected sore without cleaning and medical care [3].

- **Rationale**:  
The question is about medicines that *prevent* migraine attacks in people who get headaches that come and go (episodic migraines). The articles mainly study valproate/divalproex and topiramate. They report how often attacks occur before and after starting medicine, how many people get at least a 50% cut in attacks (“responders”), and how the drugs compare with placebo and other preventive drugs. I focus on typical benefits (how many people improve and by how much) and safety, using numbers averaged across the studies, and explain them in everyday language.

- **Full Answer**:  
Preventive migraine medicines do not cure migraine, but they can clearly reduce how often attacks occur for many people whose headaches come and go.

With valproate/divalproex, about 44–50% of patients cut their migraine frequency by at least half, compared with about 14–21% on placebo [4][7][8]. On average, people went from about 4–6 migraines every 4 weeks to roughly 2–4, whereas placebo groups improved only slightly [1][4][7][8]. Similar responder rates (about two‑thirds) were seen when divalproex was compared with propranolol and flunarizine, suggesting effectiveness comparable to standard preventives [2][9].  

Topiramate works about as well as valproate: both significantly reduced monthly migraine frequency, intensity, and duration, with responder rates around 64–72% in small trials [3][6].  

Most side effects were mild (such as stomach upset, sleepiness, or dizziness), and serious problems were uncommon in these short‑term studies [1][2][4][7][8].

- **Rationale**:  
Research on long‑term (preventive) migraine medicines like valproate, divalproex, and topiramate shows they usually **reduce** how often migraines occur, but rarely make them disappear completely. Across several trials, these drugs cut monthly migraine attacks by about 1–3 episodes compared with starting levels and work clearly better than placebo, but many people still have “come and go” headaches. About half to three‑quarters of patients get at least a 50% drop in migraine frequency, meaning one‑quarter to one‑half do **not** improve that much. Side effects are usually mild to moderate, but can limit use for some.

- **Full Answer**:  
Preventive migraine medicines are **partly effective, not useless, but not a cure**.  
Divalproex and valproate typically reduce migraine attacks by about 1–2 episodes every 4 weeks compared with placebo [1,4,7,8]. About 44–50% of patients on divalproex achieve at least a 50% drop in attack frequency, versus 14–21% on placebo [4,7,8]. Other studies show 63–78% of patients improve on divalproex or propranolol [9] and about 65–72% respond to valproate or flunarizine [2]. Topiramate and valproate both significantly cut how often, how long, and how severe migraines are [3,6].  
This means **many people still have migraines that come and go**, and about one‑quarter to one‑half get limited benefit, but for the rest, attacks become noticeably less frequent and disabling [1–10].

- **Rationale**:  
The studies look at people with repeating (“come and go”) migraine attacks several times per month, not constant daily pain. Several daily medicines, especially valproate-type drugs (sodium valproate/divalproex/valproic acid) and topiramate, were tested against placebo. These drugs didn’t cure migraine, but they clearly reduced how often attacks occurred, and sometimes how long or how intense they were. Many patients cut their attack frequency by about half, and side effects were usually mild. This shows that preventive medicines can work well for recurring migraine, though they must be taken every day and monitored by a doctor.

- **Full Answer**:  
Yes. Daily “preventive” medicines can help people whose migraine attacks come and go.  

Drugs such as sodium valproate, divalproex, or valproic acid reduced monthly migraine attacks and days compared with placebo in several trials [1][4][5][7][8][10]. Around 44–75% of patients on these medicines cut their migraine frequency by at least half [4][7][8][9].  

Topiramate showed similar benefits, lowering monthly frequency, intensity, and duration of headaches [3][6].  

Side effects (like stomach upset, sleepiness, tremor) were generally mild and comparable to placebo at usual doses [1][2][4][7][8][10].  

These medicines don’t stop an individual attack immediately, but they can meaningfully reduce how often and how bad migraines are over time.

- **Rationale**:  
The studies all looked at people whose migraines “come and go” several times per month. These people took preventive medicines every day, and researchers counted how often attacks occurred over weeks. Compared with placebo, valproate/divalproex and topiramate clearly reduced how many migraine attacks people had, even though the headaches were intermittent. Typically, about half to two‑thirds of patients had their number of attacks cut by at least half. The medicines did not usually stop migraines completely, and they did not work for everyone, but they made attacks less frequent and sometimes shorter or less intense. Side effects were generally mild to moderate. This shows that preventive medicine can work for recurring, on‑and‑off migraines, but it is usually reserved for people who have multiple attacks each month, not for very rare headaches.

- **Full Answer**:  
Preventive migraine medicines do work for headaches that “come and go” repeatedly.

In multiple trials of people with several migraine attacks per month, daily valproate or divalproex cut attack frequency by about 1–2 attacks every 4 weeks compared with placebo, and about 44–75% of patients had their migraines reduced by at least half [1][4][7][8][9]. Topiramate showed similar benefits, reducing how often, how long, and how intense attacks were [3][6]. Lower doses of valproate (about 500–600 mg/day) were effective with fewer side effects [7][10].

These medicines do not cure migraine and may not help everyone, but they clearly reduce how often episodic (on‑and‑off) migraines occur in many patients. They are usually considered when migraines happen several times a month or significantly affect daily life.

- **Rationale**:  
The articles all look at radioactive iodine treatment given after thyroid cancer surgery. They measure how often this treatment completely destroys remaining thyroid tissue (and sometimes small areas of spread), compare low vs. standard doses, and compare two ways of preparing the body (stopping thyroid hormone vs. using rhTSH injections). I used their reported ablation (clear‑scan) rates and side‑effect data to explain, in everyday language, how effective and tolerable this treatment is when cancer or thyroid tissue remains after surgery, noting that most patients studied were low risk.

- **Full Answer**:  
After surgery for differentiated thyroid cancer, doctors often use radioactive iodine (“radioiodine”) to destroy any remaining thyroid tissue and some small areas of spread. In several studies, this treatment cleared visible thyroid remnants on scans in about 85–100% of patients, depending on the dose used and how stimulation was done [1–3]. Lower doses (about half) of radioiodine were about as successful as higher doses when special thyroid‑stimulating injections (rhTSH) were used [2–3]. In one study, success rates were similarly high (about 89%) even in patients who already had spread to neck lymph nodes [3]. Using rhTSH to prepare for treatment, instead of stopping thyroid hormone, kept people feeling well and reduced radiation to the blood and bone marrow without lowering ablation success [1,4]. These studies mostly involved low‑risk patients, so results may not fully predict outcomes for people with more extensive or distant spread [1–3]. Your doctor can explain more.

- **Rationale**:  
The articles describe patients with differentiated thyroid cancer who had surgery and then received radioactive iodine to destroy any leftover thyroid tissue or small cancer deposits. Success was mainly judged by follow‑up scans showing no iodine uptake in the neck and by very low blood levels of thyroglobulin (a tumor marker). Across studies, most patients met these success criteria, including some with spread to neck lymph nodes, so only a minority had incomplete ablation.

- **Full Answer**:  
In these studies, radioactive iodine after surgery was effective for most people with remaining thyroid tissue or small cancer deposits.

Using scan results, 89–100% of patients had successful destruction of remnant tissue, meaning treatment was ineffective in about 0–11% of cases [1][2][3].  

Using blood tumor‑marker tests (thyroglobulin), success ranged from about 66–96%, meaning 4–34% still had evidence of remaining tissue or cancer [1][2][3].  

Importantly, similar success (about 89% by scan) was seen even in patients with spread to neck lymph nodes [3].

- **Rationale**:  
These studies involve people with differentiated thyroid cancer who had surgery but still had small pieces of thyroid tissue left. They all test radioactive iodine as a treatment to destroy this remaining tissue. Across studies, most patients had “successful ablation,” meaning scans showed no thyroid tissue and cancer markers were very low months later.

- **Full Answer**:  
Yes—for differentiated thyroid cancer, radioactive iodine after surgery is effective at treating cancer that remains or has spread.  

When patients received radioactive iodine after thyroid removal, 88–100% had successful elimination of remaining thyroid tissue on follow‑up scans [1–3]. Blood levels of thyroglobulin, a marker of thyroid cancer, were low in most patients [1–3]. This benefit was seen even in some patients with lymph‑node metastases [3].  

Different preparation methods (stopping thyroid hormone vs using recombinant TSH) and different iodine doses had similar success, while recombinant TSH caused fewer hypothyroid symptoms and less radiation to the blood [1,2,3,4].

- **Rationale**:  
The studies describe people with differentiated thyroid cancer who had surgery but still had remaining thyroid tissue or cancer, sometimes in nearby lymph nodes. They were given radioactive iodine (“radioiodine”) as medicine after surgery. Nearly all patients had successful “ablation,” meaning no or minimal remaining thyroid tissue or cancer was seen on follow‑up scans and blood tests [1–3]. This success was similar whether patients were prepared with thyroid hormone withdrawal or with recombinant TSH (rhTSH), and it remained high even when lymph node spread was present [3]. These results show that, at least for this type of thyroid cancer, medicine given after surgery often does work very well, though no treatment is guaranteed to cure every patient.

- **Full Answer**:  
For differentiated thyroid cancer, medicine given after surgery often does work for cancer that stayed or spread locally.  

After surgery, many patients receive radioactive iodine to destroy remaining thyroid tissue or small cancer deposits [1]. In large studies, essentially all patients had successful ablation of remnant tissue on follow‑up scans and blood tests [1], and high success rates (about 85–95%) were seen even when lower radioiodine doses were used [2]. Using either standard doses or half‑doses of radioiodine after stimulation with recombinant TSH led to similar ablation success (about 89%), including in patients with spread to lymph nodes [3].
